And a lot of it is, you know, in smartphones we stack up a lot of chipsets into a very small form factor. In the car it's similar, we have to stack up a lot of components into a form factor. But increasingly more important, especially in the EV world, the software becomes very important—how do you manage battery power, how do you manage the software to drive the car in a safe manner? I think those are very important similarities with the smartphone business. Obviously managing the supply chain is also very important. We have a lot of suppliers that are the same between the smartphone business as well as the EV business and that's why there's a lot of cross-collaboration. I think people trust the engineers of Xiaomi that we can build something that is world-class and that is very popular with the users. How far behind is Europe when it comes to EVs now? Look, we still look up to a lot of the OEMs, the traditional OEMs. We still think that there are a lot of things to learn from them—the way that they build the brand, the way that they build the drivetrain, the way that they give people a real good driving experience. Those are the stuff that we still look up to a lot of the European OEMs. What we think that they are lacking is, number one, the smartness of the car. I think they are trying to implement a lot of the smart features into the car right now, but I think in that sense they are probably behind what we are seeing in China right now. And then the second is kind of the whole NEV, the new energy vehicle development, right? I think that a lot of the focus was on electric at that time, but really I think when we look at our EV we call it the smart electric vehicle, right? And I think that some OEMs may look at electric too focused, but less focus on smart, right? I think that that's one thing that the users right now really want. Especially given the Xiaomi advantage, it's how do I link it with all of our ecosystem products? How does it work better with my smartphone? How does it work better with my IoT devices? You know, things like that, so that I can create not just an EV but also a smart life ecosystem beyond the EV. And how long do you think it will take the Western brands to catch up on that particular element? I think a lot of people are learning, right? I think a lot of people are studying how the Chinese EV development has become so advanced. We have obviously received quite a lot of European OEMs who've come to visit us, who want to have experience sharing with us. Not just we sharing with them but they also share with us, you know? It depends on how serious they are, and I think you already see a couple of the European collaborations with the Chinese players as well, right? So I think that that's going to be helpful in driving the industry forward. Now I think it was the CEO of Ford who used your SU7 for a little while, what did he say about it? He said he couldn't get rid of it after six months of driving it. Because he loved it too much? Apparently. I haven't spoken to him in person, but apparently based on what he's been saying in public, he loves our car. How is the EV market going to develop going forward? Look, I think the EV penetration will continue to go higher. In China, new car sales are already 50, 60 percent new energy vehicle, right? So that's very high already. But if you look at a lot of the global market, the new EV market is still quite low in terms of penetration. So I think that as the infrastructure continues to develop, as people appreciate the smartness and the drivability of these new electric vehicles, the penetration will go up. So when you see European and American producers actually pulling back their EV efforts, what do you think about that? Look, I think obviously there is a lot of investment that need to go in, especially for many of the legacy brands because they need to retool the whole systems to get NEV done. So, you know, a lot of them are probably not pushing that agenda as forward. But honestly speaking, we don't think that they... we think that the NEV is going to continue to penetrate. Whether the Chinese players are coming in or whether the European and American players are developing them themselves, or also the Japanese and the Koreans, so I actually think that the trend will be there longer term, right? There are obviously maybe hiccups along the way, but the whole industry will move forward in this direction. Sometimes there's a major breakthrough in terms of smartness of the vehicles and also the infrastructure. I think there are a lot of places whereby the infrastructure is probably not as well developed in other markets for the EV market to take off. Let's move on to robotics and humanoids, what are you doing there? I've just seen your latest models and it's pretty mind-boggling. Yeah, look, we've been doing robotics for five or six years now. That's an investment that we started making back in 2019, 2020. But then I think we are beginning to see the inflection point in this market. A combination of the hardware getting better as well as the software getting better, i.e. AI, you know that is driving the brain of the robot. The way that Xiaomi internally is thinking about it is we are making these humanoid robots to enhance our own manufacturing capability and efficiency. We haven't launched any 2C robot. So all the robots that we are developing or the humanoid robots we are developing right now are used in our own manufacturing scenario. I think the video already showed that there were two robots that are working consecutively for three hours with a very low margin of error. But still I think that's a long way to go because as you know, I'm sure that we'll move to AI at some point in this conversation, right? The AI right now is mostly focusing on learning from text data and from voice data that we have accumulated for a long, long time. But then the physical data are still quite a way behind in terms of the collection and the interpretation. So I do think that when you develop the large language model for physical AI, right now what we are still lacking is a lot of data that don't exist, right? And so you have to simulate it, you have to find out the use cases and find the corner cases etc., but the text and the voice are quite well developed at this point. Can you tell us about the improvement in the grip functionality in your latest model? Yeah, look, I think if you look at the video that shows... the hand is much smaller than the other versions. The hand is very similar to a human hand size now, right? And also the degree of freedom that it offered. I think one of the biggest challenges with the hand is how many degrees of freedom do you have in the hand. And then the second problem that we are trying to solve is the cooling, right? It's like a smartphone, it's like a car. When you use the machines for too long, they get heated, right? And once it gets heated, the consumer electronics rule would say that the performance will degrade, right? So what we're trying to do also is introduce a cooling system so that you can do some heat management of the hand as well so that it doesn't get overheated while you are doing the work repetitively. When do you think you'll have this for consumers? That's not the focus of Xiaomi right now to be honest, because we actually think that there is a big space for us. Ultimately we do hardware, right? We do a lot of hardware and there's a lot of manufacturing involved, right? So we want these humanoid robots to be able to improve our manufacturing efficiency first. Maybe to recreate our entire manufacturing processes, it could be, but that's already a huge market. So right now we're not thinking about doing any 2C robot at this point in time. Who do you think will win this race? It's hard to say, but suffice to say that if you look at this market right now in terms of innovation, in terms of entrepreneurship, in terms of technology leadership, there are two big superpowers in the world, right? The US and China. So the winner will come from one of these two places, but I don't know where yet. What's next? Well, I think we talk a lot about what we've been doing. I think that the next thing for us is to continue to develop our AI capabilities because I think that AI is going to be very important for the next wave of innovations and it'll impact all of our businesses. How do you use it today in the company? Well I mean, I think the simple things are coding, right? So a lot of our software engineers now use bit-coding. Second one is, can we improve our sales efficiency? Can we predict sales better, right? i.e. do I even need to hire an experienced sales store manager or I can use AI to predict what kind of sales should each store generate every day? Marketing materials, right? We can use AI to generate the first few drafts. And very importantly, even in the manufacturing process we've been using AI a lot, right? When we talk about our car, we have a big gigapress, right, that does the rear floor for the car. In terms of the material we use for that rear floor, we simulated over 100-plus formulas. We used AI to predict the performance of each of these formulas and ultimately we picked two to be the material for that rear floor that we do for the gigapress, right? In terms of each piece that we manufactured, they went through an X-ray room where they take a lot of pictures and they use AI to look into that to see whether there are defects in the manufacturing process, right? So you don't use humans to do it anymore, you just use AI to take pictures very quickly. They can diagnose whether this piece is good or not, and then you can use AI in terms of material generations and a lot of those things. So we are using AI in a lot of different areas, but there is more to do going forward. You've also developed your own language model. Yes, we just launched it two weeks ago. So we launched our own large language model two weeks ago and it has performed pretty well. I mean if you look at public scoring systems like openrouter.com, you can see that we've been ranked very highly on that table. So we are very glad that we have a large language model that people like and people use. And at much lower costs than the likes of Claude? Well I mean, these costs are never publicly disclosed information, so I don't quite know the cost of it, but our price that we offered is lower than Claude but much higher than the other models from China that are being offered right now. What are the cost advantages you have when it comes to, you know, the utilization of these models, giving lower energy costs, simpler model structures and so on? We did try to innovate a bit on the infrastructure of the model. So obviously we have the benefit of seeing how other people do it before and then we try to optimize our path. So in terms of infrastructure we have done some innovations, although we didn't talk too much publicly about that. Number two is if you think about the model development right now I think there's still a lot of way to go. The good thing for Xiaomi in a way is I already have a big ecosystem that we can use to test, we can collect data from those user behaviors, and we can refine our model. Last thing is obviously as you know that we put it as an open source model, so we also are trying to invite the best minds globally to help improve that model as a result, right? Because ultimately I think if you look at whether it's our car or our smartphone, we want to invite other people to join us together so that we can help improve the ecosystem together as well. So let's say 5, 10 years from now when you have integrated all your products with the latest AI models and so on, what's the vision of the world? What is it going to look like? As we think about how AI is going to change the world and how we fit into our own ecosystem, we think about it more in the physical AI world, right? How do we get AI into our phone, how do we get AI into our smart appliances, how do we get AI into our car, and then how to interconnect these things to make users' lives more efficient, much better? I think that's how we see this, right? Obviously other people are using AI to serve the coder community, and other people are trying to use AI to make very useful chatbots for the users so that they can manage their lives better. But for us, for Xiaomi, because we have over a billion connected devices globally and we have over 800 million smartphone users globally, how do we interconnect these devices so that we'll enhance people's productivity in the physical AI world? I think that's something that we are looking at. I have to ask you a question about the founder, Lei Jun, who's kind of considered a mix of Bill Gates, Steve Jobs, and Elon Musk. How is it to work with him? Well, Mr. Lei himself is an entrepreneur, a serial entrepreneur, but he's also a product person, right? I think if you look at when we got into the car industry, we decided that he should be the CEO of the car business in order to take it forward. And he will... I think you've seen other interviews of him where he personally has driven 150 different models of cars himself, and taken detailed notes so that he understands what's on offer in the market and how can he improve on it. I can assure you that you can find very few CEOs that have this real dedication to start off with this car business, right? He makes us, like all the management team... I personally driven 3,000 kilometers before the first car was launched in our own car just to, again, follow his philosophy that you have to understand the product. You have to love your product before you are in this business, right? He made us all take professional racing car licenses in order to know how to drive properly. So he's obviously a visionary, he defined the company's culture, but he also tried to help us enhance our knowledge not just on the business but also on the products that we offer. What are the other ways he influences the corporate culture of the company? Well I think look, he's obviously a very hard-working guy, right? People know in China that he works very, very hard and that obviously inspired all of us to follow suit, right? He's a visionary, I mean I think he's considered a visionary, he's also a very good investor in China. So I think those are the stuff that he defined the corporate culture. What does it mean to work hard in your book? Work hard and work smart, right? I mean obviously we have a very complicated business. We are in smartphones, we are in IoTs, we are in cars, we have AI, we have operating systems, and we are running our business in over 100-plus countries. We're also in the manufacturing industry, etc., so there's a lot of things to manage on a day-to-day basis. By that already you can spend a lot of time doing daily jobs. But at the same time I think what we need to do is work smart, i.e. what are the priorities? What are the three important things we want to achieve this quarter, this month as well as this year, right? And we focus on those three things that matter, right? As opposed to getting immersed into a lot of small tasks that may not have much of an impact. So for the management suite we are more focusing on what's the most important things and how do we tackle that on a quarterly basis, on a yearly basis, on a monthly basis, and on a daily basis. You mentioned Chinese speed in the beginning, what's the key to gaining speed? Well look, I think number one is having the right infrastructure, right? If you look at the manufacturing supply chain in smartphones, in appliances, and also in the EV industry, it's very well developed in China already. That helps any business kickstart very, very quickly. Number two is the amount of innovation and entrepreneurship that is happening here, right? People are willing to spend the commitment on innovating stuff as opposed to just following stuff. And I think the third is really that people in China do spend a lot of time working. And they tend to spend more hours, right? I mean if you spend more hours, I think the investment will pay off at some point. What are your plans for Europe? Look, we are already quite strong in Europe in terms of consumer electronics. Last year we ranked number three in Europe in terms of smartphone sales, so we're up there with Apple and Samsung. Next year, in the second half of next year, we're planning to export our cars into Europe. So that's going to be a new and big venture for us, and we are already investing a lot in Europe right now. As you can see, we have the car racing on Nürburgring, we also set up an EV research center in Munich this year with 75 to 100 engineers over there already developing some of the coolest products. I think we've showcased in Barcelona this March a Vision Gran Turismo product, which is a concept car that we have, and that's developed by our European team. Great fun. How do you personally relax? Well look, I did a lot of... many people say working is relaxing, so I do work quite a lot, but at the same time you have to find the right time. I spend a lot of time reading as well, I spend a lot of time doing exercise. What do you read? All kinds of books. Research reports—I do like reading research reports on other companies. I do like reading business books; there are obviously a lot of successful examples in many of the industries that we're in, whether it is Apple, whether it is Tesla. I read a lot of those books. But at the same time I try to read a lot of books that talk about business failures, right? Why do some companies fail? It's something that you always can learn, right? I think there's one phrase that I really like: history always repeats itself. And so as you read more about these business stories, you know how people can be successful and you need to find your own path to success, but at the same time you want to avoid the pitfalls that many companies have faced in the past. Working is relaxing, what does that mean? I mean you do need to enjoy your work, I think. It's very important that you need to enjoy what you are doing. You need to enjoy working with your colleagues, working with other partners, and you need to enjoy understanding what your users need. So those are in itself quite an enjoyable experience as well as relaxing experience. But at the same time I do spend quite a bit of time running outside, right? I'm going to do a half marathon in Beijing this weekend, so that's something that I will do. And I think that those are the times that you can think about what happens next, some of the long-term plans. But I do also enjoy meeting up with partners, customers, and friends over a nice meal, having a nice glass of wine, and that's something that you can learn from them as well. I mean I do enjoy talking to a lot of industry people, people from other industries as well, and in every conversation you try to get a different perspective of what's important to them and you can learn from them about their experience also. What is your advice to young people? Look, I think the world is evolving very, very fast, right? I do think that having the interest to learn about the latest developments is very important. We all talk about AI, right? How is AI going to change the job market in the future, how is AI going to change how people are doing things in the future? I think the young people do need to catch up on the latest trends because if they don't catch up, then they may face job security issues in the future. They may not be able to understand what the next stage of development is going to be. So I do think that young people do need to spend time understanding the latest trends. But at the same time, as I mentioned previously, I do like to read about history, I do like to read about other businesses' success or failure, because you can always learn something from history. There's going to be... as I mentioned before, history always repeats itself, so there's something that you can learn. One of our chairmen said many years ago—which resonates a lot with me—that 99% of the problems already have a solution out there, you just need to find it. And maybe with AI you can find it faster now. Well, you for sure have found the solution to very many problems and big thanks for all your hard work. We are so happy to be a shareholder in your group and good luck with everything going forward. Thank you Nicolai, thanks for having me. Thank you.

Tesla Q1 2026 Financial Results and Q&A Webcast

Tesla(2.9M) ·65:20 ·176.5K views · 2026-04-22
Tesla is pivoting into a massive $25B+ capital investment phase for 2026, prioritizing the "revolutionary" scale-up of Optimus, AI-5 chip architecture, and unsupervised Full Self-Driving (FSD) over short-term free cash flow.
TL;DR Tesla is pivoting into a massive $25B+ capital investment phase for 2026, prioritizing the "revolutionary" scale-up of Optimus, AI-5 chip architecture, and unsupervised Full Self-Driving (FSD) over short-term free cash flow. FSD Software Roadmap: Version 14.3 was a...

TL;DR

Tesla is pivoting into a massive $25B+ capital investment phase for 2026, prioritizing the "revolutionary" scale-up of Optimus, AI-5 chip architecture, and unsupervised Full Self-Driving (FSD) over short-term free cash flow.

AI, Autonomy, and Chip Infrastructure

  • FSD Software Roadmap: Version 14.3 was a major architectural update, while the upcoming Version 15 (targeted for late 2026/early 2027) will be a complete software overhaul running on AI 4 hardware.
      • The goal for V15 is to move beyond human-level safety to "unsupervised" autonomy globally where legal.
  • Robotaxi Expansion: Operations have expanded to Dallas and Houston using the same software source as the Bay Area. Management emphasized a shift in strategy where the vehicle is viewed primarily as the "delivery mechanism" for FSD.
  • Vertical Integration (Terrafab): Tesla is constructing a "Research Chip Fab" on the Giga Texas campus.
      • Strategic Rationale: Elon Musk stated the fab is not for leverage over suppliers but because the industry cannot grow logic and memory fast enough to meet Tesla’s future AI demands.
  • Custom Silicon: The AI 5 chip has "taped out" and is expected to be the best edge inference chip for compute; development has already begun on AI 6 and Dojo 3.

The Evolution of Optimus

  • Production Timeline: Initial production for Optimus is slated to begin in Fremont around late July or August 2026. A second Optimus factory is under construction at Giga Texas, with production starting in Summer 2027.
  • V3 Design Reveal: Expected by mid-2026. Musk noted a hesitation to reveal the "polished" V3 design early because competitors perform "frame-by-frame analysis" to copy Tesla’s technology.
  • Technical Intelligence: Optimus will use xAI’s Grok for "orchestration" (high-level management) and voice interaction.
      • The robot is designed with enough local intelligence to operate for "several hours" without a cellular or Wi-Fi connection, similar to how Tesla vehicles drive safely offline.

2026 Financial Outlook and Capital Expenditures

  • CapEx Surge: CFO Vibhav Taneja projected 2026 CapEx to exceed $25 billion. This supports the ramp-up of six factories, AI infrastructure, and the Texas chip fab.
  • Free Cash Flow: Management expects negative free cash flow for the remainder of 2026 due to this heavy investment cycle.
  • Margins and Revenue:
      • Auto Margins: Improved sequentially to 19.2% (excluding credits), aided by one-time warranty true-downs of $230M.
      • Order Backlog: Q1 ended with the highest order backlog in over two years, with significant demand growth in France and Germany (150%+ QoQ).
      • Energy Margins: Reached a record 39.5% gross margin, though this was boosted by $250M in one-time tariff recognitions.

Manufacturing and Energy Storage

  • Production Constraints: The primary limiter for vehicle growth is currently "battery pack capacity" rather than cell supply. Tesla is retooling its Reno facility and ramping 4680 cell production in Berlin to address this.
  • New Product Ramps: Cybercab and Semi-truck are in the "slow S-curve" of initial production. Musk expects "exponential" growth for these lines toward the end of 2026.
  • Megapack 3: Production is scheduled to begin later this year at a new factory outside Houston to meet "very strong" global demand for energy storage.
  • Solar Strategy: To combat a stall in residential solar, Tesla introduced a new lease product to capture tax credits and launched a "best-in-class" mounting system and proprietary panel.
"I think Optimus will be our biggest product, not just Tesla's biggest product ever, but probably the biggest product ever." — Elon Musk, CEO
"We have evolved our vehicle sales strategy where we now emphasize FSD as a product and vehicle as only the delivery mechanism." — Vibhav Taneja, CFO
"We just anticipate hitting the wall if we don't make chips ourselves... the rate at which the industry is growing in logic but even more so in memory is just [insufficient]." — Elon Musk, CEO
"While this may seem a lot [referring to $25B CapEx] and we will have the impact of negative free cash flow for the rest of the year, we believe this is the right strategy to position the company for the next era." — Vibhav Taneja, CFO
Good afternoon, everyone, and welcome to Tesla's first quarter 2026 Q&A webcast. My name is Travis Axerod, head of Investor Relations, and I am joined today by Elon Musk, Vibhav Taneja, and a number of other executives. Our Q1 results were announced at about PM Central Time in the update deck we published at the same link as this webcast. During this call, we will discuss our business outlook and make forward-looking statements. These comments are based on our predictions and expectations as of today. Actual events or results could differ materially due to a number of risks and uncertainties, including those mentioned in our most recent filings with the SEC. During the question-and-answer portion of today's call, please limit yourself to one question and one follow-up. Please use the raise-hand button to join the question queue. Before we jump into Q&A, Elon has some opening remarks. Elon? Thank you. So, I think we've got a very exciting year ahead of us with 2026. We're going to be substantially increasing our investments in the future, so you should expect to see a very significant increase in capital expenditures. But I think it is well-justified for a substantially increased future revenue stream. Obviously, Tesla's not alone in this. I think you've seen in most, if not all, certainly the major technology companies substantially increasing their capital investments, and we're going to be doing the same. I think it's going to pay off in a very big way. So, we're investing in and improving our core technologies: battery, powertrain, AI software, AI training, chip design, and manufacturing expansion. We are laying the groundwork for significantly increased manufacturing production. We are also strengthening our supply chain across the board—batteries, energy, AI Silicon and everything—and laying the groundwork, like I said, for what we expect to be a significant increase in vehicle production in the future. And of course, we are looking at a very significant increase, well, actually releasing Optimus, but increasing our internal production for testing. Then, we will probably be able to have Optimus be useful outside of Tesla sometime next year. As you've heard me say a few times, I think Optimus will be our biggest product—not just Tesla's biggest product ever, but probably the biggest product ever. I remain convinced of that conclusion. So, on the vehicle side, it's always, I think, worth noting that the Tesla car is incredible value for money, and they're all autonomy ready, depending on what part of the world you're in. The supervised full self-driving is getting extremely good. We have just started production of Cybercab and will begin production of our semi-truck soon. I should say whenever you have a new product with a completely new supply chain, new everything, it's always a stretched-out S-curve. So you should expect that initial production of Cybercab and Semi will be very slow, but then ramping up and going kind of exponential towards the end of the year and certainly next year. In fact, we'll be ramping up production of all vehicles and all factories to the best of our ability through the balance of this year. On the energy front, the United States and the whole world will need a lot of energy storage to meet growing electricity demand. The demand for our Megapack is very strong, and we're excited to begin production of Megapack 3 later this year in our new, world-class factory outside Houston. For full self-driving and Robotaxi, version 14.3 was a major architectural update and we have a whole pipeline of major improvements to full self-driving. We believe these will lead to unsupervised full self-driving being available anywhere in the world that it is legal to do so. And then there's a version 15 hopefully by the end of this year, but certainly by early next year. That will be a complete overhaul of the software architecture and will run on AI 4. At that point, we're really just increasing the safety level of FSD above human safety level even more. Meaning, I think even within version 14 we're significantly safer than a human, but V15 will take that to another level. We've expanded Robotaxi to Dallas and Houston, using the same software source and the Bay Area. The limiting factor for expansion is really rigorous validation, making sure things are completely safe. We don't want to have a single accident or injury with the expansion of Robotaxi, and to the credit of the team, we have not had a single one to date. For Optimus, we're preparing Fremont for start of production later this year. Again, totally new supply chain, totally new technology, so therefore the production S-curve is always very slow in the beginning, but it will ramp up to significant numbers next year. We're constructing a second Optimus factory at our Giga Texas location, and that will probably start production around summer next year. The V3 Optimus design is almost ready to demonstrate. I think we want to just make sure it's polished. Like it works functionally, but there's some aesthetic elements that need to be finalized, and I think probably middle of this year we should be able to show it off. We're also a little hesitant to show V3 off because we find our competitors do a frame-by-frame analysis whenever we release something and copy everything they possibly can. So, I think there's some value to, you know, not showing new technology until it's close to production. Congratulations again to the Tesla AI Chip team for taping out AI 5. That's going to be a great chip, I think, probably the best edge AI inference chip for edge compute that exists. And certainly, I think unequivocally it is the best value for money. The team did a great job and we already have a lot of momentum for designing AI 6, and we've begun to discuss ideas for Dojo 3. So this is all very exciting. We've also finalized plans for the Research Chip Fab on the Giga Texas campus and we'll start construction of that this year. In conclusion, Tesla is working on a lot of large ambitious projects. They're all very challenging, but I think they're going to be revolutionary. This is what the team does best: solve the hardest problems and build amazing products. And I'd like to thank the Tesla team for all their hard work and to thank all of our supporters. Great. Thank you very much, Elon. Vibhav also has some opening remarks. Thanks, Travis. So, 2026 has had an interesting start not just for us, but I think the world in general. On the Autos business, we have seen a resurgence in demand in EMEA and certain countries like France and Germany showing over 150% quarter-over-quarter growth in deliveries. In APAC, we've witnessed growth in South Korea and Japan, again in terms of deliveries. Even out here in the US, we've seen a slight growth in terms of quarter-over-quarter deliveries. On the order backlog front, we ended the quarter with the highest Q1 order backlog in over two years. Whilst the recent increase in gas prices has had a positive impact on the order rate, this improvement started before the uptrend in gas prices. This is due to the work done by the Tesla team in bringing more compelling and affordable vehicles to market. Ten years back when we launched Model 3 in the US with the promise of a $35,000 starting price—which if you adjust today for inflation, translates to about $48,000 in today's dollar terms—the starting price of Model 3 today is way less than that while the product is way more compelling from where it started. Given this setup, we're focused on increasing our overall production volume, something that we already started in Q1. This volume increase is evidenced by Giga Berlin reaching a record output of over 61,000 units in Q1. We plan to keep growing volumes further not just in Berlin but across all our factories. Our biggest limiter continues to be our battery pack capacity and we are actively working on resolving that. Auto margins, excluding credits, improved sequentially from 17.9 to 19.2%. Note that we've had certain one-time benefits from warranty true-downs around 230 million and some relief on tariffs. We have not realized any benefit from the recent Supreme Court ruling on IPA tariffs as there is still a lot of uncertainty around the final outcome. Both tariffs and sustained high interest rates continue to add to our automotive cost. Interest rates and subvention costs are recognized upfront. If interest rates continue to rise, our cost of subvention will continue to impact auto margins. On the FSD adoption front, we continue to see improvement reaching nearly 1.3 million paid customers globally. The bulk of the growth came from subscriptions while upfront purchases only increased 7% as we removed the purchase option in some markets in Q1. We recently received approvals for FSD in Netherlands. This sets us up well for an EU-wide approval later in Q2. And you know, we're just gated by how the regulators go about it. Additionally, we've also received approvals in China. The broader approval is still not there, but we're working with the regulators in the country and we're hoping that we can get approval by Q3. With these approvals coming through, we expect the broader adoption of the software in the existing fleet and incremental demand for our vehicles. With all this in mind, we have evolved our vehicle sales strategy where we now emphasize FSD as a product and the vehicle as only the delivery mechanism. As we've noted previously, the energy storage business is inherently lumpy, tied to customer deployment timelines. In Q1, we deployed 8.8 gigawatt-hours of energy storage, a 38% sequential decline. However, we still expect 2026 deployments to be higher than 2025. We set yet another record with gross margins in this business over 39 and a half percent due to some one-time benefits from certain tariff recognitions of more than 250 million from certain tariffs, which we paid in prior quarters. On a normalized basis, we continue to expect energy compression from here with increasing competition and tariff impacts. As previously discussed, tariffs in this business can have outsized impacts as most of the battery cells are procured from China. Our order backlog for this business is robust and we're doing our best to build not just based on existing demand but also on expected demand. Services and others improved sequentially from 8.8% to 9.2%. This includes a collection of efforts meant to support our customers like service centers, used cars, paid supercharging, parts sales, insurance, and even our robotaxi business. We're making deliberate investments in the infrastructure to help the robotaxi in the future. We grew the robotaxi fleet quarter-over-quarter and we expect to keep ramping the fleet as we accelerate and get into other geographies. On the operating expenses side, there we did increase sequentially from a full quarter stock-based compensation expense for the 2025 CEO compensation plan for which one milestone is still deemed probable. Additionally, our spend on AI-related initiatives, including expense on development of our own AI-5 chip and new products like Cybercab, Semi, Optimus and Megablock, etc., continue to be at elevated levels and we expect this trend to continue for the full year 2026. Net income was impacted from mark-to-market charges on our Bitcoin holdings, which depreciated 22% as compared to the last quarter, and the unfavorable impact of FX primarily from our large intercompany borrowings. On free cash flow, we ended the quarter with over 1.4 billion. As Elon mentioned, we are in a very big capital investment phase, which is going to start now and would last a couple of years. So, based on that, our current expectation for 2026 is over 25 billion of CapEx. And you know, just to remind you, we are paying for six factories, which we are going to go into operation. Some have already started, some would go into operation later part of this year. We're further increasing our investment in AI-related initiatives, including the AI infrastructure to support Robotaxi and the launch of Optimus. We've already started placing orders for the research semiconductor fab in Austin and for solar manufacturing equipment. While this may seem a lot and we will have the impact of negative free cash flow for the rest of the year, we believe this is the right strategy to position the company for the next era. We'll make such investments in a very capital efficient manner. We're actively working on our mission of building a future of amazing abundance. However, that requires not just a lot of investment, but an immense amount of execution. The future is going to be great, and the whole Tesla team is rising to the occasion to make this a reality. I would like to end by thanking the Tesla team, our customers, investors, and vendors for having confidence in us on this journey. Thanks. Great. Thank you very much, Vibhav. Now we're going to go to investor questions, starting with a question from say.com. The first question is, when will we have the Optimus 3 reveal, which we already touched on, but the rest of the question is, when will Optimus production start since we ended the model X and S production earlier this than midyear? And then, what's the expected Optimus production rate exiting this year? And what are the initial targeted skills? Well, as I was saying, what we found is that when we've unveiled various Optimus versions, we found out how competitors literally do a frame-by-frame analysis whenever we release something and copy everything they possibly can. So, I think we want to push the Optimus 3 unveil, maybe closer to production. Start of production is we're assuming it's somewhere around the late July-August timeframe. And I should say whenever you have a new product with a completely new supply chain, new everything, it's always a stretched-out S-curve. So you should expect that initial production of Cybercab and Semi will be very slow, but then ramping up and going kind of exponential towards the end of the year and certainly next year. And, in fact, we'll be ramping up production of all vehicles and all factories to the best of our ability through the balance of this year. The V3 Optimus design is almost ready to demonstrate. I think we want to just make sure it's like polished. Like it works functionally, but there's some aesthetic elements that need to be finalized, and I think probably middle of this year we should be able to show it off. We're also a little hesitant to show V3 off because we find our competitors do a frame-by-frame analysis whenever we release something and copy everything they possibly can. So, I think there's some value to, you know, not showing new technology until it's close to production. And at that point we're really just increasing the safety level of FSD above human safety level even more, meaning I think even within version 14, we're significantly safer than human, but V15 will take that to another level. We've expanded Robotaxi to Dallas and Houston, using the same software source and the Bay Area. And the limiting factor for expansion is really rigorous validation, making sure things are completely safe. We don't want to have a single accident or injury with the expansion of Robotaxi and to the credit of the team, not had a single one to date. And we'll be ramping up production of all vehicles in all factories to the best of our ability through the balance of this year. On the energy front, the United States and the whole world will need a lot of energy storage to meet growing electricity demand. The demand for our Megapack is very strong, and we're excited to begin production of Megapack 3 later this year in our new, world-class factory outside Houston. For full self-driving and Robotaxi, version 14.3 was a major architectural update, and we have a whole pipeline of major improvements to full self-driving that we believe will lead to unsupervised full self-driving being available anywhere in the world that it is legal to do so. And, in fact, we'll be ramping up production of all vehicles and all factories to the best of our ability through the balance of this year. And, you know, whenever you have a new product with a completely new supply chain, new everything, it's always a stretched out S-curve. So you should expect that initial production of Cybercab and Semi will be very slow, but then ramping up and going kind of exponential towards the end of the year, and certainly next year. And, in fact, we'll be ramping up production of all vehicles, in all factories, to the best of our ability through the balance of this year. Great. Thank you very much. The next two questions we've already answered about Robotaxi rollout and the data that we're observing. So, we will end on the last question, which is: what is Tesla doing to scale the energy generation business with solar? Residential roof deployments have stalled. Will Tesla move to regional solar and battery farms perhaps coupled to superchargers, and will we deploy solar through utilities? The overall US residential solar market is going through a bit of a correction after the loss of the homeowner tax credit last year, but we still see strong demand shaping up for the second half of the year. Tesla introduced a lease product this year that allows us to capture the tax credit ourselves and offer competitive pricing for homeowners. We have also debuted our own solar panel with superior performance and aesthetics, as well as our own best-in-class mounting system. That gives us a fully integrated home energy ecosystem. We believe, we strongly believe, that solar and storage markets globally will continue to grow at both residential and utility scale, and we will continue to invest in that growth. Great. Thank you, guys. And our next analyst is going to be Colin from Wells Fargo. Colin, please feel free to unmute yourself when you're ready. Hey, thanks for taking my question. A quick one first on the FSD adoption. So you have 180,000 new paying users this quarter, and I compare that to your overall installed base. It might be 15%, but then if I shrink that to the US, or to North America where most of them are, it's probably more like 30, 35%. And I'm trying to compare that to—you probably sold about 100,000 cars in North America in the quarter—so you're winning twice more FSD users than you're selling cars. And then if I add to that picture the fact that I guess it's mostly hardware 4 owners who subscribe to FSD, it sounds like most drivers in North America who have hardware 4 would already be using FSD. Is that the right way to think about it and the kind of success FSD is meeting today? Yeah, I think you're thinking about it the right way, Pierre. And the other thing which I'll share is that, you know, you can't just look at one quarter versus the other quarter in terms of churn, but we are actually seeing churn of subscribers also coming down, which again is a reflection of the product getting better. Obviously, if subscriptions are going up, that is a good metric. The other thing also to note is that we are seeing customers actually drive longer, which again, you could correlate it. That's why you have lesser churn because people are liking the product. And if you—I mean, I've said this before—if I just use my own personal behavior, right? I literally get in the car, I press a button and it just goes. Earlier I used to park, now I don't even have to park, and that is the experience which we want everybody to gain. That's why you're starting seeing it in the numbers come through. Excellent, thank you. And if I may, maybe a quick follow-up, completely different. It's more on the Optimus architecture and you talked about the partnership with XAI and Grok. I was wondering if you can share with us anything about how the system 2 intelligence is going to be implemented. Is that going to be onboard, on chips inside Optimus, or if we should think about like your fleet of like a million Optimus being produced a year actually driving very significant inference demand in data centers as well for system 2 thinking? Well, we think we can put a lot of intelligence locally in the robot. Certainly it needs to be enough intelligence that if the robot gets disconnected, like if it's a bad cellular signal or there wasn't Wi-Fi, Optimus can't just get stuck. It needs to have enough local intelligence that it can still do useful things even if it loses the connection, kind of like the car. Like the car does not need any cellular or Wi-Fi connection to be able to drive safely. So that's the... yeah, Optimus needs kind of a manager to tell it what to do, broadly speaking. Like if, you know, otherwise it's going to keep doing the same thing it did before. So you know, I think you need kind of an orchestration AI, which Grok would be good for orchestration. And then, for Optimus's voice, having a low latency, intelligent voice AI, Grock is actually very good for that. So if you want to talk to Optimus and have kind of a Grok-level conversation, you kind of need to connect to a Grok-level AI for that. But I would expect that the amount of interaction, apart from like the voice stuff and asking complicated questions of the robot, that necessarily needs a large AI model to answer. Grok would probably have about as much interaction with Optimus as a manager would have with the people on their team. Meaning Optimus could probably work for several hours without any management oversight. Great. Thank you, guys. The next two questions we've already answered about Robotaxi rollout and the data that we're observing. So, we will end on the last question, which is: what is Tesla doing to scale the energy generation business with solar? Residential roof deployments have stalled. Will Tesla move to regional solar and battery farms perhaps coupled to superchargers, and will we deploy solar through utilities? The overall US residential solar market is going through a bit of a correction after the loss of the homeowner tax credit last year, but we still see strong demand shaping up for the second half of the year. Tesla introduced a lease product this year that allows us to capture the tax credit ourselves and offer competitive pricing for homeowners. We have also debuted our own solar panel with superior performance and aesthetics, as well as our own best-in-class mounting system. That gives us a fully integrated home energy ecosystem. We believe, we strongly believe, that solar and storage markets globally will continue to grow at both residential and utility scale, and we will continue to invest in that growth. Great. Thank you, Mark. Did you have a follow-up question? Yeah, thanks, Travis. My other question was on batteries and the company mentioned batteries as a constraint on its growth. Can you speak more to how Tesla expects to resolve this, and to what extent that might come from ramping up your own LFP and 4680 battery cell manufacturing, or is this something that you'd expect to resolve primarily with increased sourcing from suppliers? Thank you. Yeah, so at the moment I think the limiter is not the cells itself, it's the battery pack capacity. And you know, like I said in my opening remarks, we're actively working on resolving this. There's more capacity being added as we speak, and I'll let Lars add a few more things to it. Yeah, thanks, Vibhav. As you guys may have seen in Berlin, we started launching Model Y battery packs with our in-house 4680 cells a few months ago, and that is ramping up nicely, adding to Berlin's output and helping with the demand surge that we've seen in Europe. As well, we're adding additional capacity in our Reno facility, sort of retooling it as it's been building packs now for, you know, almost 10 years, in order to put in some more efficient lines and get additional output out there. And then, you know, we continue to have growth in China as well, ramping in-house LFP module production and battery packs associated with that. So, all of those things are happening now and in the next months and that's really plans we laid out a few months back to increase that output with the growing demand. All right. Thank you, guys. And our next analyst is going to be Colin from Wells Fargo. Colin, please feel free to unmute yourself when you're ready. Oh, great. Thanks for taking my questions. Your chip suppliers generally generate pretty good economics on the chip they sell. Your approach has historically been on vertical integration. Part of that has been to get better economics. So, I know the longer-term goal of Terrafab is to get the supply you need, but how much of Terrafab is also motivated to get better economics on your mid-term chip purchases, and how long is it going to take to ramp to get to yield that achieves that type of economic parity? No, I mean, Terrafab is not some sort of mechanism to generate leverage over our chip suppliers. It's just literally we don't see a path to having enough efficient quantity of AI chips down the road as we scale production to high levels. It's the rate at which the industry is growing in logic, but even more so in memory. It just doesn't, you know, we just anticipate hitting the wall if we don't make chips ourselves. So that's the reason for the Terrafab. And then, but at some point I think it will make sense for us to convert all hardware 3 cars to Hardware 4, because that's what enables them to enter the Robotaxi fleet and have unsupervised FSD. And then there's a version 15. Hopefully by the end of this year, but certainly by early next year. And that will be a complete overhaul of the software architecture and will run on AI 4. At that point we're really just increasing the safety level of FSD above human safety level even more. Meaning I think even within version 14, we're significantly safer than human, but V15 will take that to another level. Excellent. Thank you. And if I may, maybe a quick follow-up, completely different. It's more on the Optimus architecture and you talked about the partnership with XAI and Grock. I was wondering if you can share with us anything about how the system 2 intelligence is going to be implemented. Is that going to be onboard, on chips inside Optimus, or if we should think about like your fleet of like a million of Optimus being produced a year actually driving very significant inference demand in data centers as well for system 2 thinking? Well, we think we can put a lot of intelligence locally in the robot. Certainly it needs to be enough intelligence that if the robot gets disconnected, like if it's a bad cellular signal or there wasn't Wi-Fi, Optimus can't just get stuck. It needs to have enough local intelligence that it can still do useful things even if it loses the connection, kind of like the car. The car does not need any cellular or Wi-Fi connection to be able to drive safely. So that's the... yeah, Optimus needs kind of a manager to tell it what to do, broadly speaking. Like if, you know, otherwise it's going to keep doing the same thing it did before. So you know, I think you need kind of an orchestration AI, which Grok would be good for orchestration. And then, for Optimus's voice, having a low latency, intelligent voice AI, Grok is actually very good for that. So if you want to talk to Optimus and have kind of a Grok-level conversation, you kind of need to connect to a Grok-level AI for that. But I would expect that the amount of interaction, apart from like the voice stuff and asking complicated questions of the robot, that necessarily needs a large AI model to answer. Grok would probably have about as much interaction with Optimus as a manager would have with the people on their team. Meaning Optimus could probably work for several hours without any management oversight. Great, thank you, guys. The next two questions we've already answered about Robotaxi rollout and the data that we're observing. So, we will end on the last question, which is: what is Tesla doing to scale the energy generation business with solar? Residential roof deployments have stalled. Will Tesla move to regional solar and battery farms perhaps coupled to superchargers, and will we deploy solar through utilities? The overall US residential solar market is going through a bit of a correction after the loss of the homeowner tax credit last year, but we still see strong demand shaping up for the second half of the year. Tesla introduced a lease product this year that allows us to capture the tax credit ourselves and offer competitive pricing for homeowners. We have also debuted our own solar panel with superior performance and aesthetics, as well as our own best-in-class mounting system. That gives us a fully integrated home energy ecosystem. We believe, we strongly believe, that solar and storage markets globally will continue to grow at both residential and utility scale, and we will continue to invest in that growth. Great. Thank you, Mark. Did you have a follow-up question? Yeah, thanks, Travis. My other question was on batteries and the company mentioned batteries as a constraint on its growth. Can you speak more to how Tesla expects to resolve this, and to what extent that might come from ramping up your own LFP and 4680 battery cell manufacturing, or is this something that you'd expect to resolve primarily with increased sourcing from suppliers? Thank you. Yeah, so at the moment I think the limiter is not the cells itself, it's the battery pack capacity. And you know, like I said in my opening remarks, we're actively working on resolving this. There's more capacity being added as we speak, and I'll let Lars add a few more things to it. Yeah, thanks, Vibhav. As you guys may have seen in Berlin, we started launching Model Y battery packs with our in-house 4680 cells a few months ago, and that is ramping up nicely, adding to Berlin's output and helping with the demand surge that we've seen in Europe. As well, we're adding additional capacity in our Reno facility, sort of retooling it as it's been building packs now for, you know, almost 10 years, in order to put in some more efficient lines and get additional output out there. And then, you know, we continue to have growth in China as well, ramping in-house LFP module production and battery packs associated with that. So, all of those things are happening now and in the next months and that's really plans we laid out a few months back to increase that output with the growing demand. All right. Thank you, guys. And our next analyst is going to be Colin from Wells Fargo. Colin, please feel free to unmute yourself when you're ready. Oh, great. Thanks for taking my questions. Your chip suppliers generally generate pretty good economics on the chip they sell. Your approach has historically been on vertical integration. Part of that has been to get better economics. So, I know the longer-term goal of Terrafab is to get the supply you need, but how much of Terrafab is also motivated to get better economics on your mid-term chip purchases, and how long is it going to take to ramp to get to yield that achieves that type of economic parity? No, I mean, Terrafab is not some sort of mechanism to generate leverage over our chip suppliers. It's just literally we don't see a path to having enough efficient quantity of AI chips down the road as we scale production to high levels. It is the rate at which the industry is growing in logic, but even more so in memory. It just doesn't, you know, we just anticipate hitting the wall if we don't make chips ourselves. So that's the reason for the Terrafab. And then, but at some point I think it will make sense for us to convert all hardware 3 cars to Hardware 4 because that's what enables them to enter the Robotaxi fleet and have unsupervised FSD. And then there's a version 15. Hopefully by the end of this year, but certainly by early next year. And that will be a complete overhaul of the software architecture and will run on AI 4. At that point we're really just increasing the safety level of FSD above human safety level even more. Meaning I think even within version 14, we're significantly safer than human, but V15 will take that to another level. Excellent. Thank you. And if I may, maybe a quick follow-up, completely different. It's more on the Optimus architecture and you talked about the partnership with XAI and Grock. I was wondering if you can share with us anything about how the system 2 intelligence is going to be implemented. Is that going to be onboard, on chips inside Optimus, or if we should think about like your fleet of like a million of Optimus being produced a year actually driving very significant inference demand in data centers as well for system 2 thinking? Well, we think we can put a lot of intelligence locally in the robot. Certainly it needs to be enough intelligence that if the robot gets disconnected, like if it's a bad cellular signal or there wasn't Wi-Fi, Optimus can't just get stuck. It needs to have enough local intelligence that it can still do useful things even if it loses the connection, kind of like the car. The car does not need any cellular or Wi-Fi connection to be able to drive safely. So that's the... yeah, Optimus needs kind of a manager to tell it what to do, broadly speaking. Like if, you know, otherwise it's going to keep doing the same thing it did before. So you know, I think you need kind of an orchestration AI, which Grok would be good for orchestration. And then, for Optimus's voice, having a low latency, intelligent voice AI, Grock is actually very good for that. So if you want to talk to Optimus and have kind of a Grok-level conversation, you kind of need to connect to a Grok-level AI for that. But I would expect that the amount of interaction, apart from like the voice stuff and asking complicated questions of the robot, that necessarily needs a large AI model to answer. Grock would probably have about as much interaction with Optimus as a manager would have with the people on their team. Meaning Optimus could probably work for several hours without any management oversight. Great. Thank you, guys. The next two questions we've already answered about Robotaxi rollout and the data that we're observing. So, we will end on the last question, which is: what is Tesla doing to scale the energy generation business with solar? Residential roof deployments have stalled. Will Tesla move to regional solar and battery farms perhaps coupled to superchargers, and will we deploy solar through utilities? The overall US residential solar market is going through a bit of a correction after the loss of the homeowner tax credit last year, but we still see strong demand shaping up for the second half of the year. Tesla introduced a lease product this year that allows us to capture the tax credit ourselves and offer competitive pricing for homeowners. We have also debuted our own solar panel with superior performance and aesthetics, as well as our own best-in-class mounting system. That gives us a fully integrated home energy ecosystem. We believe, we strongly believe, that solar and storage markets globally will continue to grow at both residential and utility scale, and we will continue to invest in that growth. Great. Thank you, Mark. Did you have a follow-up question? Yeah, thanks, Travis. My other question was on batteries and the company mentioned batteries as a constraint on its growth. Can you speak more to how Tesla expects to resolve this, and to what extent that might come from ramping up your own LFP and 4680 battery cell manufacturing, or is this something that you'd expect to resolve primarily with increased sourcing from suppliers? Thank you. Yeah, so at the moment I think the limiter is not the cells itself, it's the battery pack capacity. And you know, like I said in my opening remarks, we're actively working on resolving this. There's more capacity being added as we speak, and I'll let Lars add a few more things to it. Yeah, thanks, Vibhav. As you guys may have seen in Berlin, we started launching Model Y battery packs with our in-house 4680 cells a few months ago, and that is ramping up nicely, adding to Berlin's output and helping with the demand surge that we've seen in Europe. As well, we're adding additional capacity in our Reno facility, sort of retooling it as it's been building packs now for, you know, almost 10 years, in order to put in some more efficient lines and get additional output out there. And then, you know, we continue to have growth in China as well, ramping in-house LFP module production and battery packs associated with that. So, all of those things are happening now and in the next months and that's really plans we laid out a few months back to increase that output with the growing demand. All right. Thank you, guys. And our next analyst is going to be Colin from Wells Fargo. Colin, please feel free to unmute yourself when you're ready. Oh, great. Thanks for taking my questions. Your chip suppliers generally generate pretty good economics on the chip they sell. Your approach has historically been on vertical integration. Part of that has been to get better economics. So, I know the longer-term goal of Terrafab is to get the supply you need, but how much of Terrafab is also motivated to get better economics on your mid-term chip purchases, and how long is it going to take to ramp to get to yield that achieves that type of economic parity? No, I mean, Terrafab is not some sort of mechanism to generate leverage over our chip suppliers. It's just literally we don't see a path to having enough efficient quantity of AI chips down the road as we scale production to high levels. It is the rate at which the industry is growing in logic, but even more so in memory. It just doesn't, you know, we just anticipate hitting the wall if we don't make chips ourselves. So that's the reason for the Terrafab. And then, but at some point I think it will make sense for us to convert all hardware 3 cars to Hardware 4, because that's what enables them to enter the Robotaxi fleet and have unsupervised FSD. Excellent. Thank you. And if I may, maybe a quick follow-up, completely different. It's more on the Optimus architecture and you talked about the partnership with XAI and Grock. I was wondering if you can share with us anything about how the system 2 intelligence is going to be implemented. Is that going to be onboard, on chips inside Optimus, or if we should think about like your fleet of like a million of Optimus being produced a year actually driving very significant inference demand in data centers as well for system 2 thinking? Well, we think we can put a lot of intelligence locally in the robot. Certainly it needs to be enough intelligence that if the robot gets disconnected, like if it's a bad cellular signal or there wasn't Wi-Fi, Optimus can't just get stuck. It needs to have enough local intelligence that it can still do useful things even if it loses the connection, kind of like the car. The car does not need any cellular or Wi-Fi connection to be able to drive safely. So that's the... yeah, Optimus needs kind of a manager to tell it what to do, broadly speaking. Like if, you know, otherwise it's going to keep doing the same thing it did before. So you know, I think you need kind of an orchestration AI, which Grok would be good for orchestration. And then, for Optimus's voice, having a low latency, intelligent voice AI, Grock is actually very good for that. So if you want to talk to Optimus and have kind of a Grok-level conversation, you kind of need to connect to a Grok-level AI for that. But I would expect that the amount of interaction, apart from like the voice stuff and asking complicated questions of the robot, that necessarily needs a large AI model to answer. Grock would probably have about as much interaction with Optimus as a manager would have with the people on their team. Meaning Optimus could probably work for several hours without any management oversight. Great. Thank you, guys. The next two questions we've already answered about Robotaxi rollout and the data that we're observing. So, we will end on the last question, which is: what is Tesla doing to scale the energy generation business with solar? Residential roof deployments have stalled. Will Tesla move to regional solar and battery farms perhaps coupled to superchargers, and will we deploy solar through utilities? The overall US residential solar market is going through a bit of a correction after the loss of the homeowner tax credit last year, but we still see strong demand shaping up for the second half of the year. Tesla introduced a lease product this year that allows us to capture the tax credit ourselves and offer competitive pricing for homeowners. We have also debuted our own solar panel with superior performance and aesthetics, as well as our own best-in-class mounting system. That gives us a fully integrated home energy ecosystem. We believe, we strongly believe, that solar and storage markets globally will continue to grow at both residential and utility scale, and we will continue to invest in that growth. Great. Thank you, Mark. Did you have a follow-up question? Yeah, thanks, Travis. My other question was on batteries and the company mentioned batteries as a constraint on its growth. Can you speak more to how Tesla expects to resolve this, and to what extent that might come from ramping up your own LFP and 4680 battery cell manufacturing, or is this something that you'd expect to resolve primarily with increased sourcing from suppliers? Thank you. Yeah, so at the moment I think the limiter is not the cells itself, it's the battery pack capacity. And you know, like I said in my opening remarks, we're actively working on resolving this. There's more capacity being added as we speak, and I'll let Lars add a few more things to it. Yeah, thanks, Vibhav. As you guys may have seen in Berlin, we started launching Model Y battery packs with our in-house 4680 cells a few months ago, and that is ramping up nicely, adding to Berlin's output and helping with the demand surge that we've seen in Europe. As well, we're adding additional capacity in our Reno facility, sort of retooling it as it's been building packs now for, you know, almost 10 years, in order to put in some more efficient lines and get additional output out there. And then, you know, we continue to have growth in China as well, ramping in-house LFP module production and battery packs associated with that. So, all of those things are happening now and in the next months and that's really plans we laid out a few months back to increase that output with the growing demand. All right. Thank you, guys.

2026 Annual Wenger Lecture with Dr. Ngozi Okonjo-Iweala

AUWCL International and Comparative Legal Studies(0) ·61:21 ·0 views · 2026-04-22
Ngozi Okonjo-Iweala (Director General, World Trade Organization)
The global trading system is at a critical "turning point" where the World Trade Organization (WTO) must transition from a state of over-dependence and unilateralism toward "reglobalization"—a process of diversifying supply chains to include marginal...
TL;DR The global trading system is at a critical "turning point" where the World Trade Organization (WTO) must transition from a state of over-dependence and unilateralism toward "reglobalization"—a process of diversifying supply chains to include marginalized regions while...

TL;DR

The global trading system is at a critical "turning point" where the World Trade Organization (WTO) must transition from a state of over-dependence and unilateralism toward "reglobalization"—a process of diversifying supply chains to include marginalized regions while reforming foundational multilateral rules to ensure long-term peace and stability.

Current Disruptions and "Over-Dependencies"

  • Systemic Stress: The global trading system is experiencing its most significant disruptions in 80 years, fueled by geopolitical tensions, ongoing wars, and a rise in unilateral actions that bypass collective rules.
  • The Risk of Over-Dependence: Dr. Okonjo-Iweala emphasized that the system was designed for *interdependence*, but has devolved into risky *over-dependencies* in several sectors:
      • Market Demand: Excessive reliance on the United States.
      • Critical Inputs: Over-dependence on China for rare earths and Taiwan for cutting-edge semiconductors.
      • Health Security: During the pandemic, 80% of vaccine exports were concentrated in just 10 countries.
  • Physical Choke Points: Recent crises have highlighted the fragility of geographical trade routes.
      • The Red Sea & Hormuz: Conflicts and militia activity have upended energy, fertilizer, and chemical shipments, leading to global price spikes.
      • Climate & Accident Risks: Drought in the Panama Canal and the 2021 Suez Canal blockage demonstrate that physical vulnerabilities can cost the global economy nearly $10 billion per day.

The Strategy of "Reglobalization"

  • Defining the Concept: Coined by economist Bob Koopman, "reglobalization" refers to the restructuring of trade to be more inclusive and diverse rather than retreating into protectionism.
  • Diversification as Robustness: Moving beyond simple "resilience" to "robustness" requires drawing in regions currently on the margins of trade, such as the African continent, which has accounted for less than 3% of global trade for decades.
  • Supply Chain Adaptability: Despite recent shocks, global value chains have shown surprising flexibility.
      • Creative Logistics: Shipping companies are rerouting around the Cape of Good Hope.
      • Market Depth: When the war in Ukraine disrupted Egypt’s grain supply, the multilateral system allowed for a rapid pivot to sourcing wheat from the U.S. and EU.

Reforming the Multilateral System (MC14)

  • A "Member-Driven" Challenge: In the WTO, the Secretariat does not make decisions; the members do. Dr. Okonjo-Iweala noted that when members criticize the WTO, they are effectively criticizing their own collective inability to update the rulebook.
  • The Cameroon Ministerial (MC14): Described as a "turning point" ministerial, the conference focused on foundational reforms.
      • Core Principles: Discussions addressed the Most Favored Nation (MFN) principle, non-discrimination, and the balance of rights and obligations.
      • Plurilateral Agreements: There is a growing movement toward "plurilaterals" (agreements between subsets of members) for issues like e-commerce and investment facilitation to bypass the paralysis of full consensus.
  • Ongoing Negotiating Goals: Immediate priorities include a permanent solution for the moratorium on e-commerce customs duties, new disciplines for fisheries subsidies, and agricultural reform.

The Intersection of Trade, Law, and Peace

  • Trade as Human Instinct: Professor Patty Day characterized trade as a "fundamental human instinct" (Eat, sleep, breathe, trade) and argued that human history is essentially the history of trade.
  • The Rule of Law: President Jonathan Alger and Dean Heather Hughes highlighted that international stability depends on the rule of law. They noted that American University’s international law program (ranked #4 in the U.S.) is dedicated to fostering the democracy and stability that trade requires.
  • Trade for Peace: The WTO maintains a "Trade for Peace" program, operating on the belief that a rule-based multilateral system is the only sustainable way to prevent global conflict.
"The global trading system was designed for interdependence, not over-dependence. And these over-dependencies have fueled resentment, anxiety, and trade and political tension." — Dr. Ngozi Okonjo-Iweala
"Trade may not directly lead to peace at all times, but there is an indisputable connection between the two... the rule-based system is the only way to maintain peace in the world." — Professor Patty Day
"In a member-driven organization, when members level criticisms, they are in effect criticizing themselves. Because there's no WTO out there to hit at other than members." — Dr. Ngozi Okonjo-Iweala
"Studying the history of trade is studying human history... it's a requirement of a well-rounded education." — Professor Patty Day
It is really my greatest pleasure and honor to welcome you all to the 2026 Wengler Lecture on International Trade Law. Today we are extremely privileged and honored to have the WTO Director-General, Dr. Ngozi Okonjo-Iweala, here to speak about global trade, over-dependencies, and the future of the multilateral system. So, I want to give— thank you so much for being here. I want to also thank President Alger and Dean Hughes and all the wonderful folks and dignitaries from the WTO and from the embassies that are here with us, our students, our alumni, and our honored guests. I would like to remind everybody to please take time to put your phones off or put them on vibrate. I would also remind everybody that the parking here, if you didn't pay or you didn't put the code in, you should do so because they are very diligent. I am a Professor of International Trade and I have been teaching here for 29 years. I am very lucky to be teaching trade and I say that for two reasons: one is the importance of trade. I always tell my students it is a fundamental human instinct. You breed, you eat, you sleep, you trade. Studying human history and studying the history of trade is studying human history. And it's, I think, a requirement of a well-rounded education. I also have been very lucky that I started teaching in 1997, which was two years after the creation of the World Trade Organization. And that is why when I started to teach WTO law—and I have taught that consistently every single year—we were convinced, and I am convinced today, that the rule-based system is the only way to maintain peace in the world. We also have been very privileged to host the WTO Moot Court competition here many years. Our students compete in the WTO ELSA Moot Court every year; we're one of the two American schools that do so. Others do as well, but we've done so consistently. And thanks to the WTO and colleagues like Santiago, Angela and others, we have also brought students to Geneva every summer since 2011 to teach them about WTO rule-making and dispute settlement, as well as when we visit other international organizations in Geneva. In 2005, we hosted a conference here on trade and peace. I was instrumental in organizing that at the time, and we had John Jackson and Julia LaCarte Murro here at that time, and James Houton, the IMF historian. And they spoke about the complex relationship between trade and peace, and it became very clear that trade may not directly lead to peace at all times, but there is an indisputable connection between the two. We are not in 1947, we are not in 1994, we are in 2026, but yet we believe here, I think at the law school and at American University, that the multilateral rule-based system is vital. And that is why we had this conference today on reimagining the multilateralism for the future. It's really an honor and a privilege for me to introduce our dean, Dean Heather Hughes, who has been a great supporter to us and our international programs, to say a few words of welcome and introduce our president, President Alger. I also want to point out that there are these cards on the tables. If you have any questions, we may have a little bit of time afterwards. Please write them on them and pass them to the end, and we will see how many of them we can answer after the Director-General's remarks. Thank you very much. Thank you so much, Patty Day. It is just a joy to see this room packed with so many people engaging around these essential topics, and I very much look forward to this Wengler Lecture. It is my pleasure to introduce the 16th President of American University, Jonathan Alger. President Alger joined us in 2024 after a long and successful run as president of James Madison University. He's a nationally recognized leader on the topic of civic life, civil discourse, and the role of higher education in fostering democracy and the rule of law. So, that is what we do here, and here, at the Washington College of Law, where the world gathers to study law, we are so proud to have President Alger. Good afternoon, everybody. This responsive crowd—it's great to see you all here. Thank you so much, Heather, for your kind introduction. Thank you, Patty Day, for your amazing leadership, and thank you all for being here. I have to say, as a lawyer and someone who believes in the rule of law, it's kind of inspiring and encouraging to see all of you in this room today. And we also want to acknowledge and thank the Henry E. and Consuela S. Wengler Foundation for their generous support of this program, and specifically, of the lecture that we are about to hear. And I love this theme of Reimagining Multilateralism for the Future. So, that sounds like there is a future for multilateralism. I like that. That's encouraging. You probably all know that here at American University's Washington College of Law, international law has really been a defining strength for several decades here. This is not something that we are new at, at AU or at Washington College of Law. And I love the fact that Patty Day pointed out that trade and peace go hand in hand, and how important that relationship is, and how important the rule of law is to both of those things, right? To provide that kind of international stability and the international relationships that are so important for both trade and peace. Our international law program this year was ranked number four in the U.S. alongside Yale, Columbia, and Berkeley, which is pretty, pretty great, and it's a distinction that reflects of course the expertise of the faculty, the excellence of the faculty and our students together, and the dedication that they have to these topics. And again, it's a long-standing, far-reaching dedication that we have here. You've heard the curriculum has for quite a while had this course on the WTO, law courses here on campus related to the WTO, and the Moot Court competition. There aren't many law schools in the country, to my knowledge, that have that kind of depth in this area, and so we're really grateful to have the leadership here.
