And it's very fitting that today's Wengler endowed lecture features a global leader, of course in trade and development, as you heard, Dr. Ngozi Okonjo-Iweala. Dr. Okonjo-Iweala is the Director-General, as you've heard, of the World Trade Organization, the first woman and first African to hold this position, as I understand, and she is currently in her second four-year term as Director-General. She's an economist and international development expert with more than 40 years of experience—a child prodigy, obviously. She served as Nigeria's first female, and I love this, first female and longest-serving finance minister, I understand, and is the country's first female foreign minister. So many different hats. During a 25-year career at the World Bank, she rose to the number two position of managing director of operations. So, kind of a dull career, nothing much going on in any of these areas. She graduated magna cum laude from Harvard University and earned her PhD in regional economics and development from MIT. And this is very important: she's received honorary degrees from 21 universities, but there's one in particular that matters, and that is right here at American University. So she's an Eagle, everybody. So, we are delighted to have you with us. It is an honor and a privilege, and thank you for your leadership over all of these years and for giving us hope and encouragement for this important work going forward. Welcome to Dr. Ngozi Okonjo-Iweala. Thank you so much for being here. Well, thank you so much, President Alger, for that very kind introduction. I often joke that when people introduce me, I start looking around the room to see who they are talking about because I often don't think of myself in those terms. But I want to say thank you to you, and to Dean Hughes and to Professor Patty Day. Thank you so much; we know all about you and your work and we are very happy to be here. I must say I'm not here alone. I have some wonderful colleagues who are here. There are some old friends sitting in the back: Maria Pagan, Ambassador Santiago is here and his lovely wife, we have our former Chief Economist Bob Koopman, DG DJ Northquist who is here, Nicole, Dr. Bright, our Chief of Staff, and of course, Angela. And Ismail, oh, and Mike. Oh wow, they are—wow. What's going on, you know? So they've come to surround me. So, lovely to see you all here, and I'm very honored and I'm delighted to deliver this eighth Wengler lecture on International Trade. And thank you to you and your team for the invitation and the support, and of course to the foundation for sponsoring this. I know you've had a full day on multilateralism today, and I would have loved to be in the audience to listen and learn because sometimes we talk too much. You know, I spend a lot of time making speeches. Sometimes it's good to sit and listen. But unfortunately I was running from one meeting to the other at the IMF and the World Bank, so I look forward to hearing more about it. And it's great to be back here. You know, in 2022, I had the privilege of giving the commencement address to the School of International Service, and of course, then received the honorary doctorate which I treasure, as you heard. The following year, I returned for a public conversation on trade, with—with trade, the global commons, and geopolitics with your former President, a dear sister and friend, President Sylvia Burwell. And then, I started thinking to myself: I'm coming this time. It's becoming a tradition to come here to join you at AU. But you know, I feel very much at home because for so many years, a couple of decades, I was driving my children to school at NCS and St. Albans and passing this campus every day. So, now beyond its proud history of educating women, the AU WCL is a source of top-notch teaching and analysis, as we've heard, on the WTO, and there are not too many places like that. And it's also the alma mater of former and present senior legal experts at the WTO Secretariat. The WTO community thus owes a debt of gratitude to this school and to Professor Alay for her service to the John H. Jackson Moot Court competition, which continues to train and inspire the next generation of trade lawyers from around the world. I also liked what you had to say about the trade for peace. I think trade has delivered for peace, although many people now choose to forget that, and we have a Trade for Peace program at the WTO, which is run out of the Accessions Division, whose Director is here, Micah, so we are trying to follow in those footsteps. Let me now turn to my topic, which is global trade, over-dependencies, and the future of the multilateral trading system. In tackling this topic, I want to touch on the need for greater proactivity and urgency in looking at and tackling the challenges of choke points, if you will, confronting global trade today, whether they are through trade rules and policies, or even physical trade choke points. And in doing this, look at the role of the WTO and what we try to accomplish in Yaoundé. I tend to begin my talks these days by saying that it's the understatement of the century that the global trading system is experiencing the worst disruptions since the past 80 years, in the way that it was created in the wake of the Second World War. Unilateral actions have disrupted global trade and global trade rules. Companies, consumers, and countries around the world are grappling with the impacts of these disruptions, as well as with the wider uncertainty associated with ongoing wars, conflicts, and geopolitical tensions. The disruptions and unilateral actions did not happen in a vacuum. They were fueled by perceptions within the United States and elsewhere that the trading system is not working as well for its participants as it should. The U.S. has long complained about the lack of a level playing field for fair competition, and about inadequate transparency with respect to some members' use of subsidies and other behind-the-border policies. Many emerging markets and developing countries have had complaints of their own, not only about level playing field issues with respect to agriculture for instance, but also about inadequate policies based on industrialization. The poorest WTO members have complained about their less than satisfactory integration into the global trading system, with shares of global trade of some continents like Africa hovering below 3% for decades now. And the fact is, that most of these complaints and criticisms are valid. The problem is that over time, WTO members have not been able to overcome their differences and to tackle these problems and deliver effective responses, which is what needs to happen for a member-driven organization to change. WTO members' collective inability to update the global trade rulebook has contributed to the frustration and unilateralism we now see. But permit me to dwell on this for a minute. The WTO is a very interesting body. I can tell you one thing: I can't complain of boredom. I can complain of other things. It's a body where members take pride in saying that it is a member-driven organization. Members take the action and make the decisions. And this is great. We like it. Ownership by members. The WTO is not the Secretariat or the Director-General. If I even try to claim too much about my role, vis-a-vis the WTO, I'm quickly and firmly put in my place. So, in a member-driven organization, when members level criticisms, they are in effect criticizing themselves. Because there's no WTO out there to hit at other than members. The point is, that to deal with these valid criticisms, members need to find a collective way forward to get to solutions and actions. In parallel to members' complaints, it became clear, especially during the COVID-19 pandemic and its aftermath, that the trading system had built in some over-dependencies. Over-dependence on the U.S. for market demand, over-dependence on China for rare earths and other key supplies, over-dependence on Chinese Taipei for cutting-edge semiconductors, over-dependence on only 10 countries worldwide for more than 80% of the global vaccine exports, just to cite some examples. The global trading system was designed for interdependence, not over-dependence. And these over-dependencies have fueled resentment, anxiety, and trade and political tension. Over the past 6 weeks, the Red Sea has reminded us that vital global supply lines are also over-dependent on a small number of geographical choke points. The crisis in the Strait of Hormuz has upended a large share of global trade in energy, fertilizer, and vital chemical inputs for semiconductors and other key goods. This has resulted in price spikes in energy, fertilizer, and petrochemical products worldwide, and export restrictions, subsidies, and other policy responses, with major implications for global economic stability, growth, and food security. In fact, recent years have provided several examples, albeit at smaller scale, of why geography and physical choke points still matter, even in our increasingly digital and intangible economies. Militias in Yemen were able to disrupt shipping through the Red Sea and the Suez Canal. Mining of the Black Sea as part of the war on Ukraine disrupted global supplies of grain and fertilizer from that region, causing high food prices in 2022. Drought in Central America has repeatedly forced reductions in shipping volumes through the Panama Canal. And in 2021, a single ship ran aground in the Suez Canal for 6 days, backing up hundreds of ships and affecting nearly $10 billion a day of trade. A recent article in the Economist listed several more vulnerable high-traffic shipping routes, from Turkey's Bosphorus and Dardanelles, which connect the Black Sea to the rest of the world, to the Strait of Malacca and the South China Sea, each of which sieves between a fifth and a quarter of global sea-borne trade in value terms. These physical over-dependencies fuel fears about supply chain fragility and add to the anxiety about a global trading system under strain. The point I want to make is that while these over-dependencies are real, and we must be more proactive rather than reactive in the way we tackle them, the trading system itself has shown greater adaptability and resilience. It provides solutions that can be built on not only to tackle challenges, but more importantly, to seize emerging opportunities. Logistics companies have been incredibly creative. Shipping companies reacted to the crisis in the Red Sea by sending more goods around the Cape of Good Hope, for transport costs only modestly higher relative to the value of shipments. Even with respect to choke points like Hormuz, although vastly costlier to circumvent, we are already seeing the Gulf countries start to pivot. Key goods are being trucked in from ports on the Red Sea and the Gulf of Oman. Saudi Arabia has stepped up its oil exports through a pipeline to the Red Sea that itself was built in response to conflict in the strait in the 1980s. The multilateral trading system, embodied in the WTO, has played a crucial role in building resilience. It has made it possible to source inputs and final goods from almost everywhere in the world, under broadly transparent and comparable conditions. In 2022, when the war in Ukraine cut Egypt off from its usual grain imports from the Black Sea region, deep global markets for cereals meant it could source wheat from the U.S. and the EU instead. This flexibility has allowed businesses and countries to reroute trade relatively smoothly, in turn, softening the blow of successive global shocks that we have endured. The result is that global value chains remain a central feature of the world economy. Even amid the tariff actions of the past year, the evidence is that supply chains have remained resilient and are rewiring. Firms are changing production locations, transport routes, and inventory levels, as well as temporarily absorbing cost shocks through lower margins. In addition, different types of goods and services are taking more prominence in global trade, helping build this resilience and propelling trade growth. At the WTO, we have seen resilience pop up in our numbers. Global goods trade volumes expanded by 4.6% in 2025, significantly faster than global GDP, fueled by trade in AI-related goods as well as front-loading of goods ahead of tariffs. Services trade volumes expanded even faster by 5.3%. This year 2026, we are forecasting slower but still reasonable goods trade growth of almost 2%, which could drop to 1.5% if the run war lingers on. For the future of the world trading system, however, the question we have to ask ourselves is this: How do we build resilience into robustness? What more do we have to do with our supply chains and our trade rules and policies to ensure continued predictability, stability, and reliability of the global trading system? For supply chains, you have heard me say it before, the answer lies in building on the already existing trend of adaptability to spur greater diversification and de-concentration. We have to build value chains to draw in parts of regions, countries and—yes—parts of the world that have been left on the margins of the global trading system, even whilst they make for efficient and effective production while also harboring some of the world's most valuable resources. We have to diversify trading partners and trading routes to ensure robustness and diminish the impact of external shocks or choke points, and in general, diminish over-dependencies whilst encouraging inclusion. At the WTO Secretariat, we have dubbed this "reglobalization," a term coined by our former Chief Economist Bob Koopman, now a professor here at American University and sitting in the audience. Thanks to you, Bob. And we have to look at global trading rules, WTO rules and practices, to see how improving these can help build robustness into the global trading system. We have to look and see what can be kept in place in these rules, what has to be reformed or put aside, and what new rules or arrangements have to be created to support a modern and better functioning global trading system. Ladies and gentlemen, friends, that brings me back to the issue of the complaints and criticisms of the system by members that I outlined at the beginning of the talk. WTO members in Geneva in the run-up to a ministerial conference in Cameroon and during MC14, worked hard to tackle the challenges of what has to be reformed. I have to tell you that I came into the WTO having looked at things, believing we needed reforms. In a fast-changing world where technology is moving sometimes at incredible speed, I saw an organization that if we didn't launch these reforms, we would be held back. I wasn't so much worried—although I was, maybe—about how to take advantage of what is happening now. I was thinking of the future of emerging opportunities that are so exciting in trade that we hardly talk about because we are so focused on the things that are depressing and don't work. We have exciting opportunities in services trade, in digital trade, in green trade. Do we have the rules to take advantage of these? So I was very concerned that we should reform. And the criticisms being leveled—well, maybe not every single one is valid, but I largely believed that most of them were valid and needed to be tackled. And my own sense of frustration is: why is it taking so long? And why has this not been done? So I was one of those very excited when in the run-up to the ministerial conference in Cameroon, and during MC14, when ambassadors worked hard to try and look at these issues. It's not that we've not been talking about reform at the WTO. We have. If you look at our papers you see a lot of talk about reform. If you look at our actions, you also see some action at the committee level, which is also very important, that some reforms were being done. But this time, I thought we needed profoundly different reforms to move this organization forward. And so, having Ambassador Petter Alberg of Norway, who was made the facilitator to make these reforms work under the aegis of the General Council, was exciting. It took 9 months. When he started, there were members who did not believe that—they believed the status quo was fine. They didn't believe we needed these reforms. In an organization where there's lack of trust among members, it's not easy to bring everyone together to accept reforms. But they worked very hard in Geneva, the ambassadors, and you have to give them credit. And at the end of the 9 months, I will call—I won't call it a silver lining, maybe a bronze lining, I've invented a bronze lining—began to emerge, in the sense that virtually all members accepted that serious discussions would be necessary among ministers in Yaoundé on foundational issues. These include core WTO principles of MFN, non-discrimination, and the balance of rights and obligations, that were being questioned by some members. Virtually all members also agreed on the need for serious reforms, looking initially at issues of governance and decision making, including greater flexibility for plurilaterals, which are allowed in the WTO by the way. Many don't know that. It's allowed. And they want to work in this way going forward. What the ministerial was also not able to deliver was an extension of the long-standing WTO moratorium against levying customs duties on electronic transmissions. Does everybody know what that means? Yes. I think sometimes when I try to describe it to leaders, their eyes glaze over, but this is a special audience. We also hoped to incorporate the Plurilateral Agreement on Investment Facilitation for Development into the WTO rule book provisionally. Launch a new Plurilateral Agreement on e-commerce, and give a renewed push to negotiations on agriculture and additional fisheries subsidies disciplines, and also have some decisions on development issues. So we had a number of objectives. So how did we do? To sum up MC14, we worked our tails off negotiating non-stop till the wee hours of the morning for 4 days. We got close to our main objectives. We delivered some, but we ran out of time to complete the package. First of all, despite the conflict in the Middle East, attendance was good, in line with all but one previous ministerial. The 120 delegations present were led by 102 ministers, of which 77 were full ministers and 25 were vice ministers. Only the Abu Dhabi MC 13 has surpassed this recently with 120 ministers in attendance. And the ministerial delivered on the most important objective—some have quoted me saying this is a turning point, this was a turning point ministerial, and I termed it that, because to me, a very important part of what we had to deliver was the reforms. And if ministers could not come together to agree on these reforms—and I was really afraid that you know, we were either going at a turning point to the good, or it would be a turning point to the not so good. I think this is what reform is about: at least have the debate to say, "Okay, maybe this is not the place to have these kinds of things," or maybe we need to amend our rules, that there are some subsidies that are good and might help us deal with this or not. So there are reasons why we have this number, and and we're not—this is not an issue. I think sometimes when I try to describe it to leaders, their eyes glaze over, but this is a special audience. We also hoped to incorporate the Plurilateral Agreement on Investment Facilitation for Development into the WTO rule book provisionally. Launch a new Plurilateral Agreement on e-commerce, and give a renewed push to negotiations on agriculture and additional fisheries subsidies disciplines, and also have some decisions on development issues. So we had a number of objectives. So how did we do? To sum up MC14, we worked our tails off, negotiating non-stop till the wee hours of the morning for 4 days. We got close to our main objectives. We delivered some, but we ran out of time to complete the package. First of all, despite the conflict in the Middle East, attendance was good, in line with all but one previous ministerial. The 120 delegations present were led by 102 ministers, of which 77 were full ministers and 25 were vice ministers. Only the Abu Dhabi MC 13 has surpassed this recently with 120 ministers in attendance. And the ministerial delivered on the most important objective; some have quoted me saying this is a turning point. This was a turning point ministerial, and I termed it that because to me, a very important part of what we had to deliver was the reforms. And if ministers could not come together to agree on these reforms—and I was really afraid that, you know, we were either going at a turning point to the good, or it would be a turning point to the not so good. I think this is what reform is about, at least have the debate to say, "Okay, maybe this is not the place to have these kinds of things," or maybe we need to amend our rules, that there are some subsidies that are good and might help us deal with this or not. And if ministers could not come together to agree on these reforms, I was really afraid that, you know, we were either going at a turning point to the good, or it would be a turning point to the not-so-good. Only the Abu Dhabi MC13 has surpassed this recently with 120 ministers in attendance. And the ministerial delivered on the most important objective. Some have quoted me saying this is a turning point; this was a turning point ministerial, and I termed it that because, to me, a very important part of what we had to deliver was the reforms. And if ministers could not come together to agree on these reforms, I was really afraid that, you know, we were either going at a turning point to the good, or it would be a turning point to the not-so-good. I think this is what reform is about—at least have the debate to say, "Okay, maybe this is not the place to have these kinds of things," or maybe we need to amend our rules, that there are some subsidies that are good and might help us deal with this or not. So there are reasons why we have this number and we're not—this is not an issue. I think sometimes when I try to describe it to leaders, their eyes glaze over, but this is a special audience. We also hoped to incorporate the Plurilateral Agreement on Investment Facilitation for Development into the WTO rule book provisionally. Launch a new plurilateral agreement on e-commerce, and give a renewed push to negotiations on agriculture and additional fisheries subsidies disciplines, and also have some decisions on development issues. So, we had a number of objectives. So, how did we do? To sum up MC14, we worked our tails off negotiating non-stop till the wee hours of the morning for four days. We got close to our main objectives. We delivered some, but we ran out of time to complete the package. First of all, despite the conflict in the Middle East, attendance was good, in line with all but one previous ministerial. The 120 delegations present were led by 102 ministers, of which 77 were full ministers and 25 were vice ministers. Only the Abu Dhabi MC13 has surpassed this recently with 120 ministers in attendance. And the ministerial delivered on the most important objective. Some have quoted me saying this is a turning point; this was a turning point ministerial, and I termed it that because, to me, a very important part of what we had to deliver was the reforms. And if ministers could not come together to agree on these reforms, I was really afraid that, you know, we were either going at a turning point to the good, or it would be a turning point to the not-so-good. I think this is what reform is about—at least have the debate to say, "Okay, maybe this is not the place to have these kinds of things," or maybe we need to amend our rules, that there are some subsidies that are good and might help us deal with this or not. So there are reasons why we have this number and we're not—this is not an issue. I think sometimes when I try to describe it to leaders, their eyes glaze over, but this is a special audience. Only the Abu Dhabi MC13 has surpassed this recently with 120 ministers in attendance. And the ministerial delivered on the most important objective. Some have quoted me saying this is a turning point; this was a turning point ministerial, and I termed it that because, to me, a very important part of what we had to deliver was the reforms. And if ministers could not come together to agree on these reforms—and I was really afraid that, you know, we were either going at a turning point to the good, or it would be a turning point to the not so good. I think this is what reform is about: at least have the debate to say, "Okay, maybe this is not the place to have these kinds of things," or maybe we need to amend our rules, that there are some subsidies that are good and might help us deal with this or not. So, there are reasons why we have this number and and we're not—this is not an issue. I think sometimes when I try to describe it to leaders, their eyes glaze over, but this is a special audience. We also hoped to incorporate the Plurilateral Agreement on Investment Facilitation for Development into the WTO rulebook provisionally. We wanted to launch a new Plurilateral Agreement on e-commerce, and give a renewed push to negotiations on agriculture and additional fisheries subsidies disciplines, and also have some decisions on development issues. So, we had a number of objectives. So, how did we do? To sum up MC14, we worked our tails off negotiating nonstop till the wee hours of the morning for 4 days. We got close to our main objectives. We delivered some, but we ran out of time to complete the package. First of all, despite the conflict in the Middle East, attendance was good, in line with all but one previous ministerial. The 120 delegations present were led by 102 ministers, of which 77 were full ministers and 25 were vice ministers. Only the Abu Dhabi MC13 has surpassed this recently with 120 ministers in attendance. And the ministerial delivered on the most important objective. Some have quoted me saying this is a turning point; this was a turning point ministerial, and I termed it that because, to me, a very important part of what we had to deliver was the reforms. And if ministers could not come together to agree on these reforms—and I was really afraid that, you know, we were either going at a turning point to the good, or it would be a turning point to the not so good. I think this is what reform is about: at least have the debate to say, "Okay, maybe this is not the place to have these kinds of things," or maybe we need to amend our rules, that there are some subsidies that are good and might help us deal with this or not. Only the Abu Dhabi MC13 has surpassed this recently with 120 ministers in attendance. And the ministerial delivered on the most important objective. Some have quoted me saying this is a turning point; this was a turning point ministerial, and I termed it that because, to me, a very important part of what we had to deliver was the reforms. And if ministers could not come together to agree on these reforms—and I was really afraid that, you know, we were either going at a turning point to the good, or it would be a turning point to the not so good. I think this is what reform is about: at least have the debate to say, "Okay, maybe this is not the place to have these kinds of things," or maybe we need to amend our rules, that there are some subsidies that are good and might help us deal with this or not. So, there are reasons why we have this number and and we're not—this is not an issue. I think sometimes when I try to describe it to leaders, their eyes glaze over, but this is a special audience. We also hoped to incorporate the Plurilateral Agreement on Investment Facilitation for Development into the WTO rulebook provisionally. We wanted to launch a new Plurilateral Agreement on e-commerce, and give a renewed push to negotiations on agriculture and additional fisheries subsidies disciplines, and also have some decisions on development issues. So, we had a number of objectives. So, how did we do? To sum up MC14, we worked our tails off negotiating nonstop till the wee hours of the morning for 4 days. We got close to our main objectives. We delivered some, but we ran out of time to complete the package. First of all, despite the conflict in the Middle East, attendance was good, in line with all but one previous ministerial. The 120 delegations present were led by 102 ministers, of which 77 were full ministers and 25 were vice ministers. Only the Abu Dhabi MC13 has surpassed this recently with 120 ministers in attendance. And the ministerial delivered on the most important objective. Some have quoted me saying this is a turning point; this was a turning point ministerial, and I termed it that because, to me, a very important part of what we had to deliver was the reforms. And if ministers could not come together to agree on these reforms—and I was really afraid that, you know, we were either going at a turning point to the good, or it would be a turning point to the not so good. I think this is what reform is about: at least have the debate to say, "Okay, maybe this is not the place to have these kinds of things," or maybe we need to amend our rules, that there are some subsidies that are good and might help us deal with this or not. So, there are reasons why we have this number and and we're not—this is not an issue. I think sometimes when I try to describe it to leaders, their eyes glaze over, but this is a special audience. Some have quoted me saying this is a turning point; this was a turning point ministerial, and I termed it that because to me, a very important part of what we had to deliver was the reforms. And if ministers could not come together to agree on these reforms, and I was really afraid that you know, we were either going at a turning point to the good, or it would be a turning point to the not so good. I think this is what reform is about: at least have the debate to say, "Okay, maybe this is not the place to have these kinds of things," or maybe we need to amend our rules, that there are some subsidies that are good and might help us deal with this or not. So there are reasons why we have this number and—and we're not—this is not an issue. I think sometimes when I try to describe it to leaders, their eyes glaze over, but this is a special audience. We also hoped to incorporate the Plurilateral Agreement on Investment Facilitation for Development into the WTO rule book provisionally. Launch a new Plurilateral Agreement on e-commerce, and give a renewed push to negotiations on agriculture and additional fisheries subsidies disciplines, and also have some decisions on development issues. So we had a number of objectives. So how did we do? To sum up MC14, we worked our tails off negotiating non-stop till the wee hours of the morning for 4 days. We got close to our main objectives. We delivered some, but we ran out of time to complete the package. First of all, despite the conflict in the Middle East, attendance was good, in line with all but one previous ministerial. The 120 delegations present were led by 102 ministers, of which 77 were full ministers and 25 were vice ministers. Only the Abu Dhabi MC 13 has surpassed this recently with 120 ministers in attendance. And the ministerial delivered on the most important objective. Some have quoted me saying this is a turning point; this was a turning point ministerial, and I termed it that because to me, a very important part of what we had to deliver was the reforms. And if ministers could not come together to agree on these reforms, and I was really afraid that you know, we were either going at a turning point to the good, or it would be a turning point to the not so good. I think this is what reform is about: at least have the debate to say, "Okay, maybe this is not the place to have these kinds of things," or maybe we need to amend our rules, that there are some subsidies that are good and might help us deal with this or not. So there are reasons why we have this number and—and we're not—this is not an issue. I think sometimes when I try to describe it to leaders, their eyes glaze over, but this is a special audience. And if ministers could not come together to agree on these reforms—and I was really afraid that, you know, we were either going at a turning point to the good, or it would be a turning point to the not so good. I think this is what reform is about: at least have the debate to say, "Okay, maybe this is not the place to have these kinds of things," or maybe we need to amend our rules, that there are some subsidies that are good and might help us deal with this or not. So there are reasons why we have this number, and and we're not—this is not an issue. I think sometimes when I try to describe it to leaders, their eyes glaze over, but this is a special audience. We also hoped to incorporate the Plurilateral Agreement on Investment Facilitation for Development into the WTO rule book provisionally. Launch a new Plurilateral Agreement on e-commerce, and give a renewed push to negotiations on agriculture and additional fisheries subsidies disciplines, and also have some decisions on development issues. So we had a number of objectives. So how did we do? To sum up MC14, we worked our tails off negotiating non-stop till the wee hours of the morning for 4 days. We got close to our main objectives. We delivered some, but we ran out of time to complete the package. First of all, despite the conflict in the Middle East, attendance was good, in line with all but one previous ministerial. The 120 delegations present were led by 102 ministers, of which 77 were full ministers and 25 were vice ministers. Only the Abu Dhabi MC 13 has surpassed this recently with 120 ministers in attendance. And the ministerial delivered on the most important objective. Some have quoted me saying this is a turning point; this was a turning point ministerial, and I termed it that because, to me, a very important part of what we had to deliver was the reforms. And if ministers could not come together to agree on these reforms—and I was really afraid that, you know, we were either going at a turning point to the good, or it would be a turning point to the not so good. I think this is what reform is about: at least have the debate to say, "Okay, maybe this is not the place to have these kinds of things," or maybe we need to amend our rules, that there are some subsidies that are good and might help us deal with this or not. So there are reasons why we have this number, and and we're not—this is not an issue. I think sometimes when I try to describe it to leaders, their eyes glaze over, but this is a special audience. And if ministers could not come together to agree on these reforms—and I was really afraid that, you know, we were either going at a turning point to the good, or it would be a turning point to the not so good. I think this is what reform is about: at least having the debate to say, "Okay, maybe this is not the place to have these kinds of things," or maybe we need to amend our rules, because there are some subsidies that are good and might help us deal with this or not. So there are reasons why we have this number, and we're not—this is not an issue. I think sometimes when I try to describe it to leaders, their eyes glaze over, but this is a special audience. We also hoped to incorporate the Plurilateral Agreement on Investment Facilitation for Development into the WTO rule book provisionally. We wanted to launch a new Plurilateral Agreement on e-commerce, give a renewed push to negotiations on agriculture and additional fisheries subsidies disciplines, and also have some decisions on development issues. So we had a number of objectives. So, how did we do? To sum up MC14, we worked our tails off negotiating non-stop till the wee hours of the morning for four days. We got close to our main objectives. We delivered some, but we ran out of time to complete the package. First of all, despite the conflict in the Middle East, attendance was good, in line with all but one previous ministerial. The 120 delegations present were led by 102 ministers, of which 77 were full ministers and 25 were vice ministers. Only the Abu Dhabi MC13 has surpassed this recently with 120 ministers in attendance. And the ministerial delivered on the most important objective. Some have quoted me saying this is a turning point; this was a turning point ministerial, and I termed it that because to me, a very important part of what we had to deliver was the reforms. And if ministers could not come together to agree on these reforms, I was really afraid that, you know, we were either going at a turning point to the good, or it would be a turning point to the not so good. I think this is what reform is about: at least having the debate to say, "Okay, maybe this is not the place to have these kinds of things," or maybe we need to amend our rules, because there are some subsidies that are good and might help us deal with this or not. So there are reasons why we have this number, and we're not—this is not an issue. I think sometimes when I try to describe it to leaders, their eyes glaze over, but this is a special audience. We also hoped to incorporate the Plurilateral Agreement on Investment Facilitation for Development into the WTO rule book provisionally. Launch a new Plurilateral Agreement on e-commerce, and give a renewed push to negotiations on agriculture and additional fisheries subsidies disciplines, and also have some decisions

Marc Andreessen on how the internet changed news, politics, and outrage | The a16z Show

a16z(268.0K) ·65:58 ·7.9K views · 2026-04-22
Marc Andreessen (Cofounder and General Partner, a16z) Erik Torenberg (Host, The a16z Show) Theo Jaffee (Host, The a16z Show)
The internet has reinvented the 24-hour news cycle into "Randommonium"—a relentless 2.5-day viral outrage loop—which, while socially exhausting, may be effectively shunting historically high levels of physical political violence into purely rhetorical virtual combat.
TL;DR The internet has reinvented the 24-hour news cycle into "Randommonium"—a relentless 2.5-day viral outrage loop—which, while socially exhausting, may be effectively shunting historically high levels of physical political violence into purely rhetorical virtual combat. ...

TL;DR

The internet has reinvented the 24-hour news cycle into "Randommonium"—a relentless 2.5-day viral outrage loop—which, while socially exhausting, may be effectively shunting historically high levels of physical political violence into purely rhetorical virtual combat.

The Evolution of "Randommonium" and the "Current Thing"

  • CNN’s Origin Story: Marc Andreessen describes the founding of CNN by Ted Turner and Reese Schonfeld, centered on the concept of "Randommonium."
      • The Business Plan: Schonfeld believed there was always a "current thing" occurring globally that was transfixing and controversial. CNN’s original goal was to lock onto that one thing and cover it live and fragmentary until the cycle ended.
      • Breakthrough Moment: The 1991 Gulf War served as the ultimate proof of concept, keeping viewers glued to live Baghdad footage around the clock.
  • The Internet Reinvention: Modern social media has automated this process.
      • The 2.5-Day Cycle: Andreessen observes that every viral meme or moral panic has a half-life of roughly two and a half days. After this, emotional energy shifts to a new "current thing," and the previous one is forgotten, regardless of resolution.
      • Unpredictability in Politics: Because an election cycle contains hundreds of these mini-cycles, Andreessen argues it is impossible to predict outcomes based on current news; the election will likely turn on whatever "viral meme panic" is happening 48 hours before the vote.

The Global Village and Virtualized Conflict

  • Marshall McLuhan’s Legacy: Andreessen cites two key McLuhan concepts to explain the current media environment:
      • The Global Village: Media removes privacy and forces everyone into everyone else's business. Humans, evolved for a Dunbar’s Number of 150 people, are now mentally "melting" by trying to process 8 billion people.
      • The Medium is the Message: If TV turned everything into a "morality play," the internet turns everything into a "viral social media meme" followed by a search for scapegoats.
  • Declining Physical Violence: Contrary to popular belief, Andreessen posits that we are living in a period of historically low physical political violence.
      • Rhetorical Shunting: He suggests that "virtual combat" on X (formerly Twitter) acts as a pressure valve. In the past, political disagreements led to physical duels or street brawls with machine guns (e.g., the 1930s labor movements).
      • The Moral Defense: "If you're sitting at home scrolling social media getting mad, at least you're not out on the street hurting people."

Anatomy of "Ops" and Availability Cascades

  • Availability Entrepreneurs: Referencing Timur Kuran and Cass Sunstein, Andreessen identifies "availability entrepreneurs"—individuals or groups who deliberately inject specific biases into the public consciousness to start a "cascade."
      • The Rosa Parks Example: Andreessen notes that while Parks' cause was just, she was a trained activist used by availability entrepreneurs to trigger a specific, strategic social cascade.
  • The Rise of Dark Money: A significant portion of modern "outrage" is manufactured through "Ops" (operations).
      • Dark Money Influencers: It is currently legal to pay influencers to take moral or political stances (unlike product endorsements or direct candidate donations) without disclosure.
      • AI Doomerism: Andreessen points to recent "media fellowships" and influencer camps funded by non-profits like MIRI as examples of paid operations designed to foster public fear regarding AI.
  • Context Collapse: Most viral videos (e.g., Rodney King, the Central Park birdwatcher) begin halfway through the event. This loss of context is essential for the "moral tribe" to form and engage in a "rumble."

The Media Barbell: Legacy Decay vs. New Media

  • The Trust Collapse: Public trust in centralized institutions has been in free-fall since roughly 1971. Legacy media ratings are currently in a "full-scale collapse," leading to the industry being largely hollowed out or managed by private equity.
  • The Barbell Effect: Media is splitting into two extremes:
      • Short-form: TikTok/Reels/X for high-velocity outrage.
      • Ultra-long-form: The rise of 3-to-10-hour podcasts (e.g., Joe Rogan, Lex Fridman). Andreessen notes that completion rates for these long-form deep dives are surprisingly high.
  • The "True" Internet Election:
      • The Hybrid Candidate: Donald Trump is described as a "bridge figure" who is fluent in social media but remains obsessed with television "chirons" (the scrolling text at the bottom of news screens).
      • The Future: Andreessen predicts that by 2028 or 2032, we will see the first true "Internet Candidate"—someone who ignores legacy television and newspapers entirely to run a 100% digital campaign.

Notable Quotes

"The news is called the news, not the importance. If you could imagine a version of the news that's called the importance... nobody would buy it because nobody cares. People want the hot thing. They want the outrageous thing." — Marc Andreessen
"The internet version of 'if it's on television, it's a television show' is 'if it's on the internet, it's a viral social media meme.' If an alien invasion happens later this afternoon, it will be turned into a social media meme." — Marc Andreessen
"My theory is: social media wars up, physical violence down. If the virtual world can become arbitrarily rhetorically violent without people actually getting physically hurt, that gives people a way to exercise all of their anger." — Marc Andreessen
Each viral social media meme explosion is like a two-and-a-half-day panic cycle. It was not that long ago that grown men in serious positions of power and authority in the US got mad at each other; they would literally have a physical duel. And that was like a normal part of life. If you're sitting at home scrolling social media getting mad, at least you're not out on the street like hurting people. The fact that political violence is at like an all-time low in Western society is something that people really don't talk about. The news is called the news, not "the importance." If you could imagine a version of the news that's called "the importance," and the newspaper is like, "Here are all the important things that are happening today," nobody would buy it because nobody cares. Because people want the news. They want the hot thing. They want the outrageous thing. Marc, thank you for joining Monitoring the Situation. Good morning, I could not be more excited. Let's monitor some situation. Excellent. Well first we'll get a bit meta here and we'll talk about media. We've been talking about this idea, Monitoring the Situation, we've been talking about investing in it for almost a year now. And when we were first talking about the idea, you had brought up the book about the history of CNN. And you actually talked about this concept that they had called randomonium. What did you find so interesting about the founding or the history of CNN and how it could apply to what we're doing here or this broader media moment? Yeah, so one is I'm old enough to remember when CNN started, although I didn't actually have cable TV at the time so I didn't see it, but I remember it and the impact that it had. And then, because I'm an obsessive, there's this book at one point that nobody's read, but the book is called *Me and Ted Against the World*, which is a great title. So it's the founder of CNN is this guy Reese Schonfeld. And he basically is the guy who convinced Ted Turner to go into the satellite business. That sort of created, you know, essentially modern cable TV, satellite TV, and then, you know, led downstream to streaming and everything else going on today. And you know, this has been kind of lost to history, but Ted Turner was like a great, amazing example of exactly the kind of great founder that we work with. He was just this incredible force of nature, you know, a very controversial character. Like every other thing out of his mouth horrified you. He just generated a huge number of headlines of like, "I can't believe Ted said that" over and over and over again. So he was one of these real characters in the history of the media business. And then this guy Reese Schonfeld, who was originally the lawyer that wired up Ted's first satellite deal. And so the two of them came up with this crazy idea in like, I forget, 1981 or something. And they said, "How about having a 24-hour news channel?" And this at the time was like a just completely loopy idea. Actually, Ted Turner wanted to be a 15-hour a day news channel because he assumed that there would be nobody watching overnight, you know, the nine hours overnight. And Reese was like, "No, 24 hours. People are going to stay up all night to watch this thing." And the idea was—and by the way, completely alien to what CNN is today—the idea was this concept that Reese Schonfeld came up with called randomonium. Which basically says at any point in time there is, you know, whatever there is, something happening in the world that is like the most amazing, interesting, controversial, bonkers, compelling, transfixing thing that you can imagine. The current thing, exactly. The current thing. At any moment in time there's the current thing. And so he's like, "What should a 24-hour news channel be? Obviously, it should lock onto the current thing. It should then cover the current thing continuously, right, for as many minutes, hours, or days as that thing is the current thing." And then he called it randomonium because he basically said for that period you just put whatever you get, you put on screen. And so you get whatever fragmentary reports or man-on-the-street interviews or, you know, whatever it is. You just like slap the thing on the screen. And like, if you get it wrong you apologize later, but you know we're, you know, this is real time, it's live, and then you know people are just going to be glued to this thing. And actually that, okay, so that was the original CNN business plan. They then took a while to get up and running, but then they actually did implement this. And for our older viewers who will remember as I do, the it was the real breakthrough point was the 1991 Gulf War, the first invasion of Iraq. And that was the thing that really caused the whole thing to punch through. And there was like a week there where basically just like you were—just people were glued to CNN around the clock. Because they had, you know, it was literally live on-the-ground coverage of like, you know, they were in Baghdad with like bombing raids happening and all kinds of crazy stuff and the Iraqi government and like just the whole thing. And so it was just like the most incredible show in the world. And then they spent the next, you know, whatever 20 years, you know, basically trying to repeat that experience. And they repeated it every now and then with like the Monica Lewinsky thing was that for a while and the OJ trial was that for a little bit. But it was just kind of hard to come up with the next thing like that. And then, you know, over the last 20 years, you know, CNN has kind of become something completely different today, let's say, that's not that at all. And then basically what's happened is the internet reinvented randomonium. And so the experience of monitoring the situation on especially, you know, on social media and especially of course on X specifically. And then by the way also streaming—the other part of it is streaming and now there's obviously streaming on X but also streaming on other platforms, you know, YouTube and Twitch and so forth. And so the internet reinvented this and randomonium turned into, you know, the current thing, and then monitoring the situation, you know, legitimately, justifiably became a giant meme. And so it is time. Yes, it is clearly time to monitor the situation. And why do we think the amount of current things or randomonium or situations just is continuously accelerating? It seems like it's, you know, just the amount, the sheer velocity of these moments just keeps on increasing. How do we make sense of that? Yeah, so I think there's really two things that have happened that are really different and even, you know, different than in the days of cable TV and 24-hour news. So one is just like the internet for better or for worse, like the internet plugs you into the world. The global village. McLuhan. Global village. Yes. Marshall McLuhan. Okay, so let's cite Marshall McLuhan actually for both of these. So Marshall McLuhan, who was the great media theorist of the 20th century, yeah, he had two key concepts that are relevant here. So one is the global village. And so he had this concept of the global village. And the concept of the global village, by the way, he did not view as a positive. Like he was not endorsing it. He was just in his mind he was making an observation of what was happening. Which is basically what he meant by that is—you need to conceive of what he meant by village to start with. So village basically means a community with a small enough number of people where everybody knows everybody else. So like think about a small town or a literal small village. And everybody knows everybody else and as a consequence of that there's no privacy. And basically everybody's up in each other's business all the time. And I grew up in a small enough town, you know, a rural town where I experienced this. I saw what it was like and this is like that. Everybody knows everybody who is. By the way, it's the same people year after year after year. You like get to know them like way too well. Everybody's completely up in each other's business. Like there's basically no privacy. And so, you know, and by the way one of the reasons people leave small towns to go to the big city is because they want to get away from that. They want to get to a level of anonymity and have a lot more people running around. But the village kind of has that specific thing. And what he said was basically modern media is going to turn the entire world into a village. And so you're just going to be connected—and this is, you know, pre-internet when he said this, right—but it's really happened with the internet where it's just like okay, you're connected to everybody all the time. And by the way, like by default, you know, he would find this to be like a very entertaining thing that this is actually happening because he would say of course you're getting all the downstream effects that you're getting, which is by default it's going to melt your brain. Like the human animal was not evolved. The human animal was evolved to have like, you know, there's the Dunbar's number. You can basically have a direct relationship with like 150 people, you know, famously called the Dunbar number in sociology. But the global village expects you to have a Dunbar number of like 8 billion people, right, because they're all right there and they're all up in your grill and they're all telling you what they think of you and they're all like cursing at you and calling you names. And so it's just like this like really brain-melting experience. So that's part one. So we just, we experience reality differently as a result of that. And so the thought experiment I always have is, you know, we talk a lot about the impact of the internet, social media on politics. It's like well okay, like imagine having social media during World War II. We would have had a completely different national experience, you know, had like Pearl Harbor happened with Twitter. It would have been a completely different experience than the way people lived through Pearl Harbor in 1941 with primarily with TV and radio. And so anyway, so there's this global village thing. And then there's this other thing that he said which he originally said about television. He made this key observation. He said, and this is part of his thing where he said the medium is the message. So the actual medium of the media technology actually determines a lot of the message of what's received, a lot of the content. And specifically he said this thing. He said, "If it's on television, it's a television show." And what he meant by that is if something's going to be on television, it's going to end up being turned into the equivalent of like a half-hour comedy or an hour-long drama. Right? Basically, you know, the two native media forms of television are the half-hour comedy or the hour-long drama. And so you're going to get this basically very emotional, you're going to get this very polished and sort of glossy and produced thing that's going to be a story and it's going to run the way that a television story runs or a television show runs. And like, every television show is like a morality play. Right? Every television show basically tells a moral story of basically people behaving badly and then learning the error of their ways and then famously you have like the happy ending, where everyone kisses and hugs. Right. And remember *Seinfeld* was like a huge variation of that because remember the motto of *Seinfeld*. Larry David's motto for *Seinfeld* was "no learning, no hugging." And it was what made *Seinfeld* so unique because every other television show up until *Seinfeld* always ended with everybody learning and hugging. You learn the moral lesson and then you reconcile with the people close to you. And so every real-life experience would get wedged into the format of a television show and would be turned into like this basically this morality play. Okay, so the internet version I believe the internet version of "if it's on television, it's a television show," the internet version is "if it's on the internet, it's a viral social media meme." Right, if it's on the internet, it doesn't matter what it is. By the way, if an alien invasion happens later this afternoon, it will be turned into a social media meme. Like and it will go viral and that's what will happen. And it will be turned into a viral meme and it will spread virally across the thing. And then human beings what they are, that will turn into a moral panic. You know, that'll turn into full-fledged outrage and "how dare you," and people will line up on each side and sort of tribes will form and then people will go to war with each other on social media. And you'll have all these basically back and forth, you know, all this—both all the humor and all the rage of kind of how meme culture works. And so everything on the internet, basically whatever happens in the real world, becomes like a social media—basically a social media viral moment, meme complex, and then basically moral panic and search for scapegoats. Right, like a moral emergency. And that happens to every single new event that happens. And then you combine these two and it's like okay, for better or for worse, we're all in the same room now. We're like literally in the same global village and we're sort of surfing controversial internet meme moments over and over and over and over and over again. And as far as I can tell, like if you look at the numbers, basically what happens is each viral social media meme explosion, it basically is this huge spike up and then it's like this half-life decay and it lasts about two and a half days. Right? And so each social media basically experience is like a two-and-a-half-day basically panic cycle. And then what happens is the old one isn't necessarily ever resolved or reconciled, but what happens is a new one appears, a new current thing appears and just takes over the outrage. And all the emotional energy that applied to the old one applies to the new one. Everybody forgets the old one even happened. Like a week later you can't even remember. What was the social media craze of like seven days ago? I mean it might as well have been a thousand years ago. Lost to the mists of time. Nobody knows. Like seven days ago whatever it was was the most important thing that's ever happened. And seven days later it's like it never—not even a peep. Like nothing, it's like completely gone. And so you're just going to have this like basically repeated sort of emotional shotgun blast over and over every two and a half days, essentially forever. By the way, this is like—this is why one of my theories is like it's now impossible to predict the outcome of politics. So like everybody thinks they know what's going to happen in the midterm elections in November. And I'm like okay, it's now whatever, it's April 20th, the election is whatever November 6th or whatever. All right, how many two-and-a-half-day cycles are there between April 20th and November 6th? And you know the math majors you'd be able to calculate that in their head but it's I don't know, a hundred or something or 120 or something like that. Like whatever is the thing that we think is the thing that's going to tilt the election today is going to be a hundred social media meme cycles old. Like nobody's going to remember anything that's happening right now. The election is going to turn basically on like whatever's happening in the economy and then it's going to be whatever is like the viral meme panic that's happening like that day. And so and you could say like is this good or bad, I don't know. Is this better or worse than television, I don't know. By the way, is this better or worse than the days of radio or the days of—I don't know, the printed revolution that followed the arrival of Gutenberg, the printing press? Is this better or worse than the days in which you know we were all reciting epic poetry around the campfire as our form of media? I don't know. You could have like a big value discussion about you know which is better or worse. I have an argument I will make that this notwithstanding all the craziness this is better, but like this is the nature of the world we live in. And look—put it this way—this is we're now on autopilot. Like the shape of the media determines the behavior basically from here on out. And until there's something that fundamentally displaces social media, like this is the world we live in. Hence the need to monitor the situation. Amen. And is the reason why you think it's better is because even though there's more noise there's more signal and as kind of a collective global brain we get closer to the truth or? So I would say this. We get—so a couple things. Yeah. So one is look, I would say there you could say this, there are more truth and more lies. Right? I would say both of the following statements are true: which is there's more truth on social media than we had in previous forms of media and there are probably—probably—I mean old media had a lot of lies by the way, so it's hard to say there are more lies, but let's just say there are plenty of lies in social media. In fact, one of the native forms of entertainment of social media is trying to spot the ops. Right? By the way, maybe I should pitch you a show for MTS which is you know maybe every day we should do an hour on "let's spot the ops." Right? Like what, you know, which ones are real and organic and which ones are staged bullshit you know with somebody getting paid behind the scenes. And so, you know, and everybody of course focuses on the downside and so they focus on the fact that there's all this—all these ops and bullshit which is certainly true. But there's also just like the ability for somebody to tell the truth is like much more direct and clear than it was before right? Because of—because of, you know, the collapse of the gatekeepers. And so I think both of those are true. So I think that's true. And then but the slightly different argument that I would make is that previous forms of media were really good at generating physical violence. And so the—I mean there's many many examples of this in history, but the Spanish Civil War which was kind of the the war that kind of pre-forecasted everything that was going to all the sort of tragedy of the 20th century that followed. The Spanish Civil War was kind of basically the test bed for both communism and nazism in the modern world. And you know its native media form was posters. And so you literally—it was like the idea—the ability to do color printed color propaganda posters was like the new media technology at that time right before radio. And the Spanish Civil War was like super violent and then radio was the technology that led to—you know radio was the Nazi regime's native media format. Radio was the way that basically the the fascist regimes got established as well as by the way—as well as FDR who was primarily a radio phenomenon, you know kind of the rise of modern liberalism. And then television, you know, was the native medium of like just—you know television kind of kicked in starting around World War II, so it played a role, but it then you know television had a huge role to play in the Vietnam War and then in all of the violent riots of the '60s and '70s. You know, which were kind of, you know, super crazy both, you know, across the US and Europe. And so, you know, television has a long history of leading to downstream violence. The fact that like—the fact that like political violence is at like an all-time low in Western society is something that people really don't talk about. Because it feels like we must be in this like super politically violent world because everybody's so angry about politics all the time, but like measured political violence is at like an all-time low. And so my theory is the ability to directly participate in online virtual combat is shunting away a lot of the energy that in the past would have would have translated to street violence. Right? And so it's sort of this thing is if the virtual world can become arbitrarily rhetorically violent without people actually getting physically hurt and that gives people a way to exercise all of their anger and rage over politics and to be able to attack, you know, the hated enemy without having it actually be something that translates to physical violence. And so so yeah, my theory is it's like—I don't know, social media wars up, you know, physical violence down. Nobody wants to hear that because everybody's so mad all the time about what's happening online, but I think that character—like if you're sitting at home scrolling social media getting mad, like at least you're not out on the street like hurting people. So that's my—that's my moral defense of the whole thing. Well, it's fascinating because there's kind of a different kind of ideological sorting happening because people—you say things like, "Hey, you know, now they can't even talk to their own families because they've sort of found their tribe online that agrees with them." And you know, it's no longer the people they—you know it's no longer their families and back in the day you didn't have as much—sort of the logic goes—as much political polarization of the idea that I can't even talk to someone not from the same side. Part of that perhaps is because the internet allows them to find their tribe so you know so strongly and so clearly that they're able to you know have an exclusionary tribe as well. But I guess one response to the political polarization argument is that, "Hey, at least it's not going to leading to physical combat." What might you say more broadly—is it just overstated in general because people also have this idea of filter bubbles too, but of course in some ways it's the opposite in the sense that the internet exposes you to ideas that you wouldn't have seen otherwise. Right. Yeah, so I think people just have a really rose-colored glasses view of the past. The past gets sanded off. Like in part because we only interpret the past—you know number one is we weren't there and then the modern interpreters of the past, you know, are often quite honestly not telling us the truth about what happened because they have their own modern kind of gloss or spin they want to put on it. And so you—but just the edges of the past get sanded off in retrospect. But it's just like—it's really significant. Like if you just—the easiest thing to do is go on YouTube and watch old episodes of a TV show called *All in the Family*. Which was a—which was a very a very famous, successful TV show when I was a kid. And *All in the Family* was a microcosm, a fictional microcosm of basically the culture wars, political wars of the late '60s into the 1970s. And it was—you know famously this character Archie Bunker. Who sort of represented, you know, as this white guy and he basically represent—a great brilliant actor named Carroll O'Connor played him. And he basically played a retrograde basically Republican, you know, Nixon-supporting Republican in this would be in like I don't know 1972 or something or '74. Who just basically was—was painted in the show as like the most backward, like basically archaic, you know, knuckle-dragging, racist, sexist, just like the worst possible person in the world. And then there was his son-in-law played by a young Rob Reiner, who, Archie Bunker nicknamed "Meathead," which was the character became known as. And Meathead was basically just the arch-progressive liberal. Right. And you know, sort of like—you know think I don't know young Gavin Newsom or something right? Where just like he had just like the exact politically correct, approved view of the time and so he's like you know super at the you know the equivalent then of super woke, super progressive, you know, super enlightened on on all these issues. And the conceit of the show, you know, and the guy who created the show as this famous television producer named Norman Lear. And the conceit of the show was supposed to be a moral lesson of Meathead giving basically moral lectures to Archie Bunker every week to try to get Archie Bunker to stop being such a backward-looking like 1950s retrograde basically racist sexist guy and come into the modern world. And of course what happened was Carroll O'Connor—number one the actor was just like super funny—but the other was the political environment was super heated at that point. And of course it turned out Archie Bunker ended up being the star of the show. And so the show ended up being the opposite of kind of what it was intended to be. That happened later in the '80s, that happened with a show called *Family Ties*. Which was again—it was a very similar thing which it was these two parents, baby boomer parents in the '80s and they were like super, you know, it was very left-wing producers who made the show and the characters were like super proper left-wing progressives. And then there was this character in the show, Michael J. Fox, their son, who is this teenage kid, and he played the—I forget the name I'm blanking on the name of the character but he played the son. And the son basically was a Reagan Republican as a teenager, right? And literally the son would like wear like sport jackets and ties to school and carry a briefcase. And they kind of wanted to make him into like a stereotype of like basically a you know sort of a weird Republican, you know, kind of weirdo. And again the same sort of weird thing happened which is actually the Michael J. Fox character became—oh, Alex Keaton was Alex was the character's name. Alex P. Keaton. He essentially became the star of the show. And so I bring up both of those shows because you can see in those shows, you can see the culture wars of the '70s which were actually like super intense. You can see the culture wars of the '80s which were super intense. You go back before that and you have the Vietnam War and the all the campus protests and the civil rights movement and all the riots and all the forced everything, you know, dropping the 101st Airborne into, you know, rural South to you know basically desegregate the schools at literally at gunpoint. You go back before that you have the Cold War, you know, you have the Red Scare, you know, what's now known as the Red Scare but you had, you know, communists all through the US government and you had all kinds of freak-outs about that. You had the risk of nuclear annihilation. You go back before that you had World War II which was like this—just like incredible global orgy of violence. You go back before that you had the Great Depression and the you know New Deal which was hugely controversial. You go back before that you had World War I. And then you know—if you keep going back it's like you have the Civil War and then you have the Napoleonic Wars and then you've got the French Revolution and you know you go back further than that you've got the you know the democratic revolutions all through Europe and then you've got the you know the Gutenberg printing press kicking off all kinds of violence, you know, throughout Europe in the 1600s. And so I just think yeah—the history of Western civilization, American and European society, is just like constant strife and warfare to like an absurd degree over this entire period. And we go back and we study the history and we're just kind of like, "Wow, like those people were like really really really violent. Like really crazy and really violent." And boy did they really like killing. It was not that long ago, and I don't think it's anybody alive now, but it was not that long ago that like grown men—like in serious positions of power and authority in the US—if they got mad at each other, they would literally have a physical duel. Right? Like so, you know, you and I—you and I start going at it on social media and we get to a point where we get really mad and I challenge you to a duel and like we show up in a fucking field in a fucking park, you know, at like in the morning with guns, right, and we like square off and march off and we turn around and we try to like literally shoot each other to death as like a thing that they did and that was like a normal part of life. Wait Marc, I thought the past was all matriarchies and everyone just so peaceful and— Oh, everybody was so wonderful. Everybody got along. Everybody, yes, until social media everybody thought the same thing. Yes, exactly. And everybody was all, yes, everybody was all calm and happy all the time. And so look, the way of the world is conflict. I mean just historically the way of the world is conflict. And anyway, not to beat it to death, but the you know there was plenty of conflict in the past. There were plenty of heated disagreements in the future shape of society. There were plenty of political wars. There were plenty of violent wars. By the way, just politics itself was much more violent. Like if you go back and you read about political movements a hundred years ago, like they would literally form up in the street and like fucking try to kill each other. Like it was like a common thing. Strikes. I mean just even the history of the labor movement is full of this, like strikes became super violent and, you know, management would literally—you know, the strikers would try to attack their plants and the management literally would have like machine guns and they'd be like machine gunning their striking workers. Crazy, crazy stuff. And so like we just—we live in a much crazier world than we want to give ourselves credit for and the idea that like the current level of conflict is something new is just not true. And just to that end too, when people say, "Oh, ever since 2008" or you know, "smartphone" sort of, you know, propagating that it's led to increased depression or things like that... Is that also another example of, "Hey, we were underestimating how depressed people were prior and it's kind of a measurement thing," or is it, "Oh, but lots of other things happened in 2008 too, like you know, political extremism and other things that that make people more depressed." How do you engage in that topic? Well, so the funny thing is different Western countries show different data on the quote-unquote rise in depression. And basically it turns out it correlates to social benefits programs. And so basically if you get paid or if you get like special dispensation in school for having psychological issues, all of a sudden you see a massive spike in those psychological issues. The countries that don't have that—the other Western countries that don't have that—don't show the same results. And so I actually think that's a statistical artifact of literally like government benefits programs. So like I don't even—yeah I would say I don't even I don't even I don't even concede the you know concede the premise of the question, but that's a longer debate. I would just say in general like I guess what to say? I am prepared to say that basically—call it since I don't know, 2014 or something—I'm prepared to say like it does feel like things have intensified, right? It does feel like things are like wilder, you know, than they were before that. I think the most that I would be willing to say though is I think we might have lived through an era of what you might call suppressed volatility. And call that maybe from like the end of the Cold War through 2014, where basically it just looked like the West had won everything. And then also it was sort of—by the way, it was sort of the peak heyday of centralized media. So it was if you just—like if you basically track—if you track like how distributed is media versus how centralized is media, like centralized media sort of peaked somewhere around 1970. Where, you know, literally like all the newspapers in every city consolidated down to like a single newspaper and then you ended up with only three television networks and all the radio stations—independent radio stations—got bought up. Let me give you an example. So historically, like if you go back to the days of the American Revolution, it was common in—in even like mid-sized cities, you would have like 10 or 15 different newspapers. And each newspaper would represent like a different political point of view. And then the newspapers would have these like furious, you know—and they'd just like—just like you would do today online, you—they would have these furious constant political fighting and accusations back and forth and arguments. And of course the newspaper owners would be trying to egg all that stuff on because it sold papers. But it was like very common to have like many newspapers per city that would all fight with each other. And then the economics of centralized media meant that those all rolled up into—into a single newspaper. And then that single newspaper, you know, at least for a while tried to be non-partisan and tried to be even-handed because it was trying to appeal to everybody. And then—and so—and again same thing of just like, you know, like when I was a kid, it was three television networks that were all basically the same. They were all trying very hard to be even-handed at least, you know, kind of as best they could. There were like three national news magazines, you know, there was a small number of national radio networks, you know, there were two, you know, news wire services. And so I—like I will concede—like I think we lived through a period of suppressed volatility as a consequence of the fact that we had overly centralized media. I think if you go back before that—like if you go back to before that, if you go back to like the 1930s and before—like media was much more fragmented. Much more like it is today. And then if you—like I said—if you go all the way back like 200, 250 years ago, it was like extremely fragmented. Like if you read the accounts of like Ben Franklin as a newspaper publisher and like the stuff that he got up with his newspaper... So okay, great story. So Ben Franklin ran one of the newspapers in Philadelphia originally as a printer. Okay, so what would happen was Ben Franklin wants to become—he wants to become a commercial printer, he buys a printing press, he advertises whatever, he tries to get like all the local business to print pamphlets on the printing press. But he doesn't have enough business for the printing press and so he starts a newspaper to have something to print. And so he starts this newspaper. There's like 15 other newspapers in Philadelphia at the time, but he's got to fill the newspaper with something. And so—and he's Ben Franklin, he's very creative—and so he creates basically sock puppets. He does basically ops, he does alts—alts, he does alts, yeah, yeah, exactly, alts—and they're all under pseudonyms. And so he does—I forget all the names now—but he had all these different names for them. And I think at one point in his own newspaper he had like 15 different alts going at it. And he—he wrote them all but under different names and they all had like very different points of view and then he would basically set them off to have these like raging arguments with each other in his newspaper and people would buy his—people would buy his newspaper to cover this. And then that—that culminated in the election of 1800, which was the famous election of Thomas Jefferson versus John Adams.
