Which was sort of this newspaper-centric election which was characterized by a level of slander—like the degree to which partisans of Thomas Jefferson and John Adams attacked each other in the 1800 election is like way beyond even what we see today on social media. Like they accused each other of the most horrible shit you can possibly imagine, and it became like this giant scandal. And so like this is not the—the idea that there's like whatever rhetorical combat or like that there's like this virtual environment in which there's lots of points of view and there's lots of, you know, again both lots of truth, lots of lies... like this is not new. In fact this—this is the natural order of things. We just lived through a period of artificial volatility and artificial suppression of volatility with media becoming overcentralized. And—and obviously those days are over. I want to return back to what we were talking about, the current thing and how that's accelerated over time. We previously had a conversation a while ago where we were talking about sort of the attributes of a current thing and I remember you had this one theory that it can't be something too believable, it has to be almost surreal. I'm curious if we just talk kind of what are the attributes of something that becomes sort of the current thing? Or sort of the main topic that people can't stop quote-tweeting, sharing, etc.? Yeah, so again it's kind of this thing you kind of reverse engineer it. It's like okay, to be a current thing it has to—if, you know, again to be this thing of "if it's on the internet it's a viral internet meme kind of outrage cycle"... So it—it has to be something that like—it has to be something that activates outrage. Like it has to be something that activates the emotional kind of valence of—you have to get the emotional reaction off of it. Right. And so you know it's just like—by the way this is just like also the history of the press in general. It's just like whenever you, you know, I always say the news is called the news, not the importance, right? Like you can imagine a version of the news that's called the importance and the newspaper is like, "Here are all the important things that are happening today." Right. And of course nobody would buy it because nobody cares. Because people want the news. They want the news thing, right? They want—or you know—or they want—they want the hot thing, they want the exciting thing, they want the—they want the outrageous thing. And so the internet version of that is—right, they want—they want the outrageous meme. And so so it's got to be something that provokes. And you know—and there's something about this—you know this is other people made this observation—but you know it's like if—you know you kind of the ultimate male-coded version of this is professional wrestling, right? The ultimate female-coded version of this is *Real Housewives*. Like you know reality TV, like you know or I don't know *The Apprentice* maybe somewhere in the middle. We—it's just like reality TV—like there has to—there has to be sparks. Like it has to generate a level of emotional intensity driven by some level of outrage. And then I think it has to be the kind of thing where you can have tribes that form up and square off against each other. Right? So so you know there's a little bit like—like *West Side Story* dynamic to it or something right? Where it's just like okay you can imagine there's something that's worth arguing over. Where there's—you know—where there's a real argument to be had. Like there—you know—there's some underlying thing that actually like really matters. Maybe—maybe this event doesn't matter as much, but like the actual underlying issue matters a lot. And then it's—it's going to—it's going to activate this tribal effect where you're going to be able to basically—you're going to become part of what they call a moral tribe. The people on the other side are going to become part of a moral tribe and then you're going to kind of go to war with each other. You're—you're going to have a rumble. What they would have said in the old days. You're going to have a rumble. A rumble in the streets, except you're not going to have a rumble in the streets, you're going to have a rumble online, right? You're going to go at it. And so it has to lend itself to that kind of basically tribal formation and—and—and kind of that beating back and forth. And of course you—you the tribes then attack each other, you know, on the issues, but then they also attack each other's values, and they accuse each other of being horrible people. And then when it gets really into it, they start to dox each other and try to get each other fired. Right, and it all becomes, you know, very exciting. And so so so it's got to—it's got to have the—basically it's got to have the formation of—of a moral tribe effect. In the sociological psychological lens on this they call moral tribes and then there's this concept right of moral panic. It's got to lead to a moral panic. It's got to lead to this kind of thing of "this is indicative of how society is going to shit," right, in—in like some important way. And by the way, this is why you can have like many, many, many, many different examples of current things that end up revolving back around the same handful of like underlying political or moral questions, right? Because it's really—right, it's really not about the, you know, what happened, you know, Jussie Smollett. I just watched this new—there's this new Keanu Reeves movie on Apple TV where Keanu Reeves plays a movie star who gets canceled. And his crisis lawyer played by Jonah Hill—it turns out during the course of the show—I'll just spoil it—at the course of the movie it turns out his crisis lawyer set up the Jussie Smollett was an op, it was a produced op. So in the movie world the lawyer came up with the idea that there would be a black celebrity who would get attacked in the street by white racists and that would be like a viral social media outrage thing. And then—and then literally the lawyer says, "Well then of course we went on cast and Jussie Smollett had the best pitch for why he should be—he should be the black celebrity." And then—and then there was one of his underlings he's—he's still mad at because the underling was tasked with the assignment of going out and hiring, you know, the attackers. But you know this is in the heyday of diversity and so he hired two black guys to be the attackers. Right and he's like all confused because he's like, "Am I supposed to hire black people not hire black people?" and it's like, "Well not in this specific case, you weren't supposed to hire black people to attack Jussie Smollett." Anyway, so the Jussie Smollett thing is fresh in my mind. But it was a great example which is like what happened on the street in Chicago that night during the whatever whatever blizzard cyclone thing it was? Like kind of matters, kind of doesn't matter. Who ever really knows? Who knows whatever we've got weird grainy video whatever people say different things. I mean I think we now know what happened but like in these events like what actually happened on the street who the hell knows? But the fight—you see what I'm saying? Like the moral tribes that were able to form up on, you know, one side where people are like, "Of course this is what, you know, this is what happens to people all the time." And the people on the other side who were like, "Of course this is all fake and bullshit." Right, you had this—you had this chance for these moral tribes to—to pair up and fight. And so I think it basically—I think it basically has to—I think it basically has to have that. Yeah, and sometimes it's very serious and stakes are very high and sometimes it's just an episode that's so, you know, funny and the astronomer CEO HR as a—as an example. Okay, so this—okay, this very important point. So very important point. So I would say two things. So one is, the truth or falsity of the actual event doesn't seem to matter at all. And then I think this is—I think this actually throws people. Because you start to think these are all fake. And they're not all fake. Like actual—actual bad things happen. Right? Actual bad things happen and it's—it's actually like fully legitimate to get outraged about those things. But also people are going to get outraged about a lot of things that actually didn't happen. And so so so for the purpose of the discussion of the media—again this is McLuhan thing, "if it's on the internet it's a viral social media outrage cycle." Like whatever are the facts of the thing, it doesn't really matter. In fact it's probably best—your point—it's probably best if it's actually hard to tell what actually happened. Because that makes it something that you can more easily argue about. And so that's one thing. And then to the point that you just made, I really agree with that also, which is the degree of importance of the actual issue doesn't matter that much. Right? And so if it—so if something happens to a single person, you can—and then something happens to 10,000 people, you can have the same level of outrage in—in each case. Like the outrage doesn't scale with the number of people affected. Right? In a lot—in a lot of ways maybe the outrage is even higher when it's a specific person because it—because then it becomes personalized. Right. And then you—and then you get in the old—what is it the old Stalin thing which is "a single death of the tragedy a million deaths is a statistic." Like maybe it's even the small-scale horrible things that happen that are the most emotionally intense because they're the things that you really—you're going to—you're going to react to a person—you know kind of—kind of the most intensely. And so so—and I think that's the thing if you're doing the meta observation of this. It's like okay you're just going to—you basically—you just have to expect this same phenomenon, this outrage cycle to play out every time regardless of truth or falsehood and regardless of the actual magnitude of the underlying issue. Because—because again what's being argued about is not the specifics about what actually happened, that's not the point. The point is the ability to form up moral tribes and then yeah and then have the rumble. Yeah, and just to that end too, when people say, "Oh Jussie Smollett," that is fresh in my mind. There's George Orwell actually has a—a great thing on this. Because George Orwell famously covered the Spanish Civil War which was kind of—as I said—is kind of the original where a lot of this stuff sort of played out for the first time in the 20th century. And he—he—he did this thing one time where he talks about his version of this—he talks about the role of the atrocity. And he said basically the—the key point of propaganda in any war—so you get two countries that go to war and you know they've each got a moral claim of like why they're the justified—you know nobody ever attacks everyone's always defending—like and I only attacked him first because he was going to attack me otherwise. And so every—each side in a war, each country, has its own basically moral position why the war is justified. But he said—he said basically Orwell said—like it's hard to sell like high—it's hard to sell like these high-blown—you know these high-flying kind of abstract ideas of like why we need to go to war. And so he said what happens is the propaganda efforts of each government in a war become basically looking for atrocities. And so specific point events. A famous one is the My Lai Massacre in Vietnam but you we could—we could name many many other examples. And so you're looking for these very specific events where something very specific and concrete happened that's clearly over the moral—over beyond the realm of moral acceptability. Just clearly like in—you know usually involving innocent people getting killed. Right. And in the Spanish Civil War it was like, you know, nuns getting killed or something like that, right? And—and the—again it's the point of the atrocity—the point of the atrocity is not that it affected a hundred thousand people. The point of the atrocity—in fact maybe the best atrocity only affects a small number of people because you want to personalize it. And then he said—he says this. He says the truth or falsity of the atrocity doesn't matter at all. Like it—the atrocity—if the atrocity is made up, the political value of the atrocity is just as high. Right? And so it doesn't matter—like once the atrocity cycle gets running it doesn't matter whether the underlying thing is true or false. Because the—because the point of it is to have the—have the atrocity be a propaganda win for moral—for moral justification and then for moral demonization of the other side. And by the way, lest—lest people think that I'm being cynical when I go through all this, I'm not saying I think this is all good. Like I'm not saying I think that this world is great. I'm not saying that this—I think that this is all positive. I'm saying something different, which is I think it's very important to understand how this actually works. Because when you find yourself in the middle of one of these things, I think it's very useful to have at least a slice of your brain that's basically saying, "All right, I need to be aware of the fact that I'm getting pulled into something." Right, that I'm being triggered by emotion and I'm being triggered and I'm being slotted into a moral tribe and I'm mounting up to go to war with the hated enemy and it's on the basis of something that very well might not be true. Right, and I think—and this—you know this is a René Girard thing which he sees—this scapegoating cycle always works because people always forget that there's a scapegoating cycle. Like—like whenever you have to—like crucify the scapegoat, it's always the most morally important thing that's ever happened for the duration of that cycle, right, until it's forgotten by the next one. And so, so a big part of what I'm trying to do is explain why, as we surf all of these current things that we like, we should at least keep part of our brain that basically says, "Look, this is the state of the world we live in, this is the state of humanity, this is the state of the media. Like, this is what's happening. We can like it or hate it or we can debate the pros and cons, but it is what's happening, and all objective events are going to be filtered through that." To the extent that you care about objective truth, you have to get through that and you have to get to the other side and be able to actually still deal with objective reality. But to do that, you need to understand the cycle. And it's also important to understand because if people are ever involved in the cycle or implicated in it, they typically say, "Oh, but I'm innocent. Once everyone finds out the truth, they're going to exonerate me, they're going to clear me." They, of course, don't fully appreciate that people don't give a shit. People are, you know, predisposed to hate you and they'll just find any sort of reason, you know, or certain people perhaps. Yes, yes, yes, if yes, if you are—yes, if you are actually an—if you are actually innocent and if you're caught up in one of these things, it's horrifying, it's terrifying. I mean, look at cancellation. I mean, this happened to a lot of people over the last decade. You know, people would get canceled for things they literally never did and for, you know, for people then imputing beliefs or, you know, some I don't know, stray comment or misreported comment. And then it gets imputed into "you know, this person is a horrible racist" and off and away it goes. And they have to get fired and purged from society and, you know, have no way to come back. Or, you know, or whatever the issue was, right? And, you know, we, you know, there were these moments, you know, these viral video—yeah, oh, that's the other thing is like, okay, the viral videos. The viral videos, right? The viral videos have become so much like the—by the way, the first viral video that mattered was actually Rodney King. And it was, and it actually—and I'm not going to, I don't want to litigate Rodney King, good or bad or whatever it was, the whole separate conversation—but the Rodney King video starts with the beating. So it doesn't show everything that led up to that. And that set the pattern. And again, I'm not, I'm not excusing anybody for anything or whatever, I'm just saying, like, the video started midway through the event. All of the really effective viral videos basically since then, including the ones that have been really important over the course of the last decade, they've all had that characteristic. They all start halfway through the event. So you don't see the run-up. And so you don't—you know, the Central Park bird watcher was the famous, you like—you don't see the run-up of what happened before the video started. What was the other one? Who was the other person who got like brutally canceled based on a video? I can't remember. I mean, there have been a whole bunch of them. But this is the thing, right, and so you, you get a viral video and it kicks off halfway into the event. Why does it, by the way, why does it kick off halfway into the event? That's because that's when the event became interesting enough to be able to put on a video. Like, that's when people don't just like walk around with their phones out; that's when they pull the phone out. But by that point, you've lost all the context, right? And so, and again, this is one of those things where, okay, you're watching a viral video and you can feel yourself getting outraged. The key question ought to be, "All right, what happened before that?" Right? And you can—and that takes work, right? Like, because it's not, it's not right there on the screen, and you have to like really try hard to like go back and like figure out eyewitness reports. And by the way, like, if it's a criminal thing, half the time you don't find out what happened until the actual trial happens, so you don't find out for like another year. Because they don't put the evidence out until the trial. And so that—that's another thing where people get basically—people get used basically. People get ginned up into these outrage cycles without actually having the context for what happened. The—I want to go back to this conspiracy. You know, we have a friend who thinks everything is an op, and maybe I'm naive and I miss out on the ops that are actually there. But I'm curious what mental model one might have to think about how much is coordinated vs not. I've been inspired by the idea of decentralized or emergent collusion, just sort of this idea that a lot of people have incentives to go in the same way. Even if they're not coordinating explicitly, they almost might as well be, or something like that, or it might explain why so many things seem coordinated. But, you know, ever since joining a16z, so many people have impugned onto us things that we're not doing, so it's made me skeptical of people calling for conspiracy. And so I'm curious what sort of mental model you might have for the idea of ops and how that might help one understand the social platforms today? Yeah, so I think as best I can tell—and it's tricky because it's hard to tell, and there are, you know, some real experts in this that really, really focus on this—but as far as I can tell, like, there's some, there's some truth on both sides. The answer is messy, and it's messy for two reasons. So messy number one is just like there are a lot of things that I think are happening that are truly organic and then there are a lot of things that are happening that are ops. And I think, I think there's a combination. And then I think—maybe you're alluding to this—and then there's this thing which is like something can start as an op and then become real, right? Which is kind of the point of the op, right? If you—let's put it this way: if you and I are going to be Machiavellian and we wanted to start a movement, right, and we wanted to start a moral outrage cycle because we had some underlying goal, because we were either trying to win an election or trying to get a political something, you know, a law passed or something like that. We were going to like—we're maximum like Machiavellian deviousness and we're going to like have a black budget, you know, and we're going to pay influencers to lie and all this stuff that people say, you know, bot—we're going to have our bot army and so forth. Like, the point of it is not to just like have a bunch of propaganda online with a bot army behind it. The point is to get people to actually believe it, right? That is the goal, right? And so, and then, you know, you don't want to have to pay for like every post, right? At some point you want them to be happening organically even just out of a self-economic interest, and so you actually want—you're actually trying to generate the thing. Actually, there's a great paper on this. There's Timur Kuran, who's the famous sort of guy who talks about preference falsification, and then Cass Sunstein wrote this thing years ago where they talk about this phenomenon relevant here; they call it availability cascades. So their term for this kind of thing that we've been talking about, which is like a viral outrage cycle, their term for it they call availability cascade. And what they mean by availability cascade is sort of—cascade means basically the process. It's the sociological term for sort of an idea or concept kind of cascading through a society, kind of building up momentum as it goes. And then availability they call is based on the idea of availability bias, which basically is we tend to over-focus on the thing that's right in front of us. And so we tend to over-focus on the thing that's basically present in our mind at that time, which may or may not be as important as other things that we're not focused on. But, but in the paper, the significance of it is, in the paper, they identify the role of a specific kind of person they call availability entrepreneur. And the role of the availability entrepreneur is to try to inject into the public consciousness availability bias as a deliberate exercise to basically say, "This is the specific thing that you should be focused on right now." Like, this is the specific person who's been hurt by something, this is the specific whatever, you know, moral event that's happened, this is the specific issue, this is the specific thing. And basically what they say is like, every time you see an availability cascade, if you trace it back, there was always what they call the availability entrepreneur, which was basically the person who was like, "Aha, I am going to put..." Oh, I'll give you the classic example. And again, this is not a value judgment and, you know, this was like, you know, fantastically good that this happened, but Rosa Parks was the classic example of this. Like, the story that we all got told in school was Rosa Parks was just like another random, you know, sort of a random person, you know, with the thing on the bus. Rosa Parks was like an actual—she was a trained activist. Like, they—she went to—like, there was this whole like school of activists and they—like, she was like a specifically trained... it was an availability entrepreneur who set up an availability cascade targeted on Rosa Parks that ended up causing fundamental and obviously very, very positive changes to American society. But like, it started as an op. Like, she started as, she started as an op. But the point of the op was not to be the op; the point of the op was to become what happened, which was to become a nationwide movement that ultimately led to, led to fundamental change. Okay, so anyway, the point is like just because things start as ops doesn't mean they're not real, right? And if—and if the op leads to real outcomes, then you might as well con—you know, it's like a m—at some point it's just a matter for historians of how it started. Like, it's a footnote to the Rosa Parks story today that she was a trained activist. It doesn't really matter because she—because that op unlocked a broad-based, actual movement in American society that led to actually profound change. And so this is the problem with this topic, which is "Oh, that's just an op" is basically like cope. Like, okay, right? It might have started as an op, but if it ends up in a real thing, it doesn't matter, you see what I'm saying? It doesn't matter that it started as an op. And then, by the way, conversely, it also doesn't matter if it didn't start as an op, because again, it—it's the same outcome. And so, so I do think I think both things are true. I do think there's this furious fight. I do think there are serious operators and, you know, you can go online and you can find out about these things. This is a big issue, by the way, in political influencer world right now. This is—this is actually a big thing. So if I want to run an advertising campaign to sell shampoo, legally in the US if I hire influencers to say good things about the shampoo, they—I believe this to be true—they have to disclose that it's a paid commercial. They can't just like pretend that they just happened to stumble on this shampoo. If I donate money to a political candidate, I have to disclose that as part of election—federal election law. If I pay an influencer to take a position on a moral topic that is not a product that is sold or a political candidate, but is just like a position on a moral or political thing, that doesn't qualify either as an advertisement or as a political donation. It's fully legal to do that in the dark. Well especially AI, right? A lot of—a lot of paid doom out there. We were just talking about Aila's, you know, a new media fellowship for doomers and yeah, it's a perfect example and it, yeah, seems to be having a real impact. This is a great example. So this is the thing where, you know, they're—they're living the meme, right? They're living the meme because—so this is a great—so, so I don't know if you, you may have covered all this already, but yeah, so like, they're—we, we know we're in this AI—we're in this pitched AI policy battle. And we, we know through that, and we, we're deep into this, we know that there's dark money in, in AI. Like a lot of the AI doomers and stuff is basically bought and paid for. Like, so there's these, these influence operations that are happening, and they fall into this bucket of—it's, it's neither an advertisement to say AI is going to kill everybody, nor is it a political candidate. And so you—there are organizations and companies that are paying the, you know, there's this... And again, not everybody who says that is—is being paid, but there, there is a lot of dark money in, in that, in that world right now. And so, and of course, you know, the partisans in that world insist full-on, of course, that that is absolutely not happening until yesterday when they just go ahead and like live the meme and they just like announce the program. And so they announced the program in black and white right there on a website, and it's like a influencer camp in which you're going to go dot dot dot and, by the way, it's free, right? Which means, you know, you're—you're being paid. And then they say, "Where's the money coming from?" and they say, "Oh, you know, we're a non-profit being funded by, in this case, MIRI, which is one, one, one organization with its own kind of interesting funding history." And then they say, "And other donors," right. It's like, and it's literally, you know, it's literally like, it's is it—I don't know, is it a conspiracy if it's in plain sight? But and I'm just—I'm picking on that one because that's the one that happened yesterday. But like, that for sure is happening all throughout politics; it's for sure happening on lots and lots of social issues. It's—it's a thing. And so yeah, and so like, but again it goes back to this thing of like, okay yes, there are ops, yes, you can't believe what you see. By the way, the other—there are bot farms. Like, there are obviously bot farms. And there are obviously bot farms that are being deployed. You know, there are—there are online influencers, some of them very controversial, where you know, they clearly—there's clearly a bot army behind them. By the way, you could be an influencer online and have a bot army behind you and have nothing to do with the bot army, right? Like, somebody could be paying for the bot army and you might not even know about it. And again, today, it's totally legal to do that. Because again, if you're not selling a product and you're not donating to a political candidate, like, it's actually in this grey—it's actually fine to do. And so I think it's 100% the case that, like, there's ops all over the place and quite frankly, like, the longer I, I—the deeper I get into it, the more I'm like, yeah, there's probably more ops than I've, than I've, than I've been willing to acknowledge. But I would just say on the other side, it's like, I don't know, does it really matter? Like, is that really the thing that—because the ops are only going to work—be another way to put it is: the ops are only going to work if they, if they resonate into something that people are going to pick up and run with anyway. Like, to become a mass movement, this is what Timur Kuran says about the role of the availability entrepreneur—like, it's not enough to just like have your cause and then just like expect that you can kind of trick people into running with your cause. There has to be something to it that resonates with them, you know, kind of in them already, that cause—that causes them to like—and if maybe they pick it up and run with it based on a paid op and maybe—and by the way, maybe in the counterfactual they would have picked it up and run with it anyway, you know, without the op, and you know, you'll never know. That's a great mental model for it. Thinking about a last topic for today, I want to close on this idea, sort of the bridge between legacy and new media, because we see—just give some examples—obviously the *New York Times*, they've made the adjustment to the new world very well from a business perspective. You know, we had CBS acquire *The Free Press*, and so there's some, you know, acquisitions there, strategic acquisitions for legacy media companies who are trying to innovate. You know, on MTS ourselves we're sort of experimenting with—with players from both worlds. You know, we have new school podcasters and Twitch streamers. You know, Theo—is Theo—are you 21 years old? Yeah. 21, you know, he's born to be a, a Twitch streamer. But we also have—I mean, 21, I mean, over the hill. Exactly. Grey hair, grey hair, washed. Yeah, washed. But we also have Mark Halperin, who you know is, you know, legacy and legendary, you know, commentator and host, who—who funny enough, you know, we've talked about how Trump is—is the bridge figure in that he was successful both in legacy and in this new m—media climate, and it's rare for people to do both. Halperin, of course, interviewed him, did a very famous interview with him when he was talking about the Bible verses and, you know, getting him to mention some specific ones and you know, Trump, of course, didn't want to pick favorites. Anyway, so I'm curious how you think about this—sort of the bridge, either analysis of—of—of Trump as the bridge or more just, how do you see legacy players and new media players sort of playing out, or even the styles of what will work in this new, new format? Yeah, I mean, so I guess, I don't know, you maybe start with data, right? So the data shows that basically—focus on American data because it's what I'm most familiar with—so just in the data you basically see a couple things. So one is, trust in centralized institutions of all kinds is collapsing in the US. So basically any kind of legacy or incumbent institution, you know, trust is collapsing fast. By the way, that collapse in trust didn't start with the internet; it's actually started around 1970. It actually—it's part of this—it's part of this "WTF happened in 1971" meme is when basically trust in centralized institutions in the US started to fall off a cliff. That's true for many kinds of centralized institutions and it's been true for, you know, basically 55 years. But media's included in that. And so trust in centralized media is collapsing and, you know, keeps hitting new record lows basically every time they run the survey; it collapses further. And so, so that's certainly true. And then you, and then if—the business version of it is just very clear, which is—you know, we were talking about CNN earlier—ratings for all the centralized media properties are in full-scale collapse. You know, cable news is collapsing, you know almost every—almost every newspaper has collapsed, almost every magazine has collapsed, you know basically all the—all the legacy formats have collapsed, you know with only very rare exceptions. And so, you know, just that—and then by the way, correspondingly, it's just sort of obvious, you know, the rise of podcasts obviously is—is obvious now. And by the way, it's amazing; like, the election of '24 was the one where podcasts kind of flipped the—you know, went from being like, "I don't know if this matters" to like all of a sudden everybody's like, "oh wow, this matters like a lot." And so the rise of podcasts, of course, is undeniable at this point; the rise of social media, the rise of—of livestreaming, you know, these things are clearly happening. And so like, the—the changing of the guard, reordering of the businesses and of the cultural and social phenomena are clearly happening and, I think, are undeniable. You know, if you extrapolate forward, like obviously all, you know, essentially all of the remaining old media properties that don't figure out a way to adapt to the new world are going to, you know, they're—you know, going to get basically run by private equity and that'll be that. You know, which is kind of the story of the newspaper industry these days. So I, you know, that—like, it's almost like not even interesting to say that anymore just because it's like, it's so obvious that that's happening. And then obviously social media, you know, continues to grow as a phenomenon, keeps being very important. And so yeah, and you know, look, we're still—we're still living in this—we're still living in a hybrid world today, right? And so we're still living in a world in which, like, you know, to some extent it still matters what's on TV, but like, not nearly as much as it did. But, you know, pretty soon it's not—it's not going to anymore. So you know, so that world is changing fast. Yeah, I, I just think, yeah, the main thing is going to be, you know, the—the people who adapt to that are going to—they're either going to adapt or they're not. You know, you mentioned Mark Halperin, you know, who's a good friend of both of ours, you know, is—I think is doing like an amazing job adapting to that. And in a way that's just like, I would say, shockingly different and better than, you know, most of his former, former colleagues. You know, there's great examples. Then you get the *New York Times* has adapted to that in an interesting way. You know, the Hasan Piker apology newspaper has fully committed itself to—to the future, let's say. So you know, they're doing their thing. Yeah, you know, most of legacy properties won't adapt; they, you know, they never do. There's going to be lots and lots of new things that emerge. And maybe I should say here again to not sound—not sound cynical about this, like, it is really striking, I would say the following. So like, what are the two like mega, mega, mega trends in video and by the way, also in politics over the course of the last like five years? It's basically two things. It's short-form video in the form of TikTok, Instagram, and—and—and X, you know, short-form reels or short-form video. And in there you get like the full battery of accusations of like, you know, destroying attention span and everything's trivial. But on the other side is the rise of the three-hour podcast, right? And so on the podcast side you have this incredible phenomenon with three hours. And by the way, some of these, you know, Joe and—and Lex and others are now pushing this to the point where, you know, Lex now has like, in some cases, seven or eight hour podcasts, right? I think was it Balaji did like a ten-hour one on Lex. Yeah, so Balaji went for ten hours on Lex. And so and it's actually interesting because if you talk to those guys, they get data on completion rates and it's actually a very large percentage of their—of their listeners or viewers who actually listen to the whole thing. So like, many, many, many Rogan podcast or Lex podcast people actually listen to the full three hours. And so it's not—the thing I really reject is this idea that like—and I don't want people to think that I believe this is—like, this idea that everything is going to become just like trivial short-form social media bullshit like that. No, not at all. You have this corresponding thing which is you—it's like a barbell. You also have this—you have this massive rise in substance. And on the substance side I would point to the long-form podcasts, I would point to Substack. By the way, you know, there's a big push on X to have long-form essays. I would push, you know, online, online, online courses on every topic are available, and you just, yeah, you have this ability. Even AI—one of my favorite features of AI is what they call deep research, which is a feature on both—I think it's on particularly on—on ChatGPT and on Claude. But like, you set it loose and it will write you like, literally a 30-page, 30-page answer, you know, that's basically like a textbook on any topic. And so, so I think there is this other side which is you—you have this—you also have this growth in—you have this growth in depth. Oh, the other thing is just—and we're an example of this—the rise of the practitioner media vs practitioner-driven media. So people who are actually doing a thing showing up and talking about it, right? And of course, you know, that leads to howls of outrage of "that's not journalism if you don't have somebody asking like whatever super snarky critical questions about it or whatever." But like, the idea for somebody who is actually doing a thing—I mean, you know, Andrej Karpathy in AI, right, is just like shows up and like explains like, "here is how this all works" is just like amazing. And you know, in—in old media, like basically like when I was younger, like Charlie Rose did that. I grew up actually listening to Charlie Rose, watching Charlie Rose. But Charlie Rose literally was on at fucking midnight. Like, he was on CBS at midnight and he would have these hour-long conversations with these super interesting people doing super interesting things. But he was on at midnight and you had to stay up at midnight to watch Charlie Rose, right? And it was one of the great things about getting a DVR is you could record Charlie Rose and watch it during—during normal, normal daylight hours. But you know, we lost that, or we didn't have that for a very long year—we have that now. And so, so I do think it's worth noting like, there is this in my view clearly incredible, positive, informative, educational, super helpful, super constructive thing on the other side of it, which is of course a lot of what we try to do. Yeah. And you know, 2024 was the podcast election; it'll be interesting to see what 2028 will—people have been very concerned, you know, or sort of predicting, you know, deep fakes for a while and how that might influence things. But we haven't really seen anything there super material, it seems, but it'll be interesting to see what media formats or just how the 2028 election is—is different than the 2024 one in this conversation. We've had deep fakes forever. Like, people have believed all kinds of crazy bullshit. I mean, you know, was the—the Gulf of Tonkin incident that kicked off the—really kicked off the Vietnam War—like, I think we now know that that was a fake. Like, that's actually quite well established. And then some people might say Russiagate turned out—we now know it was a quite elaborate fake. So the—the deep fake thing I find very funny because it's like, it's not like we've been swimming in truth all this time and all of a sudden now, now there's going to be deep fakes. But anyway, that—maybe that's a separate conversation. The—I think the—the substantive comment I would make is so, so I actually think we have—people talk about like, famously talk about the 1960 election, Kennedy vs. Nixon, where television—where famously where television took over from radio, where if you—they had their famous debate and if you listened to it on the radio, you thought Nixon won, and if you watched it on TV, you thought that—that Kennedy won. So so you know, the audio vs. audio plus video. And so people have been talking for, you know, that was considered like the first television election where television was like the determining factor in who won or lost, along with, you know, maybe some shenanigans in Chicago. Anyway, speaking of deep fakes. So anyway, people have been talking for a while about when will there be the first internet election. And, and you know, people like to point to 2016 being, you know, Trump using social media, or maybe 2024 being the podcast election. So we've had three examples of internet mattering for elections, for sure. But I think we haven't had the true internet election. And as a consequence of that—and by the way, we therefore haven't really had the true internet candidate yet. And actually it's interesting as people talk today—this is not a pro or negative, pro or con comment on—on Trump himself for the purpose of this discussion, but people talk about Trump as the internet candidate, but he's really—he's really a hybrid in that he cares enormously about what's on TV. And he's on, and he's on TV a lot, and he watches TV all the time famously in order to—because TV is basically how he gets a read on the pulse of—of sort of what's happening and of what people think about things. And so like, I think Trump's kind of underlying theory is the things that are on TV are the things that the people running the TV network know that their viewers want to see, and so therefore if you watch it on TV, you can get a sense of what people actually think. And I, you know, and as TV has been the dominant medium for the last 60 or 70 years, you know, that's a pretty good theory; obviously it's worked pretty well for him. And then he—kicked—and then he also, like, cares a lot about like, affecting what's on TV. And there's this famous Trump story that lots of people have told where he'll be in a meeting and there will be a TV on in the background, he'll be watching it and he'll have it with the sound off, but he'll be watching the chiron—the, you know, the thing at the bottom of the screen, the text that shows what—what they're saying. And if he doesn't like what's on the chiron, he'll tweet something to change the subject, right? And so he'll tweet and then he'll literally watch and the chiron will change and he'll be like, "you know." So, right, he's able to single-handedly with his power of his Twitter to turn one current thing into another current thing, right? Okay. So, so my—but my point is like, he's a hybrid. So he's very social media fluent, but he's also very television. And of course his entire career was made on television. So he's very television fluent. He's one of the best, you know, television programmers in history and he's a social media native. There will be an internet candidate. There will be an internet president who gets elected in, I believe, entirely based on the internet. Where they don't pay any attention at all to what's on TV, because it doesn't matter to them one bit and they're going to be entirely online and they're not going to care what's in the—on TV and they're not going to care what's in the newspaper, and they're going to be 100% a creature of the internet. And I think we haven't—I believe we haven't seen that yet. I don't know when we're going to see it. I'm pretty sure it's possible it's '28; probably my guess is '32. Yeah, and so I think we all have—yes, for better or for worse—we all have that to look forward to. Yeah. The—well, I think that's a great note to—to wrap on. This has been a great discussion, all things media. Marc, thank you for coming on the first day of Monitoring the Situation. I am proud to be part of the monitoring. Excellent. WATCH LIVE: Lutnick testifies on Trump’s Commerce Department budget request before Senate committee

PBS NewsHour(5.2M) ·110:45 ·16.3K views · 2026-04-22
Howard Lutnick (U.S. Secretary of Commerce)
Secretary of Commerce Howard Lutnick defended the Trump administration’s FY2027 budget, touting a "Golden Age" of domestic manufacturing and semiconductor investment while facing sharp bipartisan criticism over proposed deep cuts to economic developm...
TL;DR Secretary of Commerce Howard Lutnick defended the Trump administration’s FY2027 budget, touting a "Golden Age" of domestic manufacturing and semiconductor investment while facing sharp bipartisan criticism over proposed deep cuts to economic development agencies and the...

TL;DR

Secretary of Commerce Howard Lutnick defended the Trump administration’s FY2027 budget, touting a "Golden Age" of domestic manufacturing and semiconductor investment while facing sharp bipartisan criticism over proposed deep cuts to economic development agencies and the management of $21 billion in remaining broadband funds.

Economic Strategy and Trade Policy

  • Reshoring and Manufacturing: Secretary Lutnick highlighted record-setting domestic investments, including a $200 billion commitment from Micron for semiconductors and a $550 billion trade deal with Japan involving nuclear and natural gas projects.
      • Industry Targets: Focus remains on steel, aluminum, copper, pharmaceuticals, and semiconductors through Section 232 actions.
  • Trade Performance: Lutnick claimed the administration has dramatically reduced the trade deficit, reaching a 16-year low in October 2025, with exports increasing to $3.4 trillion.
  • International Relations: Senator Shaheen challenged Lutnick’s rhetoric regarding Canada (notably a comment that their economy "sucks"), arguing it has led to a 30% drop in Canadian tourism to states like New Hampshire.
      • Lutnick’s Response: He defended the stance, citing Canada’s "disrespectful" treatment of U.S. spirits and dairy under USMCA.
  • Tariff Impact: Senator Gillibrand raised concerns that tariffs act as a "ton of bricks" on small businesses relying on imported inputs.
      • Domestic Focus: Lutnick countered that "if you build in America, you don’t have to pay the tariff," framing them as a necessary tool to reverse the "hollowing out" of U.S. industry.

Broadband (BEAD) and Infrastructure

  • Funding Disputes: A central point of tension was the remaining $21 billion in "non-deployment" funds within the Broadband Equity, Access, and Deployment (BEAD) program.
      • Discretionary Concerns: Chairman Moran expressed concern that a "catch-all" provision in the statute might allow the Department to divert these funds away from actual broadband connectivity.
      • Precision Agriculture: Senator Fischer urged the Department to ensure these funds support "precision agriculture" to maximize economic development in rural areas.
  • Status Update: Lutnick reported that 54 states and territories are currently drawing down the initial $21 billion for deployment, with a plan for the remaining funds expected within two months.

National Security and Technology Oversight

  • Export Controls: Senators Coons and Shaheen questioned the licensing of H200 AI chips to China, suggesting they provide the PLA with advanced war-fighting capabilities.
      • Lutnick’s Defense: He asserted that the U.S. is not selling its "best" chips (specifically the Blackwell architecture) and that President Trump is balancing a complex relationship with President Xi.
  • Rare Earth Minerals: Senator Van Hollen questioned a $1.6 billion federal stake in "USA Rare Earth," alleging a lack of transparency and a potential conflict of interest involving Lutnick’s former firm, Cantor Fitzgerald. Lutnick maintained the deal was vital to breaking China’s "chokehold" on critical minerals.
  • Quantum Computing: The budget proposes a 32% cut to quantum research at NIST. Lutnick argued this shift is intentional, as the "private sector is leading the charge," and the government should transition to a coordinating role.

Proposed Agency Cuts and Reorganization

  • Program Eliminations: The budget proposes the total elimination of the Economic Development Administration (EDA) and the Minority Business Development Agency (MBDA).
  • NIST and NOAA: Lutnick defended a 54% cut to NIST and a 27% cut to NOAA, suggesting the agencies have "drifted" from their core missions.
  • Manufacturing Extension Partnership (MEP): Despite bipartisan support for MEP, Lutnick defended proposed cuts by citing Inspector General reports that alleged "excessive compensation" for executives and inaccurate performance statistics.
  • Census 2030: Lutnick proposed using the U.S. Postal Service to conduct the census to save costs on hiring and training 500,000 temporary workers.
      • Criticism: Senator Schatz cited GAO studies suggesting letter carriers are not trained for this role and that the move could delay mail delivery.

Notable Quotes

"President Trump is committed to ushering in a golden age of domestic commerce, bringing jobs back to the United States... Trillions of dollars of capital investment are pouring into America." — Secretary Howard Lutnick
"I’m worried that you... use that last catch-all phrase to do something other than broadband deployment... I would encourage, insist that this money be used [for connectivity]." — Senator Jerry Moran
"Insulting our closest ally and neighbor... how does that help the businesses in my state... who are hurting because of the loss of Canadian business and tourism?" — Senator Jeanne Shaheen
"We are not selling our best chips to China under any circumstances... There is a delicate balance in the relationship with China. President Trump has the best relationship with President Xi and he balances that." — Secretary Howard Lutnick
WA and the International Trade Administration. Congress rejected those proposals then on a bipartisan basis because they would have undermined the very economic competitiveness and innovation this administration claims to champion. So what does the fiscal year 27 budget proposal do? The same thing you proposed last year. EDA is proposed to be eliminated. MBDA eliminated. Maryland: the Manufacturing Extension Program eliminated. NIST cut by 54 percent. NOAA cut by 27 percent. ITA cut by 25 percent, even as the trade enforcement caseload is skyrocketing. These proposed cuts to important economic development programs come as consumer sentiment has fallen to 47.6 percent, the lowest reading in the 74-year history of the University of Michigan survey. This is lower than the worst of the Great Recession, and lower than the worst of the post-COVID inflation crisis. These proposed cuts arrive as inflation just hit 3.3 percent, the highest level in two years, and gas prices are way up. GDP growth in the fourth quarter of last year came in at just a half a percent. And on trade, the illegal tariffs cost the American people 166 billion dollars, estimated to mean 1,700 dollars on average per American family. And they haven't gotten their money back. So, Mr. Secretary, you would think that we would see a budget that supports trade and economic growth, not one that would make things worse, not one that this committee rejected on a bipartisan basis. So I hope, Mr. Chairman, that the committee is once again able to work on a bipartisan basis here and put together a budget that meets the needs of the American people. I do look forward to your testimony, Mr. Secretary, and the questions. Thank you, Mr. Chairman. Senator Van Hollen, thank you. We will in that regard. Secretary Lutnick, you are now recognized for your testimony. Welcome. Thank you, Chairman Moran, Ranking Member Van Hollen, and members of the subcommittee. It is an honor to join you here today. Over the past 15 months, President Trump has proven that the American economy is the strongest and most resilient in the world. When businesses look for places to expand manufacturing, drive innovation, and hire more workers, they decisively are choosing the United States. Micron: 200 billion dollar investment in memory semiconductor manufacturing in Idaho, Upstate New York, and Virginia. TSMC's 165 billion dollar advanced manufacturing investment in America's semiconductor logic sector and Texas Instruments' 60 billion dollar investment for seven US semiconductor fabs across three manufacturing mega sites in Texas and Utah are just a few examples of the Trump administration's commitment to driving semiconductor investment and bringing jobs back to the United States. I could spend our entire time together listing the record-setting commitments company after company and industry after industry have made to build in America. Trillions of dollars of capital investment are pouring into America, led by President Trump and the United States Department of Commerce. I am proud of the leadership role the Department of Commerce has played in providing this support and encouragement these businesses need to revive American manufacturing, and we have only just begun. Over the past few months alone, the Department of Commerce has announced multiple, multiple 15 billion dollar plus record-setting critical energy investments across the country. From Pennsylvania and Georgia to Texas and Tennessee, these will accelerate economic growth, ensure US energy dominance, bolster critical supply chains, and strengthen US national and economic security. In addition to the massive historic trade agreements with Korea and Taiwan, the impressive 550 billion dollar trade deal with Japan has announced six projects in the last six months. This includes up to 40 billion dollars for small modular nuclear reactors in both Tennessee and Alabama, and 33 billion dollars for a 9.2 gigawatt natural gas-fired power plant in Ohio. There is also 17 billion dollars for a natural gas-fired power generation hub in southwest Pennsylvania and 16 billion dollars for another natural gas-fired power generation hub in East Texas. In addition, there is 2 billion dollars for a deepwater crude oil export terminal in the Gulf of Mexico and 600 million dollars for the critical high-pressure, high-temperature synthetic diamond grit facility which we need in Georgia. Just last month, Secretary Wright and I were in southern Ohio to break ground on that 9.2 gigawatt natural gas facility. This project will develop the largest natural gas facility in history. It'll lower electricity costs across the region, create thousands of American jobs, and strengthen national security while helping the US meet the energy needs of the future. Beyond these historic investments, the Department of Commerce continues around the clock to implement President Trump's America First trade policy. Under Section 232, the Commerce Department is taking action on steel, aluminum, copper, pharmaceuticals, autos, and semiconductors. We are investigating other sectors to make sure we protect, reshore, and revitalize manufacturing capacity across our critical industries. These actions have led to multi-billion dollar commitments to reshore steel and aluminum manufacturing and reshore American excellence in critical industries. In the first year of his second term, President Trump has dramatically reduced the trade deficit, lowered imports, and increased exports to over 3.4 trillion dollars, a 6 percent increase from 2024. In October 2025, the trade deficit was the lowest we've had in 16 years. I am tremendously proud of the lead role I have played in negotiating our most important trade deals, including the largest deal ever negotiated with the European Union. BIS works hand-in-hand with the Department of Health and Homeland Services to arrange historic agreements to lower our cost of drugs and our most favored nation deals. I'm happy to report the pharmaceutical industry has committed 400 billion dollars to America. ITA has helped Boeing and Wabtec and led 244 billion dollars of contracts for America as compared to the Biden industries' 17 billion dollars. So, for far too long our nation was mired in the status quo, leaving American consumers and producers alike with unfair, poorly implemented trade agreements, outsourced jobs, and paltry investments. And President Trump is committed to ushering in a golden age of domestic commerce, bringing jobs back to the United States, and that's exactly what he's done in the first 15 months of this administration. I look forward to working with you as the department continues to spur economic growth and secure unprecedented prosperity for the American people. Secretary Lutnick, thank you. Let me begin with where we left off in February. I was expecting something concrete from the Department at that February 10 hearing. You committed to using the remaining 21 billion dollars in BEAD non-deployment funds and to conduct listening sessions with stakeholders to discern the best use. So my impression from that hearing was that those funds will be utilized to make certain that there is connectivity and broadband availability to all Americans, and that you would conduct listening sessions to help determine how the best way to accomplish that goal is. During those listening sessions, staff indicated that guidance would be issued by March the 11th following those stakeholder engagements. However, that guidance has not yet been released. Additionally, Senator Collins and Senator Fischer and I submitted a letter to you on March the 5th regarding BEAD funding and talking about an available round of broadband deployment, and we've yet to receive a response. Could you provide us with an update and perhaps something more than "soon" in your response? Sure. I'm glad to talk about the high-class problems that we have. So the Department of Commerce rebid the BEAD program. We have 54 states and territories done. Forty—I think 46—have literally signed the documents and are drawing money and the rest have the documents; they're just finishing up the documents. So these are states and territories drawing down on the execution of the plan for the first 21 billion dollars. Then on the redeployment money, we have 21 billion dollars remaining. We held listening sessions; over a thousand people attended, with 280 comments. We're sorting through those comments and trying to determine the plan which we're going to go over with the administration and, unfortunately, I am going to answer as it's going to come shortly. But that is the plan. We are working on it. Thank you for not using the word "soon". Thank you for not using the word "soon". Please next time don't tell me the answer is "soon" or "shortly". Okay. Mr. Secretary, so what do you expect? Without—I mean, perhaps you're still going through the details, analyzing those comments which is what we would expect you to do—but what do you think the plan is going to be? Is this—are these dollars going to be spent for purposes of deploying broadband in the United States to customers that would not otherwise be served? It's our objective to make sure that the funds are used state-by-state broadly across our great nation to help Americans with respect to broadband and within the statute to which you've written. So we will—we will adhere to the statute that's been written and the moneys will be deployed across our great nation. Well, Mr. Secretary, I—I worry. I worried in the last hearing and I worry in your answer today that you will follow the laws of the statute. That doesn't sound like something I should be worried about, but there is a catch-all provision in a list of things that are permissible and the very last one would suggest you have significant discretion in this regard. And I—I will state to you and for the record I'm worried that you should—I would hope that you do not use that last catch-all phrase to do something other than broadband deployment. And so when you tell me that you're going to follow the statute, I can't complain about you following the statute. But I would certainly suggest, hope, encourage, and insist that this money be used because I'm absolutely certain that even though we've deployed significant billions of dollars in broadband development support, there are plenty of Kansans and Americans who will be unserved or underserved with a significant challenge in connecting with the rest of the world if this last 21 dollars is diverted someplace else. I am in complete agreement with you that our objective is to cover Americans' broadband needs and we set out to do exactly that working with the states. Your state did an excellent job trying to cover everything. If there are still places that need to be covered, I'm happy to work with your staff to try to come up with the right way to cover them. But we have a high-class problem; we've done a great job in executing this plan and we have the ability to both cover everyone in broadband and do other things that will be exceptional for America. I will take you up on your offer for our—my team, our staff and your staff to sit down and we'll—we'll try to give you the examples. Not only the examples, but the places in which we think broadband could be improved and deployed in our state. Thank you, Mr. Secretary. Senator Van Hollen. Thank you, Mr. Chairman. I'm informed, Mr. Secretary, that last night we did get the fiscal year 26 NOAA spend plan. So that makes you one out of four in terms of responses to requests and letters that were not responded to. Because you did say at the last hearing with respect to the Epstein affair that you had, quote, "nothing to hide." I assume that is still your testimony today, is that right Mr. Secretary? That is right. So why have you not responded to the letter that Senator Merkley and I sent following up on the last hearing asking for documents just to be transparent about the situation and answer the questions that we presented in that letter? The last time we were together, I agreed and answered every question on that topic with this committee and I answered each and every topic. I have voluntarily agreed in less than two weeks to sit and answer questions on this topic fully, whatever questions are asked of me voluntarily, in less than two weeks with your House colleagues. I would ask if there are any questions that you want me to answer, please give them to House colleagues and I am going to answer them all. But I am here today to testify about the budget and I look forward to discussing the president's budget with you today. But I am volunteering within two weeks to answer any and all questions on this topic. Well, Mr. Secretary, I—I'm aware of that. We—we presented you with questions and requests from this committee that have not been responded to. So we're going to have to continue pursue that. We will follow closely what happens in that proceeding. I hope you will provide them with the materials that we hope to secure here as well and again, the reason I'm asking this question at this hearing is because we didn't get a response to the letter. No response at all to that earlier letter. The other letter we haven't gotten a response to relates to the issue as I mentioned of the US decision, the Commerce Department decision to take a—a stake, a US government stake in US Rare Earth at 1.6 billion dollars in federal support. According to reports, the Rare Earth's own CEO publicly said that this deal originated in a November 2025 conversation between yourself and herself with no formal application, no public solicitation, and no competitive process. Is that accurate? I met the executives of USA Rare Earth, introduced to me by a gentleman named Ken Moelis, who is a famous banker who has a firm by his own name. And so he brought the deal to us because it is well known that China has weaponized rare earths and critical minerals against the country. It is very important for the United States to unleash our ability to do rare earths, critical minerals, and magnets. And USA Rare Earth has a mine-to-magnet integrated model and that was interesting. It was presented to me by Ken Moelis and then I introduced them to the chips team to pursue the transaction. Yeah. So, Mr. Secretary, I—I'm not—don't have—no complaint with the decision to take some US ownership stake in exchange for the investment, something that some of us encouraged the Biden administration to do in strategic sectors like this one, and certain others. It's more the complete lack of transparency and process under which this deal took place. Because, you know, before that transaction I understand that this company's total federal contracting history was worth less than 100,000 dollars. It also, as you well know, coincided with the fact that your previous firm, Cantor Fitzgerald, helped arrange the transaction on the other side of the deal, on the private investment side, which raises considerable conflict of interest concerns. So can you—can you respond to the letter? Would you respond to the letter by the end of this month that we sent that lays out some of these questions? I will take another look at the letter and see if we can work together to resolve your issues. I would—I would appreciate that, Mr. Secretary, because it would—again, in my—I want to look at this year's budget too and the challenge we've got here is when we present you questions—the chairman mentioned some of the things he asked about—and we get no answers. That's a real problem and it will—it means that we are going to spend more time focusing on the things that you didn't respond to when we want to focus on the future. Thank you. Thank you, Senator. Senator Capito. Thank you, Mr. Chairman, and thank you, Mr. Secretary, for being here. I won't ask another BEAD question, but I will—I think every time you've come I've asked you that question. But I do want to say that I heartily agree with what the chairman is saying. I'm going to be talking with my broadband council folks tomorrow and I wish I could give them something more specific on the non-deployment funds. So as soon as that comes—and I hope it will adhere to—there are going to be places that are still left uncovered. There's going to be infrastructure that still needs to be built and to support the new structures of the future to keep—to keep everybody connected into the future. So I hope those non-deployment funds can stay within our respective states, so I'm anxious to hear how you—how you do that. So I appreciate that. I wanted to ask about NOAA. You mentioned in your—or in your written statement, you said 135 million increase above enacted level for NOAA's shipbuilding and unmanned systems. So we know they have an aging fleet, they have rising repair needs, and there are also new and low-cost rapidly deployable technologies—unmanned systems—that are currently available. How do you—we asked you explicitly in the 2026 bill, directed NOAA to integrate these autonomous platforms? So I'm glad to see you're accelerating that. What kind of execution plan do you have here and what capabilities are you prioritizing? I mean, we are very excited about the prospects that these vehicles and devices bring for forecasting and for national security. Unmanned vessels and drones can provide real-time data from the middle of hurricanes where it's too dangerous to have a person in there. So we are—we are incredibly excited because these vehicles can live inside the hurricane and sort of stay there with much less risk and give us much better data. And we are really excited about the way technology is going to improve our safety of the American people going forward. I'm going to make sure that that is what—when I leave this office at the end of my term, I'm going to make sure the American people are much, much safer and they're using technology the best they possibly can. Good, good. I think that we'll have great results and I appreciate that. The Manufacturing Extension Partnership program, which is used by small and medium-sized businesses and manufacturers certainly in our state, it helps us with technical specialists and allows our—our businesses to compete globally. The budget again has suggested severe cuts to the program. I believe it did last year, but we restored those. What can you tell me about the department and about the Manufacturing Extension Program? So the awards are given out quarterly and they continue basically just the same, right. So every quarter they come up and we're distributing the money as—as you've appropriated correctly. The issue with the program is the Inspector General's reports were very, very critical of the way these programs had been run in the past, right. They said that there was, you know, wasn't accurate what they were saying they were using the money for and its effect. They were overpaying, you know, these—these hubs were overpaying the executives and there were all sorts of problems. So we want to clean those problems up. Remember, this program was set up in the late 80s and really high-tech advanced manufacturing is much better now. I think we need to reimagine it and I'd really like to sit together on talking about how to reimagine it. I think that would be very effective. Have you begun to do that now or is that just something into the future? Well, if—if you appropriate funds, we will do exactly that. So yes, of course we have thought about it because you did appropriate funds last year and we—we need to do it better. If you appropriate funds to us, I'm... because it's advanced manufacturing and America's got 18, as you've heard President Trump say, 18 trillion dollars of advanced manufacturing coming. But if you appropriate funds, we will do it effectively and efficiently and really think about it. I'd love to work with your—with your team to re-examine the way to do that. That would be great. And I will say congratulations on the announcements of a lot of the energy development. I just notice one glaring problem there: you're in Ohio and Pennsylvania and Alabama. There's at least one state, two states, three states missing from that list. So we look forward to seeing you spread that around. And as you probably know, I've been very involved in my other responsibilities over on the Environment and Public Works Committee working with my Democrat counterparts there to try to come up together for a permitting reform bipartisan bill. I think many of the projects that you're working on and can expand to would really be—would benefit greatly from us coming together on that. Thank you very much. Senator Capito and others in the committee, my suggestion based upon the Secretary's response to your question about MEP is that we invite the Inspector General to come visit with us and learn about any challenges that the Inspector General finds in the program. And if—if those as they exist we would fix them, because I think there's broad support for getting MEP... if there's something wrong in the organization of the program, we want to fix it. So we'll get the information necessary for members of this subcommittee and the committee to have an understanding of any challenges the program faces. Senator Shaheen, it is. Senator Shaheen. Yes, thank you, and great idea, Mr. Chairman. Thank you, Mr. Secretary, for being here today. Last year according to the World Travel and Tourism Council, the United States saw a 5.5 percent decline in international visitors. We were the only major economy to see any decline. In my home state of New Hampshire, tourism is the second largest industry both by revenue and it supports 70,000 jobs. Canada has long been our largest share of foreign visitors. But last year we saw a 30 percent drop in Canadian tourism largely due to the loss of trust and goodwill because of this administration's rhetoric around Canada. Secretary Lutnick, last week you said about Canada's economic strategy, and I quote, "they suck." I believe your spokesperson then said this was about Canada's economy leaching off of the US. How does insulting our closest ally and neighbor help the businesses in my state of New Hampshire and states all across this country who are hurting because of the loss of Canadian business and tourism? Canada's economy leans on the 30—the incredible 30 trillion dollar economy of America. There's no doubt about that, Mr. Secretary. But insulting our closest ally and neighbor who provides a lot of business... we have a lot of businesses in New Hampshire—small businesses who work on both sides of the border. We have all those Canadian visitors who are not coming because of your comments and comments by the administration. How does that help our economy? It is outrageous that Canada will not put US spirits on the shelf. It is insulting and disrespectful to America that they won't even put... And my husband has an interest in a company that has American spirits and they won't do it because of the insults from this president and comments like yours. So I'm—I want to go on to another question. But can I have just one small comment which is dairy is treated so badly and your state cares about dairy and we are trying desperately to get them to live to the deal that they have on USMCA and stop treating our dairy farmers so poorly, and your state cares about that and we are fighting for that. We do. But we are not going to get agreement when we keep insulting people. When we have allies and partners, we should try and work with them, not insult them. And I find your rhetoric insulting to the people in my state who are working so hard to try to ensure that they can do business. I want to move on to BEAD and the non-deployment funds because, Secretary Lutnick, as I said the last time you were here, Senator Collins and I led the negotiations around the bipartisan infrastructure law and those broadband provisions. And we—when we enacted BEAD into law, as Chairman Moran said, we understood that any remaining funds after broadband deployment would be used for adoption. New Hampshire was promised nearly 200 million dollars in BEAD funding, yet only 18.6 million has been awarded for deployment. That means New Hampshire has nearly 180 million dollars left for non-deployment activities. So when can we expect guidance from NTIA on how states can spend the estimated 21 billion dollars in remaining non-deployment BEAD funds? We finished our listening tour; we took the comments, over 280 comments and thousand people who spoke to us. We are narrowing the gap; we will make sure that we are covering all broadband access and we will have a plan that we would be happy to discuss with you, as I said, within the coming months. And do you have a deadline for when you're hoping to get that plan to us other than "soon"? My—my objective was to start by getting the money out and getting America the benefit of the bargain, which we've done, right? Fifty-four states and territories executed and out the door finally. Right, this is getting the money out the door. But I would expect over the next two months we will have our plan in place and we will be happy to discuss it with you. Okay, I only have a few seconds left but in your confirmation hearing last year, you said that the United States needed to stop China from—and I quote—"using our tools to compete with us." And you specifically called out US companies for advancing Beijing's AI ambitions. Since then, the administration has allowed powerful chips to be exported to China. Can you assure us today that the administration is going to stop those chip sales to China that would advance its military modernization? We heard yesterday at the Armed Services Committee from Admiral Paparo, who expressed concern about China being able to access those advanced chips. There is a delicate balance in the relationship with China. President Trump has the best relationship with President Xi and he balances that. I want to be crystal clear: we are not selling our best chips to China under any circumstances. We are not even selling their best scale of chips—meaning they're called Blackwell—not even that brand. This is multi-years ago and there's that balance and I understand that balance, but the president understands it the best and he is trying to balance in between there. I would tell you they have not bought any as of today. Well, I'm out of time, Mr. Secretary, but I would urge you to look at the intelligence on the chips because that is not what the intelligence says about what we are selling to China. Thank you, Mr. Chairman. Senator Shaheen, thank you. We're pleased by the presence of the full committee chairman. I recognize Senator Collins. Thank you, Mr. Chairman. Welcome, Secretary Lutnick, it's good to see you. It probably won't surprise you that my first two questions for you have to do with the lobster industry. And I want to thank you for joining with the Department of Agriculture in putting a spotlight on American produced seafood and recognizing the need for more help in that area. As you're well aware, back in fiscal year 22, Congress included language in the appropriations legislation that imposed a moratorium through 2028 on new NOAA regulations affecting the American lobster fishery to provide more time for additional research and data collection on the North Atlantic right whale and also to prevent essentially what would have occurred—the closure of the lobster fishery. With the moratorium on right whale regulations set to expire in 2028, NOAA must soon begin the rulemaking process. I'm asking today for your commitment to ensure that the department will work with the lobster sector—they are the best stewards imaginable—and also with me and the rest of the Maine and New Hampshire delegation to ensure that future regulatory actions will fully incorporate the most current population data for right whales, real-time monitoring technologies, and region-specific risk assessments rather than relying on outdated assumptions that may no longer reflect the actual right whale distribution or fishing patterns. I hope to be considered the greatest friend of the lobstermen, who are a great asset and, you know, I think they are a great asset to our country. I had the pleasure when I was last over in the United Kingdom of going out and having them serve us Maine lobster in the United Kingdom because it was important, because we opened that market; it had never been done before. So this was opening that market for our great lobster fishermen. We promise you that we will use the best data and the most precise data to make sure that our decisions are right for America, and they need to be right for the lobstermen of Maine. That is clearly—we should not be using outdated data; we should use the—the best data and to make sure that we are protecting and doing the right thing for our great lobstermen. Thank you for that commitment and for what you've already done. I want to mention a specific technology that the department has been pushing; it's called ropeless gear.
