This credit provides a general business tax credit to employers to meaningfully offset the cost of providing paid family and medical leave to their employees. I was proud to champion this back in 2017 as the first federal paid family and medical leave policy in this country, in the United States. And I also was particularly proud that it happened under Republican leadership. I was also proud that with the Working Families Tax Cuts, I was able to enhance it and make it permanent now. This is a big deal and I think we should tout it. My team's been working with yours and we are eager for the updated IRS guidance to be released. That'll be critical to ensuring that employers can accurately claim tax credits and deductions, and employers are eagerly awaiting the publication of this guidance. Mr. Secretary, do you have an estimate on when this guidance will be published and can you work to ensure it is prioritized to expand access to paid family and medical leave for American workers? Senator, thank you for your leadership on this fantastic program; it's an important part of the Working Families Tax Cut. So issuing the guidance to implement section 45S, the paid family medical leave credit, is a Treasury and administrative priority. As you mentioned, our Office of Tax Policy has worked very well with your staff on outstanding section 45S issues. We've also met with the various stakeholders and we are working diligently and hope to issue guidance early this summer. Thank you. I know our teams have been in touch with each other and I'd like to have a meeting, if we could, as soon as possible on that. Do you think our teams can work to get something set up on that? With pleasure. Thank you, sir. I recognize the department's budget request decreases funding for the Community Development Financial Institutions, the CDFI fund that's there. I want to take a moment to point out the good work that the 11 CDFIs in my state do in investing in those rural communities in the vastness of the state of Nebraska. The institutions take on the risk to serve those who are otherwise often overlooked or cannot be served by bigger financial institutions. They're sustaining daycares in small communities of just over 300 people; they're keeping retail stores in rural downtowns; initiating new businesses on the Winnebago and the Omaha reservations in northeast Nebraska, and they are supporting efforts to increase rural housing development, which is so needed. Last year Treasury certified CDFI's programs were statutory, and earlier this month announced plans for new program rules to ensure compliance with federal law. Can you talk about your plan for CDFI moving forward? Sure. So again, we want to make sure that it is doing what it is designed to do and not loaded with a partisan wishlist. We also want to make sure that the CDFI program is free of waste, fraud, and abuse. There's a CDFI Tricolor which has been a very—turned out it was fraud auto lending to minority communities. So we want to avoid that. And I think what will be very engaging for the people in Nebraska will be the new 100 million that has been allocated for rural areas. I appreciate your focus on that because many times these rural communities are left out. I appreciate that focus and your acknowledgment of that, sir. Thank you. Senator, I'm from a rural community, so I appreciate that. I know that. You have a good understanding of what's involved. As you know, my colleagues on the other side of the aisle provided the IRS with $80 billion in one-time funding as part of the Inflation Reduction Act. However, since then Congress has agreed on a bipartisan basis to rescind over half of those funds, most recently in January with the enactment of the FY 26 appropriations bills. Your budget request notes that the IRS expects to exhaust the IRA funding in FY 28. Can you talk about the department's plans for transitioning back to a place where the IRS is budgeting and relying on the regular appropriations process rather than through this one-time, really massive infusion of funds? Well, it was really a scoring gimmick for the misnamed IRA—Inflation Acceleration Act—and we are bringing it down. A large part of it we have found was in the data processing system, which is $50 billion over budget—the upgrade is $50 billion over budget. So using common sense, lowering the number of outside contractors, and refocusing the efforts, we now have the data processing under control. About 2 billion of the reduction is in that area. We think it is going to be more efficient. We are meeting the taxpayers where they are, and the online inquiries are up 60% this year. Thank you for your work on that. Thank you, Mr. Chairman. Senator Van Hollen. Thank you, Mr. Chairman. Mr. Secretary, I wanted to ask you a little bit about your recent conversations with folks in the government of the UAE. As I'm sure you probably know, President Trump and his family have done a very brisk business with the UAE over the last few years. There was the $500 million that Sheikh Tahnoun, the UAE's national security advisor and brother to the president, invested in World Liberty Financial, which is the Trump family crypto venture, right before the president's inauguration. There was the $2 billion in World Liberty stablecoin that Sheikh Tahnoun used to invest in Binance—a deal that effectively handed World Liberty $2 billion in cash at the same time that the United States government relaxed our export controls on high-end AI and chips to UAE companies. Now I understand that the UAE is looking for a swap line. The war in Iran has already cost us dearly. In my view, it's been a huge mistake, made us less safe and a lot worse off. In addition to lives lost, we're talking about over a billion dollars a day in taxpayer money. We're talking about higher gas prices, higher prices overall. And now we understand that the UAE is asking you to provide them a swap line through the Exchange Stabilization Fund. Mr. Secretary, can you talk about this request and whether or not you expect to support it? Senator, I would dispute much of what you earlier said in any linkages to this swap line. Many of our Gulf allies have requested swap lines. You would have just read about the UAE. Swap lines, whether they are from the Federal Reserve or the Treasury, are to maintain order in the dollar funding markets and to prevent the sale of U.S. assets in a disorderly way. So the swap line would both benefit the UAE and the U.S., and as I said, numerous other countries, including some of our Asian allies, have also requested them. Mr. Secretary, you said you disputed some of the facts that I presented. Do you dispute the fact that Sheikh Tahnoun through his company invested $500 million in World Liberty Financial just before the president was inaugurated? I am unaware of that. You're not aware of that? I am not aware of that. Okay. I dispute your linkages. Are you aware of the other transaction I mentioned that essentially a $2 billion—seriously? I am not. I mean, it's been so widely reported, Mr. Secretary, and been the subject of lots of questions. In fact, I believe I've asked you about this in the past. Let me go back to the issue of how funds are being used at the IRS. Part of the purpose for providing the IRS with some additional funds was specifically to go after very high-end individuals—taxpayers who are not paying their taxes, not paying what is due for the good of the country. As a result of that, according to CBO, we're actually going to see deficits go up. So deadbeat rich people will get away with not paying their taxes, and deficits go up. But the Direct File was intended to give working people a little bit of relief, and it did save on average 160 bucks and got very good reviews in terms of people's experience with it. It does go to the larger question of the IRS, which has lost 27,000 employees through actions such as RIFs, the deferred resignation program, and early retirement. 27,000 employees left because of those actions, right? Yes, sir, which there was quite a bit of bloat during the Biden years. We are just getting back to a more normalized level. So Mr. Secretary, you had to hire a lot of those people back, right? We hired some people back in specific areas, not a lot of those, sir. But how much did it cost to both first let people go and then go back and have to rehire a bunch of them? Again, it was not a bunch of them, and we have made very large gains in technology. Are you aware of the fact that the Treasury Inspector General for Tax Administration released a report in January showing how the IRS service delivery has declined because of these changes? Have you seen that? What I have seen, Senator, is that the Democrats said that the filing system was going to be a disaster. It's been a homerun. And what you are nitpicking me on now is the difference between answering a call in six minutes and nine minutes. Sir, let me tell you the way the calls used to work. The IRS under the Biden administration would answer the call in six minutes and a taxpayer would be on hold for 20 or 30 minutes. Now when the taxpayer's call gets answered, they get serviced right away. So it's a false equivalence. Mr. Secretary, what you've done is provide a windfall to very wealthy people who don't pay their taxes. Senator, why would I do that? Why would I do that? What is your theory of the case? Because this administration has time after time tilted the tax code in favor of very wealthy... Thank you, Mr. Secretary. I'm a big proponent of using technology to make our lives more efficient, improve customer service, get better outcomes, more compliance—all of those things—and certainly the IRS is a place that can happen. I can tell you when I was Secretary of State of Ohio, we did business filings and ran elections. And we took a paper process and turned it into a digital process and improved customer service. What used to take four days for forming a new business in Ohio then took four hours. Over the course of six years, with attrition and early retirements, we cut the size of the staff in half, reduced fees by 21%, and no longer needed any tax dollars to finance our agency. So I know firsthand how you can use technology to reduce headcount and improve outcomes. Government needs to do more of this. So thank you for your willingness to lead and put technology in place to do this. I know that in looking at your budget request at the IRS, that it is reduced, but you have seen the evidence as you said in your testimony—that revenue is up 12% even though the amount of expenditure was down. Okay, so I know from my experience and from your experience, we're seeing how technology can improve outcomes and improve customer service, compliance, and friendliness to the consumer. Can you give us a little more insight on how analytics and AI and new emerging technologies can do this at the IRS across and maybe even suggest other areas in the government that we could do more work like this? Well, we only have five minutes in terms of the other areas of the government. And Senator, I want to congratulate you because much of what you did in Ohio—I think Ohio too is a sleeper state in terms of the manufacturing that is growing there. What we are doing at the IRS is turning it into a digital-first agency. 98% of the returns will be filed online now, up from 97%. And the efficiency there is vast. Paper processing costs have gone from 45 million to 20 million. We're phasing in more electronic payments for faster and more secure electronic payments. As I told the senator from Maryland, the other thing that we are doing is that we, actually using electronic filing, can go back to a taxpayer and say that you will likely be audited if you submit this tax return; would you like to redo it in advance? And it comes back in a much more satisfactory status and prevents an audit, increases revenues. And as you brought up, our enforcement actions are up 12% this year, over $40 billion. And the top five enforcements resulted in more than $2 billion. Nobody wants somebody to cheat on taxes. Everybody wants to have a better experience with the IRS. These technologies seemingly are accomplishing that. Do you think that we're just at the beginning of this? How much more efficiency can we get? I think that $50 billion has gone down the drain in terms of overspending in technology at the IRS. The upgrade program has been going on since the 90s. I think we finally have a handle on it. I think that between new technologies, between the upgrades and getting the 12 IRS systems that were not able to talk to each other, it will result in better collections, better privacy—because this is how things leak out is old data systems—and a much better taxpayer experience. Finally, thank you for that. We have a 100% tax credit of donations up to $1700 under the Working Family Tax Cut Act for vouchers, school scholarships, to scholarship-granting organizations. Can you tell me how many people we expect to use this and how are the scholarship-granting organizations chosen? They apply and are certified by the IRS. We do not have the total data on that yet, but I will get back to your office on that. But I think this is a very important new program. It would be important to really want to understand how the scholarship-granting organizations are chosen and what the rules are around that. I want to understand that. So thank you and thank you for your service. We've worked with many states like yours to coordinate the state effort with the federal effort. I want to thank you, Mr. Secretary, for appearing before the subcommittee. We appreciate your time and your attention. Members will have one week to submit their questions for the record. Mr. Secretary, we'd appreciate it if the department can answer these questions as promptly as possible. This subcommittee meeting is adjourned. Thank you.

A Conversation with David Sacks moderated by Morgan Hitizg

Council on Foreign Relations(331.0K) ·29:07 ·496 views · 2026-04-22
David Sacks (Entrepreneur and Investor) Morgan Hitzig (Moderator, Council on Foreign Relations)
The Trump administration has adopted a "moonshot" approach to AI, treating it as a global race that the U.S.
TL;DR The Trump administration has adopted a "moonshot" approach to AI, treating it as a global race that the U.S. must win through a three-pillar strategy of pro-innovation private sector support, massive infrastructure/energy expansion, and aggressive technology exports. David...

TL;DR

The Trump administration has adopted a "moonshot" approach to AI, treating it as a global race that the U.S. must win through a three-pillar strategy of pro-innovation private sector support, massive infrastructure/energy expansion, and aggressive technology exports.

National AI Strategy: The "Moonshot" Framework

David Sacks outlines the administration’s focus on maintaining American dominance through three core policy pillars:

  • Pro-Innovation: Ensuring the private sector leads development rather than government-driven initiatives.
  • Pro-Infrastructure: prioritizing the construction of data centers and the energy production required to power them.
  • Pro-Export: Promoting the global adoption of American models and chips to ensure market share.
      • Strategic Goal: Sacks emphasizes that "market share is the test." Success is defined by the world running on American models/chips rather than Chinese alternatives like DeepSeek or Huawei.
  • The "One Rulebook" Initiative: The administration is pushing for a single national AI policy to preempt a "patchwork" of 1,200+ conflicting state-level bills that burden entrepreneurs with compliance costs.

Energy and the Infrastructure Bottleneck

Energy availability and data center permitting are currently the primary bottlenecks for AI advancement.

  • Ratepayer Protection Pledge: To combat "NIMBY" backlash and rising residential electricity costs, the administration reached an agreement with "hyperscalers" (major tech firms).
      • "Bring Your Own Power": Companies pledge that new data centers will not raise residential rates; in exchange, the government eases permitting for firms to stand up their own power generation.
  • Grid Modernization: Large AI companies are expected to invest in grid upgrades, creating an "economic incentive" for infrastructure that ultimately benefits all consumers through increased scale.

Debunking the AI Labor "Disruption" Narrative

Sacks argues that fears of mass unemployment are currently unsupported by data and that AI acts as a productivity "unlock" rather than a job killer.

  • Lack of Disruption: Sacks cites a Yale Budget Lab study showing "no discernible disruption" to the labor market in the three years since ChatGPT’s launch.
      • Layoff Data: Less than 5% of layoffs last year were attributable to AI according to the Challenger Gray report.
  • Blue-Collar Boom: AI infrastructure has created a massive demand for electricians, welders, and construction workers, leading to a 25–30% increase in wages for these trades.
  • The "Radiologist" Analogy: Despite advancements in computer vision, the number of radiologists has increased. Sacks notes that as the cost of a "task" (like a scan) drops, total demand for the service—and the humans needed to manage the results—increases.
  • Software Development: Despite coding assistants, demand for software developers is up 10% year-over-year. Sacks believes AI will allow companies to finally address long-standing "product roadmap" backlogs by making engineers 10x-100x more productive.

Strategic Capital and Institutional Shifts

The administration is reshaping how the government interacts with science, technology, and defense.

  • PCAST Evolution: Sacks describes the new President's Council of Advisors on Science and Technology (PCAST) as a group of "doers" and "company builders" rather than just academics, aiming to bring a business-oriented, long-dated perspective to D.C.
  • Defense & "DR. AIM": Sacks highlights a shift toward cost-effective defense tech. He notes the absurdity of using $4 million Patriot missiles to down $4,000 drones.
      • The DR. AIM Framework: Sacks identifies the future of conflict as a combination of Drones, Rockets, AI, and Missiles.
  • Crypto Regulation: Sacks remains optimistic about the CLARITY bill passing the Senate, which would provide permanent regulatory certainty for the crypto industry and end the era of "regulation by enforcement."
"If five years from now you look around the world and the whole world is running on DeepSeek models and Huawei chips, we lost. If everyone’s running American models on American chips, that’s what we want to see. Market share is ultimately the test." — David Sacks
"AI doesn't—this is what I believe—it turbocharges the individual. It allows them to accomplish more of what they want to accomplish. The idea that we’re just going to throw AI over the wall and it’s going to magically figure out how to get rid of all the humans is just not the way this stuff works." — David Sacks
"You can’t replace something with nothing. If you want to replace the patchwork of what the states are doing, you have to offer a national framework." — David Sacks
So David, thank you so much for for being here. I think the place that I wanted to start today was very much kind of on a personal professional level. You've been entrepreneur, you've been investor, you've been mafia member of the PayPal variety. Tequila company owner. That is probably the only thing wrong with Endless Frontier is we don't have Besties Tequila. What brings it all together for you? I just do things that I'm interested in and if I do one thing too long, I probably get a little bit bored and then move on to the next thing. So, you know, that whole PayPal mafia period was really only three or four years, you know, it wasn't a super long time period. So there's been a lot of, I guess I've just done a lot of projects. The, you know, ending up in government was not my plan, it was just something that happened as a result of, you know, helping organize Silicon Valley for Trump in 2024 and then got asked to come help during the transition. It was just a really exciting time and I got offered this job so I ended up doing it. That's kind of where I want to start. I think it would be really helpful for this group, just given your previous role as czar, which you wore very well. Czar in Washington is not always anybody's favorite role. Let's start with where the Trump administration policy is today on AI. Sure, so you know, we spent kind of the first several months kind of figuring out what our policy was going to be and then the President gave a major speech on AI back in July last year. And he basically declared that America's goal was to win the AI race and we sort of defined the policy in kind of a very moonshot kind of way. In the same way that John F. Kennedy declared that we had to win the space race, President Trump declared that we had to win this AI race. So first of all, you know, we recognized that we were in a global competition and the result of this competition would have a huge impact on our economy and our national security and it was one that we had to win. Some people have sort of criticized this saying, "Well, there's not an end to this race, it's something that's going to be ongoing." That may be true, but it may be an infinite game so to speak instead of a finite game, but nonetheless we do think it's a race and it's important that America stays in the lead. And then he went on to define I think the three pillars of our policy in terms of how we win the race. The first one was we have to be pro-innovation, so in America we understand that the innovation comes from the private sector, it's not the government that actually does the innovating. So we want to be supportive of our private sector and our companies. The second was pro-infrastructure. We want to be the leader in building out this new AI infrastructure and that includes data centers and that means energy, so making sure that we're pro-energy. Obviously the President has been a big backer of this going back a decade with, you know, "drill, baby, drill." And then the third was being pro-export, you know, we generally want to share this technology with our friends and partners and we want to have the most market share across the world. And you know, the way that we think about this is that if five years from now you look around the world and the whole world is running on, you know, DeepSeek models and Huawei chips, I mean we lost, right? But if we look around the world in five years and we see that everyone's running American models on American chips, that's what we want to see, right? It's market share is ultimately the test of whether our policy is working, and so we want to kind of anchor to that. And so at any event, that was sort of the policy we defined and I think it's generally been working quite well. Honest assessment, where are we? You mentioned "quite well." Is there one key place that feels the most important to get right when you think about energy, compute, sort of the infrastructure side, the model side? What feels most salient to you? Well, probably the biggest bottleneck right now that everyone's worried about is energy and I would say even more specifically data centers and then the relationship between the two. There's a very strong NIMBY backlash happening with respect to data center construction right now, and in fact even some members of Congress have introduced a ban on more data center construction. I think the President has a different approach, I think it makes a lot more sense, which is he introduced what he called the Ratepayer Protection Pledge where we got all the big hyperscalers and AI companies to pledge that if they built a new data center it would not cause residential electricity prices to increase. But in exchange for that, the quid pro quo is we'd make it easier to do permitting and make it easier for them to basically stand up their own power generation. So the idea is bring your own power and in exchange you can set up data centers. So that's really I think the way to release this pressure valve of making sure that this AI revolution doesn't happen at the expense of residential ratepayers. Without that, I mean politically it's just not going to be feasible at all. But you know, I think the big hyperscalers and AI companies were happy to do that. They are I think happy to stand up their own power generation, it was always kind of part of their plan. It's just that there's been such a rapid data center boom, I think there's, you know, hundreds of sort of wildcat developers all over the country who've been trying to get entitlements and some of them didn't necessarily think through all the implications of connecting to the grid and that kind of thing. So in any event, I think we're on a much better trajectory now that we have this sort of understanding. So are tech companies going to become energy companies? In a sense, yeah. I mean, they may want to partner with companies or people who have those expertise, but yeah, I think fundamentally our AI companies are going to have to bring their own power, figure that out. Ultimately, it makes sense for them to connect to the grid for reliability, for backup, also so they can sell power back to the grid when they're not at peak usage. So actually, and I think that as part of that, the AI companies are going to be making investments in grid upgrades. So ultimately I think this is going to benefit consumers. I mean, what ultimately brings down prices for electricity is scale, and so you want scale users of electricity who are then scale producers of it and create the economic incentive for all those upgrades to the grid. A big part of the backlash that you mentioned is on kind of at the state and local level. One of the things you've said, and I'd love to dig in on a little bit more, is it's really challenging to have 50 different policies across 50 different states. What's the impact today on some of the entrepreneurs who are in the room, we've got a lot of entrepreneurs here, and how do you see that playing out going forward? Yeah, I mean this is one of the biggest problems right now is that we've got 50 different states running in 50 different directions with respect to AI regulations. There's something like 1,200 bills going through state legislatures right now, and a lot of these bills, needless to say, don't make a ton of sense. And it's not just blue states, also red states are doing a lot of kind of knee-jerk things. But even when the regulations are sensible, the just the fact that they deviate from each other and there's different ways of complying in different states I think creates a huge challenge for entrepreneurs and innovators. The big tech companies are going to figure it out, they can pay for large legal departments and all the compliance, but I think this is really going to be difficult for entrepreneurs. So what we're trying to do, the President's called this one rulebook, is have a single framework for or have a national AI policy. So what we did there is there was an attempt last year to try and move jurisdiction to the federal level. There was not enough support in Congress for that, and the message that we received pretty loud and clear is you can't replace something with nothing. If you want to replace the patchwork of what the states are doing, you have to offer a national framework that addresses those issues. And that's what we tried to do, a few weeks ago we rolled out the White House's national AI framework and we address things like child safety and content creators and communities, the data centers, you know, all of the different hot-button issues that the states have been dealing with. I think we have a pretty good proposal, we've gotten some good engagement on the Hill, and now it's kind of up to Congress to decide what they're going to do next. There's the policy side across 50 different states, but then there's also the people side. And so you've got sort of real AI anxiety when it comes to labor participation, you've got people starting to ask, or have been asking, will there be a job for me? Let's talk a little bit about the labor side of this equation. Yeah, I mean I would say that probably after data centers that is the biggest concern that people have is that AI is going to put them all out of work. And frankly, the CEOs of our big AI companies haven't they're not really helping on this score, I mean they're saying a lot of things that are fueling the fear. I mean I think the place to start is to analyze what's actually happened so far, because from the rhetoric that you hear and the way the media presents it, you would think that job loss is already occurring and that the US economy is being roiled right now by AI and the job market is being massively disrupted. And if you actually look at the data, it's simply not true. Yale Budget Lab did a study and they said that there was no discernible disruption, I mean that's like a quote, to the labor market in the first three years after the launch of ChatGPT. Another report I look at is the Challenger Gray report which analyzes layoffs. Less than 5% of the layoffs last year were attributable to AI, it was actually just a very small category. So we're just not seeing that sort of job disruption yet. Is it possible that we see that in the future? That's obviously harder to say, but again it's not something that's arrived yet. Now what we have seen though is a lot of really exciting job creation. You're seeing it on the construction side, a lot of blue-collar jobs have been created. Wall Street Journal had a really good article about this. The construction industry is seeing a ton of demand, there's actually a shortage of, you know, jobs like electricians, welders, the workers who hang drywall or pour concrete, install equipment, build roads, all those kinds of things that are needed for this new infrastructure. There's actually a shortage of those blue-collar jobs and that's caused the trades to see their wages go up 25 or 30%. So on the blue-collar side it's been very good. And then even on the white-collar side, you know, you would think that the category that would be most disrupted would be software developers. And I just saw a recent study which said that the demand for software developers is up 10% over last year, despite the fact that coding is now so much more abundant and easily generated using coding assistants. So I just don't think we know exactly what all the impacts are going to be on the labor market. It's easy to make these broad sweeping statements about how disruptive it's going to be, but again we haven't seen it yet. Just one other job category I'll mention is radiologists, right? How many times have we heard that all the radiologists are going to be put out of business, and if you're a doctor in medical school definitely don't go into radiology, you should study something else. The New York Times had a study on this where they found that over the last several years the number of radiologists has gone up despite the fact that computer vision has gotten very, very good. I mean even before AI, the sort of the previous computer vision technology which was diagnosing X-rays increasingly well, it did not put radiologists out of work. In fact, what we saw is that the demand for scans increased because as the cost has gone down, as it's possible to do more scans, there's more abundance, people want more of them, and patients need someone to explain to them what's going on. They need a doctor to formulate next steps and work out a plan if they need to be treated. That's not something that AI is going to do. So I think there's just a lot of these examples where what people are talking about with AI automation is more of a task, it's not really the whole job, and I think that jobs will adjust as pieces of the work get made more productive. MRIs and radiology, there's also been a huge boom in things like functional health and you know, really trying to get ahead of any ailments before you you might have seen them otherwise. I think the other thing I was thinking about as you were answering that question was the Microsoft research study around trades, I think it's a really critical one. How do you see or how do you think about, you know, we're coming up to a midterm cycle. What happens in the midterms? Well, I don't want to predict that exactly. I mean, you know, I think that with respect to AI what I would say is that if you poll AI, it polls really poorly. I think the popularity of AI is I think it's like slightly worse than Congress and slightly better than Iran. I mean it's sort of in that range. And again, I think that's because of just all the fear that's been kind of pumped into the market, the media, and again, you know, I think our popular entertainment usually portrays AI in a dystopian way. Then again I think some of our leading founders haven't necessarily been incredibly reassuring on this point. So no, I mean AI has a popularity problem. Now the flip side of it is that the salience of the issue is pretty low. I've seen different types of polls or surveys where people rank the issues that matter to them and AI is like number 17. So it's like, you know, if people vote on that issue, unfortunately if they were to vote right now, they would say that AI is very negative. But the flip side of it is it's not that salient. So I think that what's important is that the economic consequences of AI are actually beneficial to the economy so that people feel like that ultimately there's a benefit there. And again just to put some numbers around this, I mean just the CAPEX I think 650 billion this year just in CAPEX from the hyperscalers, that's about a 2% tailwind to GDP just from all the construction that's happening. I don't think that includes necessarily all the energy, that doesn't include what's happening in the data center, you know, what all the tokens actually do, you know, how much productivity they're unleashing in the economy. I think the fact that code is becoming relatively cheap and abundant compared to the way it was before and more available to every kind of company is going to be an incredible unlock for productivity in the economy. The fact that any company now can design its own bespoke software, I mean it's really incredible. I mean even in Silicon Valley which has historically been the most attractive place for software developers to work and it has succeeded in kind of attracting the most talent, the rate limiting factor in every company that I was ever involved in was that we just couldn't hire enough engineers. There just were never enough engineers, the product roadmap was always way behind, we were always having to kind of triage the product roadmap to just do the most essential things because we just couldn't even if the startup was well-funded, we just couldn't find enough developers to do the coding. And now for the first time that could really change. We're actually, you know, every company can actually build its product roadmap and I think that even companies that were having a hard time recruiting software developers now will be able to, and those software developers are going to be 10x or 100x. So I just think that's going to be an incredible productivity unlock for the whole economy. I mean it's just getting started right now, it's hard to even know exactly how big that's going to be, but I think it's going to be very, very big. If there's a barbell on the software engineer side, on the other side is the kind of moneyball talent wars, a thousand core researchers. That's one of those areas that I know you've talked about before. What ends up happening on kind of the race for talent in AI? Well, this is really interesting. I mean you see that these AI companies are really competing for developers even though some of their founders are saying that developers are going to be obsolete. You know, I saw that I think Anthropic's got numerous open job recs for software developers at like half a million dollars a year, which seems like a lot to pay someone whose job is going to be obsolete and you know, not needed anymore in just a few months. But look, I think what we're seeing is that AI doesn't this is what I believe is going to happen is that it turbocharges the individual. It allows them to accomplish more of what they want to accomplish. And the more skilled they are in an area, the more knowledgeable they are, the better the results are going to be. So for example, I don't know, if I went into name your, you know, name your, you know, AI chatbot or agent harness or whatever, and I tried to design a car, I would not get very far because I don't know anything about automotive engineering. I would design slop, you know, and it might even look good or you know, appear to be a credible design but it wouldn't work at all. The brakes wouldn't work, for example. Now if you're an automotive engineer and you use the latest version of software, it can actually make you much more productive and you can accomplish more and that means that product cycles can happen faster. So it's not like you get the same results with just, you know, different users get different results based on how much context they can provide the model, you know, how good they are at prompting it, how good they are at orchestrating a growing number of agents. I mean there's so many differences. So this idea that oh we're just going to throw AI over the wall into a company and then somehow magically it's just going to figure out how to get rid of all the humans, it's just not the way this stuff works, you know. I'm going to shift gears just a little bit. There are a lot of folks in the room who sit on the public sector side, many who are on the investor side, many who are in the entrepreneurship side. One of the things that's been shifting over the last year or so in the Trump administration is how we do public-private partnership. So you've got folks here from the Office of Strategic Capital which has, you know, 205 I believe, someone can keep me honest, billion. DFC I think is also 205, looking at Ben who is coming up next after you. Can you talk a little bit about public-private partnerships today? Obviously we had Intel last year. How is that shifting and are we doing what needs to be done to counter the Chinese system? So this is an area where I don't have a ton of experience, so I don't want to get over my skis here. But my guess, my starting point for thinking about it would be that in the United States we have a very vibrant venture capital ecosystem. I mean I would say that the ability for startups to get funded, the whole "two guys in a garage" thing, and then as they start going up, you know, if they show a hockey stick then everyone floods them with money. VCs sometimes get mocked for that, that there's sort of lemmings or whatever, but the flip side of it is that, you know, if something is really working, they can rapidly, rapidly get funded. So it's a real I think advantage of the United States that we have this vibrant risk capital ecosystem. So I would start by looking at that and asking what that's not doing well enough that we need the government to get involved in. And I definitely think that there are areas. I mean one of the ways that we ended up with, you know, all the rare earths being processed in China is that we just kind of let that industry go to wherever the lowest cost manufacturing was or processing. It may not even have been completely lowest cost in China, they I think strategically targeted it, may have subsidized it, and when a new competitor tried to come on the market they may have dumped, I mean they did all sorts of anti-competitive things. But basically we didn't think too much about whether the sort of the optimal economic result was actually the right result from a national security standpoint. And I mean there was just too much blind faith in globalization, I think. And I think one of the big realizations over the past decade or so is that, you know, we have to that trade and economics is based on a foundation of security and if that foundation shifts, then you know, that trading relationship may not be stable. But I think there is a role for government there to figure out how do we make it how do we make sure that our supply chains are resilient and that we have access to or we're not dependent on, you know, our global competitors for vital necessities. Makes sense. I think one of the things that Endless Frontier is so good at is we talk a lot about history. 1961 was Eisenhower's farewell address sort of warning of the military-industrial complex, but we also had on the flip side this amazing kind of "freedom's forge" moment during World War II. I'm curious if you've got a take on kind of where we are on that public-private partnerships today? Are we more World War II, how do we actually modernize and re-industrialize or have we leaned more toward that consolidation between capital and company and government? Well, I guess I guess the concern about the MIC, it's not that we don't need an MIC because we obviously need our own defense industry and all that kind of thing. It's just that, you know, once these interests get entrenched, then you know, then they become like a lobby and so you may not get the best results, you know, they become special interests. So the question's just how do you keep procurement open enough and flexible enough that you can get startups in there and you can get new companies? You don't want it to just be a handful of the same companies that have been around forever, you actually want to get the innovation into into the Pentagon. But my sense is though that we've got some pretty good people there now who who understand this idea and are trying to get more of the startups and innovation into the defense budget. This is not an area I'm not I have not been involved in procurement, it's not my area. Well one area you are very familiar with, the President's Council of Advisors on Science and Technology. You are now at the helm. This has been a body that's been around since the Truman presidency. In some areas it's been transformative, in others it's been invisible. What can we all expect from your era of PCAST? Well, the first thing is that I think we have an amazing group of luminaries on the council of advisors and that they're not just academics or researchers. But it's also people who actually have built things, they're doers, they're company builders. And I think that reflects something that the President cares about, right? He comes has a business background and I think he respects great business entrepreneurs and visionaries. So we have that on our council. And I think you'll see us push forward with the framework, that national AI framework that I was talking about. And I think we'll also start looking at AI from a more long-dated perspective and start studying some of these issues like, you know, what happens in the economy, what happens with jobs, and again providing that more I guess long-date perspective in DC. I'm going to end on a couple of quick hit questions that I know this group is very interested in. One is, is Dario back in the Pentagon? Well, I saw the President said he's shaping up, so that's a good sign for them. You have done many things in your career, one of those things is investing, and one of those areas you've invested in is defense. A whole bunch of companies, maybe some in the room, companies like Saronic and Allen Control Systems and Anduril. What are we or those companies learning from the current conflict? Well, I think I mean I haven't done a ton of defense investing. I mean, Allen Control Systems is an interesting one because that happened because this was several years ago, I think when the this is during the Biden administration, they were in a conflict with the Houthis and I saw an article on Politico that said that the Pentagon was worried that it was using $4 million Patriots to shoot down $4,000 drones. I talked about that on our podcast and said we need to figure out like a cheaper way of doing that and then the founders met with me and said, "Hey, we have that solution." And I was like, "Okay, sounds like a good idea." I just think, you know, we're seeing we saw this with Ukraine, just the importance of drones. Drones, missiles, rockets, both the offensive capability and then the defensive capability to the ability to shoot them down in a cost-effective way. And then the importance of AI. Again, you know, I'm not involved in that area, so what I know comes from really reading about it, but the use of AI through like this Maven system seems really interesting. I was trying to think of a good acronym for this, so it's like drones, rockets, AI and missiles. I think it's like DR. AIM? I don't know, maybe you guys can come up with a better one, but that's I was just trying to figure out something to but that seems like where it's all headed. Another one that you know a lot about, which is crypto. One of the things you said early on is that the crypto industry just really needed regulatory clarity. Did we get there during your time as czar? Well, we're almost there. So we got there on stablecoins, which is basically dollar-based tokens, and we still have to enact the framework for all the other types of tokens and we have a bill, it's passed the House, it's called CLARITY, and now it's in the Senate and we just need to get it passed the Senate Banking Committee. It's already gotten out there's a piece of it that's related to commodities that actually went through the Senate Agriculture Committee. And if we just get through the Senate Banking Committee and then onto the Senate floor, I think it'll pass and we'll have that bill done. I'm still pretty optimistic we're going to get it done in the next month or so. And yeah, it all goes back to just creating a stable set of rules so that if you're an entrepreneur in the United States who wants to start a crypto company, you know what those rules are, you know how to abide by them, and you can be confident that it's not going to change five years from now or ten years from now, even if there's a change in the party that's in the White House or who has a majority in Congress. The thing that codifying the framework in law gives you is more permanence and that's what we want to give we want to give that certainty to our founders. Okay, final one from the audience but I'm going to ask it. Orbital data centers, bullish or bearish? Well, we're, you know, we're investors in SpaceX so we have to be bullish on that thing. Thank you. Bullish! Bullish, that was bullish for anyone who didn't hear it. Please join me in thanking David Sacks. Thank you so much.

OpenAI Co-Founder on the AI Race, the Sam Altman Firing, and What Comes Next

The Knowledge Project Podcast(388.0K) ·71:42 ·8.8K views · 2026-04-22
Greg Brockman (Co-Founder, OpenAI)
OpenAI co-founder Greg Brockman details the organization’s evolution from a resource-constrained non-profit to a global AI leader, highlighting the necessity of massive compute scaling, the internal resilience required to survive the 2023 board crisi...
TL;DR OpenAI co-founder Greg Brockman details the organization’s evolution from a resource-constrained non-profit to a global AI leader, highlighting the necessity of massive compute scaling, the internal resilience required to survive the 2023 board crisis, and a future where...

TL;DR

OpenAI co-founder Greg Brockman details the organization’s evolution from a resource-constrained non-profit to a global AI leader, highlighting the necessity of massive compute scaling, the internal resilience required to survive the 2023 board crisis, and a future where agentic AI serves as a personalized partner for billions.

The Genesis and Evolution of OpenAI

  • Founding Mission (2015): Brockman left Stripe to co-found OpenAI with Sam Altman, Ilya Sutskever, and John Schulman. The goal was to ensure human-level AI benefited all of humanity, despite the field being dominated at the time by "10,000-pound gorilla" Google DeepMind.
  • The For-Profit Pivot (2017):
      • Compute Realization: In 2017, the team calculated the compute power necessary for AGI and realized it was far beyond non-profit fundraising capabilities.
      • Hardware Catalyst: A meeting with Cerebras, which was building advanced AI hardware, convinced leadership that exclusive access to massive data centers was the only path to success, necessitating a for-profit structure.
  • Early Technical Roadmap: The original "technical plan" involved three pillars: solving reinforcement learning, solving unsupervised learning, and gradually learning "more complicated things."

Breakthroughs in Scaling and Reasoning

  • The Dota 2 Milestone: Brockman identifies the Dota results as a turning point. It proved that "massive compute with simple algorithms" (PPO) could exceed human performance in messy, unprogrammed environments.
      • The "Insect Brain" Insight: The neural net used for Dota had synapses comparable to an insect brain; scaling this to human-brain proportions became the evocative goal for the GPT series.
  • Prediction as Intelligence: Brockman argues that "predicting the next word" is not a pedestrian task. If a model can perfectly predict the next word out of Einstein’s mouth, it possesses Einstein-level intelligence.
  • The 2017 "Sentiment Neuron": A key breakthrough occurred when a model trained purely on next-character prediction spontaneously learned "sentiment" (semantics), proving that machines could learn meaning without explicit supervision.
  • AI-Driven Engineering:
      • Software Production: Brockman notes that the fraction of OpenAI code *not* written by AI is "vanishing." AI currently writes code better than humans given the right context.
      • Scientific Discovery: Models are now solving open math and physics problems, including resolving a quantum physics problem with an elegant formula that contradicted community expectations.

Leadership and the 2023 Board Crisis

  • The Firing Event: Brockman was informed of Sam Altman’s removal via a board video call (minus Altman). He was told he was removed from the board but asked to stay; he quit immediately after speaking with his wife.
  • Team Loyalty:
      • The "Life Raft": Initially, Brockman and Altman planned a small new venture, but the entire company rebelled.
      • Unprecedented Retention: Despite "feeding frenzy" poaching attempts from competitors, OpenAI did not lose a single employee during the weekend of the ouster.
  • Reconciliation with Ilya Sutskever: Brockman describes the pain of the conflict with Sutskever—who had been the officiant at his wedding—but notes they reached "closure" through intense post-crisis communication.

The Future: Agentic AGI and Global Compute

  • From Tools to Agents: The next phase shifts from humans doing work *with* computers to computers doing work *for* humans.
      • Universal Access: Brockman envisions 8 billion people having a "personal AGI" that knows their context, manages their goals, and takes proactive actions (e.g., buying concert tickets or solving health issues).
  • Industrial-Scale Data Centers: Brockman expects to see data centers dedicated entirely to single problems, such as "solving cancer."
      • Grand Challenges: He suggests data centers in space are a "grand challenge" due to the fragility of current hardware, but "all options" are on the table to meet the exponential demand for compute.
  • Iterative Deployment: OpenAI rejects the "ivory tower" approach of building in secret. Brockman argues that deploying intermediate models (like GPT-3 and GPT-4) allows society to build "resilience layers" and helps the lab identify unforeseen misuses, such as "medical spam."

Notable Quotes

"If you really can predict the next word out of Einstein's mouth, you are at least as smart as Einstein." — Greg Brockman
"In the words of Ilya [Sutskever], he always says that you have to suffer. If you're not suffering, you're not building value." — Greg Brockman
"One observation I have is that the right choices are extremely specific to the facts of the technology... It's not just about the model itself; it's really about how they integrate into the world with a society that is resilient." — Greg Brockman
"I think I've just learned to just keep going for something that's worth it. If you have a mission that matters, then the fact of you keep going through the ups and the downs... you just can't let those moments pull you off course." — Greg Brockman
So, how did OpenAI come about? I knew I wanted to do a startup because I felt like that was something... But you were just in a startup. Stripe was a startup. It's true, but I never... I felt like with Stripe, the problem that we were solving was not my problem, right? It wasn't the problem I'd grown up thinking about. It was an important problem that I had dedicated myself to for a number of years, but I felt like it was going to succeed with or without me. And so then I had a first moment to really think about: what is a mission that I want to dedicate myself to, where I would spend the rest of my life working on this problem just to see it play out in a slightly better way? And it was very clear to me that the top of the list was AI. Right, if you can actually make a difference in how AI will play out in the world... like, that would be a life well lived. When you were thinking about leaving, Patrick told you to go talk to Sam Altman. What happened in that conversation? Well, so Patrick had said, "Sam has seen lots of young people in your situation," and Patrick, I think, really hoped that Sam would convince me to stay. A few minutes into talking to Sam, he's like, "Okay, you clearly have already decided. It is very obvious." And so he asked, "Well, what are you planning on doing next?" And I said, "Well, I'm thinking about doing an AI company." And he said, "I'm also thinking about doing something in AI. We should keep in touch." So I had talked to Sam maybe one more time after I was leaving Stripe, and he asked, "Are you still thinking about doing something in AI?" I said, "Yes." He said, "I'm also starting to get more details and putting together this dinner in July." And I flew out for the dinner. And the thing that I remember was a topic was: is it too late to start a lab with many of the best researchers? Is it possible? And this is what year? 2015. Right, because you think about just the degree to which DeepMind had all the researchers, all the capital, and all the data. It just felt like, is it even possible to get something off the ground still? People came up with all sorts of reasons it was hard; no one could come up with a reason it was actually impossible. And so Sam and I, driving back to the city that night, I remember we looked at each other and we said, "We gotta do this. Right, like, we just have to." And so the next day I was full-time on putting this together. And it was tough because it was very ill-defined. We had a mission, a vision of saying, we think that we can build human-level AI, make it be something positive for the world, and make the benefits be something that are distributed broadly. But how? And how do you get people to actually leave their jobs to come and join this thing? Initially, the set of people that I narrowed down to were actually Ilya, Dario Amodei, Chris Olah, and myself. That was going to be the team. And we spent a lot of time together, we spent a lot of time talking about potential visions for the lab, and potential ways that things would work. It didn't quite come together, and there was just partly a question of: will this have enough momentum? You know, Dario felt like he needed to go and establish a name for himself and he wasn't sure if this was really going to be it. It was a question of just how it was all going to work. And in the meanwhile, I was starting to get John Schulman interested. He said that he was going to do it. Dario and Chris ended up deciding to go to Google Brain, and so it was really just, you know, Ilya, me, and John starting to be, maybe a few others. And so I had a group of about 10 people where many of them were saying, "I'm interested, but who else is in?" I asked Sam, "Okay, how do we break symmetry here? Like, how do we actually get everyone to say, 'Alright, we're joining'?" And Sam's suggestion was: invite people out for an offsite. So we set up a thing in Napa, and I actually made t-shirts. And this is before they had joined? There were no official offers, no one had joined, we didn't have a structure, and we had nothing, right? We just had an idea, we had a vision, and we had a mission. And we flew people out, we drove up to Napa together, and it was an amazing day. Right, the ideas were flowing, and we came up with what I would really say is almost the technical plan that we have pursued for the past 10 years. Number one: solve reinforcement learning. Number two: solve unsupervised learning. And number three was: gradually learn more complicated "things". After that offsite, I sent offers to everyone and said, "Hey, we want to get started in the next two to three weeks. Please let me know if you're in." Why did you think that DeepMind had such an insurmountable advantage? It was very much the case that Google DeepMind was the 10,000-pound gorilla in the field. They just had lots of capital, and they had the track record. And this was before AlphaGo, right? AlphaGo came out a few months later, but it wasn't a surprise, right? It's like, very much the momentum was very clearly there. And so the question of: is it really possible to build something independent and new? It wasn't obvious. At what point did you realize that, like, this nonprofit thing just wasn't going to work? In 2017, we started to think very hard about: first of all, how do we really achieve the mission? How do we actually build an AGI? What will that look like? And we started to do the math on compute, and you start to realize that it's going to take a big computer. We came across a company called Cerebras, which was building a unique piece of computing hardware, and we realized that the kind of computer that they were promising was going to be far advanced of where our compute calculations looked. And so you start to realize if we could buy a lot of those computers, we could actually probably succeed at building an AGI. If we could get exclusive access to Cerebras, that could give us an overwhelming advantage. If we could buy very large data centers, that could be something unique as well. And the thing about nonprofit fundraising is I think that there is essentially a cap to what is possible there. And so Elon, Sam, Ilya, and I all agreed that the only path forward for OpenAI, the only path to achieve the mission, was to create a for-profit entity associated with OpenAI of some form. And so we were committed to that direction, and that is something that we knew was the only way to achieve the mission. When was the moment that you realized everything was going to change for you? Was that Dota or was it before then or after? The way that OpenAI works is it's a series of moments where you realize that it's real now. And every time you think that you understand it, that it has really settled in for you, you realize that there is a new horizon you had not yet appreciated. And so along the way, I think that there was the initial launch. It was like, wow, we actually got a team together, now we can pursue this mission. But you show up at the office the next day and you're like, "Well, what do we do?" Right, we didn't even have a whiteboard. You know, Ilya and John wanted to write something on a whiteboard. I was like, "I will get a whiteboard. That's something I can do." Dota, we had our first big result. Right, that really was like, "Wow, we can actually accomplish something when we put our mind to it." You can actually see all this compute coming together, you scale up the compute, and you scale up the result. There were multiple moments with the GPT series, and I remember actually an early moment was the unsupervised sentiment neuron paper. Have you heard of that one? I've heard of it, but I haven't read it. Okay, yes, that one's an interesting one because it's 2017, and it's really the first time that we saw semantics arise from training on a language modeling objective. So you train on: learn the next character, predict the next character, and then suddenly you get a neural net that understands sentiment, understands if something is positive or negative, which is harder than it sounds. But that was a moment where you realized, "Wow, we are building machines that can learn semantics, not just where the commas are and where the nouns and verbs are, but it can really learn the meaning of sentences." You got to push that. And then of course, when you see something like GPT-4, I remember we were playing with it and someone asked, "Why is this thing not an AGI?" Right, it's like, actually really hard to put your finger on it because you can talk to it fluently in anything you want. It clearly wasn't an AGI; it was lacking something. But just if you'd described your criteria for AGI two months prior, it probably would have been compatible with what GPT-4 was. And so there are many moments along the way where you feel like it's real now, it's going to really happen, and the economy is going to transform into this compute-powered world. And I think that those moments are not yet at the end. I think that we have many more breakthrough moments where you realize that the next stage is possible. I thought Dota was like an incredible moment because it wasn't chess, like Deep Blue, and it wasn't AlphaGo, which is like computationally intensive but very defined rules. It was actually interactive against humans in a way that, like, the world is sort of structured but you have all this freedom. Yeah, that was something very compelling about it. And the ironic thing is we'd actually set out with Dota to develop new methods because the reinforcement learning at the time was clearly not going to scale. Right, that the algorithm we used called PPO, you plan over every single time step. There's no hierarchy, right? As a human, that's not how you plan your day. And so we knew that this algorithm was incredibly flawed, would never scale, and had all these problems. But you got to start somewhere. You got to push your baselines to reach the wall so you actually see the limits of what good looks like with what you have, and then you can bring to bear a new algorithm. And we just kept scaling PPO, and we exceeded the performance of the best humans. And that itself was the finding, right? That actually massive compute with simple algorithms, right, that is something where it doesn't just work in theory, it works in practice. We can really make it happen. And in this incredibly messy environment where you cannot program it, you cannot look ahead, and you cannot do a search, you just need this almost human-like intuition. And by the way, the neural net we used: tiny, tiny little insect brain. Similar number of synapses to truly an insect brain. And you realize like, wait, what if you had the same computational approach but scaled it up to something that's much more human-brain scale? What would that be like? Very, very evocative question. Is there a difference between reasoning and predicting? You mentioned sort of like predicting the next character, predicting the next word, versus actually reasoning in first principles. I think they are connected in a deep way. So on the one hand, just predicting what comes next sounds like a pedestrian task. But if you really can predict the next word out of Einstein's mouth, you are at least as smart as Einstein. And you can make arguments, "Oh, well, like, you know, it's just..." but I think that those arguments fall flat, that there's something false there because the point of prediction is not about being able to predict what is known. The point is you put yourself in a new situation you've never seen before and predict what comes next. And I think that there's something deeply connected to intelligence and prediction that there's a long story of academic literature and how you think about this compression—they're all kind of part of the same thing. Now these reasoning models, the thing that I think is very interesting is that we train them with reinforcement learning. And so there's really, back to the original OpenAI plan, there's two steps to it. The first is unsupervised learning: you train a model just by having it predict what comes next, and there it's much more static data, it's much more observational. Again, it's data it's never seen before, situations it's never seen before, but it is a situation that has already happened. Then you do reinforcement learning, which is you basically have the AI learn on its own data, right? You have it make its own: here's the action I'm going to take. You get an observation from the world and you learn from that. And it's again, the way you actually train it is still predicting. It's trying to predict if I take this action, what's the thing that's likely to happen, and you reinforce that depending on how good of a job you did. And the beauty of that is that it now is an AI that has this background knowledge and has real-world experience. But fundamentally, the technology that we use to train during the unsupervised stage and during the reinforcing stage, they're exactly the same. You are just predicting, but you've changed the structure of the data. When did things start to get tense? I think the thing about OpenAI is that if you truly believe in the mission, if you truly believe in the possibility of creating machines that have the intelligence level of humans, it means the stakes always feel very high. The question of who's making the decision, the question of what are the values that go into those decisions, the question of these things that are maybe mundane in a typical company—that are much more like office politics—start to take on this existential weight. And I think that that has colored a lot of how OpenAI, these more high-profile conflicts... you know, sometimes it's like even just the question of who gets credit for a particular thing, and suddenly it takes on this existential weight. Well, that's where I was sort of thinking about this because it's like, at that point you probably realized this technology is inevitable and it's going to change the world. And that wasn't broadly known to the world, and then I would imagine there's people who are like, "I want to be front and center. I want to take credit for this." Yes. That is the overwhelming dynamic that I have observed in this field. It's not just about OpenAI, actually. Like, one observation I had early on is that this technology is by nature very fragmentary. Right, it's sometimes... it's like when you have a lot of pressure, you can get a diamond or you can get cracks. Often you'll see diamonds form in pockets, right? Teams of people that really work together, that have a lot of high trust, that know how to operate. But sometimes you can see that they splinter off and they kind of go their own way. And I think within AI, I think we've gotten some real benefits out of diversity of approach and different groups that are really pushing each other in order to both bring this technology in a more beneficial way. Sometimes it is how to think about all the thorny questions around safety, around what does it mean to be safe, what does it mean to actually deploy this technology, and how to think about how to mitigate but also how to maximize those benefits. And that's something where I think that there's a lot of very healthy debate. It's always gone on within OpenAI's walls. Now it's starting to really happen, I think, in the world. And I think that's something that we as a society really benefit from. The sponsor of this show is CoinShares. While most of the industry was still arguing about whether digital assets were legitimate, CoinShares was quietly building the infrastructure to invest in them properly. They now manage over six billion in assets and have stayed profitable through every market cycle. Fully regulated, with the kind of transparency and governance that serious investors actually expect. Whether it's crypto ETFs, active strategies, or Bitcoin mining ETF exposure, you can access all of it through your existing brokerage account. CoinShares. The adults have arrived. Learn more at coinshares.com. This is not investment advice. You're in meetings all day. You're trying to stay present but you're also worried you'll forget the decision, the action item, the important next step. That's where Granola comes in. Granola's an AI-powered notepad for meetings. You jot down rough notes like you always do, and in the background Granola transcribes and turns them into clear useful notes when the meeting ends. There are no bots joining your calls, no distractions, just a clean notepad that helps you focus. During or after the call, you can chat with your notes. You can ask Granola to pull out action items, help you negotiate, make a decision, write a follow-up email, and so much more. I even use it when I'm listening to podcasts. Once you try it on a first meeting, it's hard to go without. Head to granola.ai/shane and get three months free with the code SHANE. That's granola.ai/shane. Take me back to the moment you found out that Sam had been fired. Where were you? I was at home. And what happened? I got a text saying, "Can we hop on a video call?" So I hopped on the video call. I noticed that it was the board, minus Sam, who were on there. Did you know at that point? No. I mean, I inferred something was up, but... 'Cause you're on the board. I am on the board, or was on the board at this point. Yes. And then what happened? I was told that the board has decided that Sam would be removed, and effectively the message that I got was the same messaging that was in the public post. And I asked if I could have any more information. I was told no, not right now. And I pressed on that, maybe another time, and again was told nothing more to share. And then was told, "Wait, there's more." Also that I had been removed from the board but would be staying with the company because I was very critical to the company, the mission. I said, again, I asked if I could get any reasons, any feedback. Was told no. Towards the end, was told that, hey, like, in this new setup that you will start to get, hopefully you can get feedback in this new configuration. And so that was the conversation. What went through your mind? It just wasn't right. Was it anger? No. I felt like I understood what had happened. How long before you knew what had actually transpired to sort of cause this? Well, there's two parts to the answer. One is I feel like I still am learning some additional facts, some additional thing that was in someone's head. To some extent, it comes down to a lack of communication, right? That you realize that there are all these different things that built up. And to some extent, you know, approximately I kind of knew, I was like, I understand for every person here, I have a pretty good model of why they acted the way that they did. But it wasn't what was most important to me in the moment. I just knew that this wasn't right. Right after I hung up the call, talked to my wife, and I said, "Gotta quit." And she said, "I agree." And you quit that day. Yes. And then what happened? That day when I quit, started to get all these messages, people saying, "I don't know what you and Sam are doing next, but I'm with you. I want to go start something with you." Like, just that was a real, honest surprise. I didn't really expect to get that kind of support, that kind of outpouring. There were a few of my close collaborators who quit that day as well—that's Jakub, Szymon, Aleksander—and the five of us, so those people plus Sam, we all got together and we started to chart out what a new company could look like. I remember feeling that first day like, "Okay, there's a 10% chance that we actually get the company back. 10%." The next day we set up a meeting at Sam's house, a bunch of people from the company came by, and we showed the vision that we'd been sketching out. So it was really one day in, you know, this fresh picture of how we'd run the project. And we spent a bunch of time over that weekend also negotiating with the board and the company and trying to figure out: is there a path back together that makes sense? That Sunday night, the board replaced Mira as interim CEO with a new person, and the company just rebelled. Like, we'd actually been at the office, we thought we were close to a deal... To come back. To come back, yeah. We thought that we had a path, and then the board made that change, and then suddenly it was everyone streaming out of the building and it was just like real chaos. I was on video calls with many of the people who had been interested in coming to this new company saying, "It's going to be okay, we have a plan." And we expanded... you know, we'd been building this little life raft, right? For the small set of people we expected to want to come. And suddenly it was like no one wanted to be associated with this entity, right? People wanted to, you know, stand up for what they viewed as right. Sam talked to Satya, who we'd been talking about, "Hey, can you be a funder? Could you, you know, help support this new endeavor?" and I was like, "Hey, actually, could we expand from the small life raft to like a big life raft?" Everybody! Yeah! "Could we take everyone?" And they're like, "Alright, well, we'll figure it out somehow." And a lot of people, this was right before Thanksgiving, a lot of people were supposed to be flying home, wherever that is, and instead they canceled their flights and the office was packed. It was like everyone's at the office just to be there, be part of it, and just you know even if they couldn't contribute to any of these conversations, they just wanted to be there as this history was made. Then this petition starts to circulate. So many people were trying to sign the petition at once, it actually crashed Google Docs. And so then you had to have certain people who were designated as the person you go to to actually put your name on the document so you don't have too many editors at once. I think that that was a statement that was really heard loudly. And I remember... you know, I probably got home around like 4:00 AM or something, went to sleep, and I woke up like 45 minutes later and I checked Twitter and I saw that Ilya had posted and had signed the petition, and it said that he wanted the company to come back together. And that was this real moment of relief. I felt so much gratitude that it just felt like, okay, like we can put this back together, we can get back to a good track. You and Ilya built this together. What was it like trying to find your way back to that relationship after? Look, it was tough. It was definitely... that was definitely a very close relationship. He'd been the officiant at my civil ceremony. Right, we'd been through extremely tough times together, and like any relationship, you always have your ups and downs. And we spent a lot of time afterwards really talking things through and really trying to understand and just articulate some of the things that we had let build up or had left unsaid between us. And I think that we got through that process; I think we had gotten to a really good place and for me it was I felt like we got to closure on everything that had happened. How did you feel about all the loyalty you inspired? Deeply grateful. Truly it was never something that I would have asked for, never something I would have expected. I think the way that I operate is I'm very much an "in the trenches" kind of leader. Try to lead from the front. And sometimes when I do that, I don't always... sorry, I'm getting a little emotional. But I don't always look back to see if everyone's following, right? I just like run right in. And when people do, when people do come and really help to build the thing, I just... it makes me feel so grateful for them and to feel like they have exceeded my expectations in every way. And so eventually everybody comes back. I'll tell you, it was not guaranteed. Because throughout that weekend all the competitors were circling. Just imagine this like feeding frenzy that people were shaping up to do. People are getting offers, and we actually did not lose a single person through that weekend. No one accepted a competing offer. I think that's incredible. It really was. That's more, you know, Coach Belichick told me this actually, when we were talking about the best teams, and he said they're not playing for money, they're playing for the person beside them. And when you were saying that all these people quit, it makes me think of that. Like, and none of them left for presumably more money, better offers. Everybody was trying to circle and poach. Yeah, that was a diamond moment. After all of this happened, you took some time off. What was going on internally with you? That was an intense experience to go through and an intense experience to come back to. And honestly, just one of the hardest moments for me at OpenAI was when Ilya left. And it was maybe the only moment in OpenAI's history where I felt like I didn't want to do it anymore. I think I needed some time to kind of find my way back to remembering like why I was doing this and why it was so important and why it was worth the pain. What did you do during the time off? I trained language models on... That's when you learned how to do it, right? Like you did the self-study thing I read on your blog? Well, no. So I had actually done that throughout the course of OpenAI. So I trained language models on DNA sequences. Oh wow. For Arc? For Arc Institute, yeah. And it was a very great experience. I took my skills that I had and applied them to a very different domain, and a domain that's very personally meaningful to both me and to my wife. You know, she has a lot of health conditions and we think about what AI can do for her health, what it can do for the health of animals that we're both very passionate about. Just it's like this application area that felt like maybe we could help in this very different way from how I've been pursuing this technology. So that was a very positive part of the experience. If I were to say, like, open a Google document, write out sort of what you learned about yourself on one page from this whole starting to Sam getting ousted to you quitting to inspiring all this loyalty to the time off and then coming back. What would you write? I think I've just learned to just keep going for something that's worth it. Right, if you have a mission that matters, then the fact of you keep going through the ups and the downs, there are going to be moments where it's all over, there are moments where we're so back, and you just can't let those moments pull you off course. And I think that the degree of just personal resilience that you have to grow during these times, because if you're leading, people look to you for that steadiness, for that support, for the direction that the whole thing will go.
