  • Agency Independence: Kennedy assured the committee that the new CDC Director would have the authority to act independently of political appointees regarding immunization schedules.

Rural Health and Social Policy

  • Rural Health Transformation Fund: The budget allocates $50 billion over five years to strengthen rural hospitals, focusing on telehealth and transitioning failing business models.
  • Reproductive Health: Chair Cassidy pressured Kennedy on reinstating in-person requirements for chemical abortion drugs to prevent "coercion." Kennedy demurred due to ongoing litigation but noted the FDA is prosecuting the sale of misbranded/counterfeit drugs.
  • Disability Services: Senator Andy Kim questioned proposed cuts to the Limb Loss and Paralysis Resource Centers; Kennedy stated these were likely cut due to perceived duplication but offered to reconsider.
  • Emerging Risks: Senator Josh Hawley raised concerns regarding the health risks (EMF, noise, neurological issues) of 4,000+ data centers being built nationwide; Kennedy committed to meta-reviews by the Surgeon General.
"Our children are the sickest generation in modern history. And decades of failed policies, captured agencies, and profit-driven systems caused it. Parents across the country demand a change, and we are delivering it." — HHS Secretary Robert F. Kennedy, Jr.
"There is something fundamentally wrong when we are spending twice as much per capita on healthcare as the people of any other nation, and yet 85 million Americans are uninsured or underinsured." — Senator Bernie Sanders
"AI is going to revolutionize medicine... we've used it to dramatically shorten the approval time for drugs. From the review of the final application normally taking 60 days, we've compressed that now to about 2 hours." — HHS Secretary Robert F. Kennedy, Jr.
"If the patient has the power, if the patient is the reason for the care and not an excuse for the bill, then prices tend to come down." — Senator Bill Cassidy
I'll go back to my experience practicing medicine. If the patient has the power, if the patient is the reason for the care and not an excuse for the bill, then prices tend to come down. The question is, how do we achieve that? I believe the President is focused on this, and Mr. Secretary, I think you believe this as well. So, I look forward to hearing how this administration's proposed budget makes the patient the one in charge. Now, there's some common sense things we can do. How can we get life-saving drugs to patients faster and cheaper? Last week, the HELP Committee held a hearing on increasing access to generic and biosimilar drugs, which cost less and allow patients to keep more money in their pockets while still having access to the medicines they need. Another thing that can lower cost is getting new drugs onto the market more rapidly. In February, I released a report as to how to do that. Getting faster treatments lowers R&D costs; those contribute to the research and development cost, and if they're higher, they contribute to higher prices. We can do this while maintaining the gold standard for safety and effectiveness. Many of the ideas discussed in my report and by our witnesses at the hearing last week—for both new treatments and generics—were included in policies that the administration has proposed in this year's budget. This is something that we can work on together. There are other ways to make health insurance and healthcare more affordable. Last week, I released the MVP agenda, Money and Value for Patients, to build upon President Trump's efforts to give money to patients rather than insurance companies to use for their out-of-pocket costs. If paired with price transparency, the patient can shop for the most affordable healthcare, eliminating unnecessary bureaucracy and eliminating profit that doesn't flow to patients, but flows to entities that pad cost in the system for their own benefit. And that price transparency bill is a bipartisan bill by members of this committee. While we speak of affordability, let's just recognize that if we don't come up with treatments and cures for things like Alzheimer's, dementia, and cancer, then the boomer generation could bankrupt our country for the cost of care for these conditions. And the way to address that is to invest heavily in science and to use that investment effectively. I would argue that includes taking the resources of the NIH and making them more accessible to researchers at a wide variety of universities so that it is the collective brainpower across our nation and not just the brainpower at a few concentrated institutions or cities. I credit Dr. Bhattacharya, who came before our committee and was very encouraging regarding his understanding of these issues. Lastly, standing up for patients includes standing up for the most vulnerable. Protecting women from the dangers of chemical abortion drugs is a step we can take right now to protect life. Frankly, many pro-life Louisianians have been disappointed by the lack of action on this front. We need a willing partner to stand up for life. Mr. Secretary, you have said that every abortion is a tragedy. I would say it's time to stop stalling on the safety study for abortion drugs. It's time to reinstate the in-person safeguards protecting women from abuse and coercion. I will note, there are criminal charges against a woman, against a mother in Louisiana and in other jurisdictions, I'm told boyfriends, coercing or tricking a pregnant person into taking these pills. All this would be eliminated with the restoration of the in-person requirement. By the way, this is public record. The young woman, the young victim, was Rosalie Marcavage. So, there is a prosecution of the person that gave it. If there was an in-person requirement reinstated by this administration, the doctor would have said, "Do you want this pill?" and she would have said, "No, I want my baby." Instead, she was coerced, and that's the tragedy that has resulted. Secretary Kennedy, there is so much this committee and the President's administration can accomplish. People in Louisiana are struggling. They expect Congress and the administration to do our part as they do their part. Let's meet the moment. Thank you again, Mr. Secretary, for coming to discuss the proposed HHS budget. With that, I recognize Senator Sanders for his opening statement. Thank you very much, Mr. Chairman. Good to see you again, Secretary Kennedy. Let me just respond to the Chairman. A strong majority of the American people believe that it is women who have the right to control their own lives, not the government. I happen to believe that is where we should be going as a nation. That's a choice of women, not the government. It is no secret that the American healthcare system is broken. Every place I go when I speak, I ask people to raise their hands. In conservative areas and progressive areas, people understand that there is something fundamentally wrong when we are spending twice as much per capita on healthcare as the people of any other nation, and yet 85 million Americans are uninsured or underinsured. We pay the highest prices in the world for prescription drugs. Our life expectancy is significantly lower than other wealthy nations. And for working-class people, it is six years shorter than for wealthy people in this country. The system is broken. It is designed to make the insurance companies and the drug companies extremely wealthy, and that is what's happening, and ordinary people are being left behind. I would have hoped that we would have an administration that would stand up for average people and say, "You know what? Let's do what every other major country on earth does: guarantee healthcare to all people." Think that's a radical idea? Every other country on earth does it. And they spend substantially less than we do. And yet we are moving in exactly the opposite direction as a result of the quote-unquote "big beautiful bill." Fifteen million Americans have been thrown off the healthcare that they need. But that's not the only problem that we have with what Secretary Kennedy and the President are doing. I talk to doctors, as I'm sure everybody on this committee does, in my own state of Vermont and all over this country. And doctors are extremely concerned by many of the actions that we're seeing from HHS and the CDC. They are concerned that Secretary Kennedy has directed the Centers for Disease Control to publish false information on its website suggesting that childhood vaccines cause autism, despite the findings of more than 40 scientific studies in seven countries that there is no link between vaccines and autism. We have seen HHS spread misinformation about the safety and effectiveness of the measles vaccine during the largest measles outbreak in the United States in over 30 years. We have seen HHS remove the director of the CDC because she refused to rubber-stamp the very dangerous and unsubstantiated vaccine recommendations coming from the agency. We have seen the agency defund promising vaccine research that will leave us woefully unprepared. Mr. Chairman, you'll remember a couple of years ago we had the leading experts in this country, and we asked them, "Do you anticipate another pandemic after COVID?" And the answer was unanimously, "Yes." There is a fear—nobody knows when another pandemic will come—and we are now woefully unprepared to address that crisis if and when it comes. We have seen the CDC and HHS under Secretary Kennedy's leadership; we have seen now 3,800 people in America have been infected with measles, the worst outbreak in over 30 years. Vaccination rates across the country are falling because parents, I think, are very confused about what they're hearing from Washington, and children are dying as a result. So right now we are looking at a healthcare system which is broken, and we have leadership in Washington which is making it even worse. And what I will chat with you about when I have my questions, Secretary Kennedy, is things that you have written which call into doubt the very existence of the germ theory, which is the basis of modern medicine. Yeah, let's talk about that in a minute. But the bottom line is what we would like to see is a broken healthcare system improved. Most of us believe that healthcare is a human right, that all people should have healthcare, and that we need public health policies which keep our kids and all people healthy, not moving us in the other direction. Thank you, Mr. Chairman. Thank you. We're joined today by the Honorable Robert F. Kennedy Jr., the 26th Secretary of Health and Human Services. In his role as Secretary, Mr. Kennedy is responsible for overseeing the nation's civilian federal health agencies, which support direct services for over 150 million Americans and keep all Americans safe by supporting sound science and through the regulation of medical and consumer products. I look forward to hearing from you today, sir. Thank you for joining us. Can you hear me? Okay. Chairman Cassidy, Ranking Member Sanders, and members of the committee, thank you for the opportunity to appear before you today to talk about the President's 2027 budget for HHS. We stand at a generational turning point. Our children are the sickest generation in modern history. Decades of failed policies, captured agencies, and profit-driven systems caused it. Parents across the country demand a change, and we are delivering it. We're ending the era of federal policies that fueled this chronic disease epidemic and replacing them with policies that put the health of Americans first. President Trump and I are challenging the status quo and the institutions that defend it as we work to make America healthy again. In just 15 months, HHS has delivered historic wins. We negotiated most-favored-nation drug prices with 16 of the largest drug companies so Americans no longer pay more than other people in wealthy countries for the same medications. We're bringing real transparency to healthcare pricing so that patients know the cost of care before they receive it. I used the full convening power of the federal government to bring health insurance CEOs to the table to reform prior authorization, and I convened the 405 biggest tech companies in our country to end information blocking and allow American patients access to their health records on their cellphones for the first time in history. We're cutting red tape, speeding decisions, and demanding transparency. We're also cracking down on waste, fraud, and abuse. This year, HHS and the USDA issued new dietary guidelines that put real whole food at the center of the American plate. We flipped the food pyramid upside down and sent a clear message to Americans: eat real food. HHS also opened the door to partnerships with industry, trade associations, nonprofits, and advocacy groups. More than 50 medical schools have committed to expand nutrition education from an average of just two hours to 40 hours. Food manufacturers are stepping up, too. More than 40 percent of the food industry has committed to phase out petroleum-based dyes by year-end, and many have already eliminated them. In conjunction with these efforts, the FDA approved six natural food colorings from fruits and vegetables. Through President Trump's Great American Recovery initiative, HHS is matching compassion with action to help Americans break the cycle of addiction. At HHS, we are prioritizing patients with ultra-rare diseases and their families and driving faster access to life-saving treatments. We're restoring gold standard science and integrity across the agency. We're protecting children from sex-rejecting procedures that expose them to irreversible harm. We're eliminating outdated and misleading warning labels on hormone therapies used to treat women during menopause. We're strengthening oversight of organ procurement. We're implementing Operation Stork Speed to ensure the safety and quality of infant formula. We're applying that same focus and urgency to rural America. The Rural Health Transformation Fund delivers the largest investment in rural health in our nation's history: 50 billion dollars over five years to strengthen rural hospitals and ensure Americans can access the care they need no matter where they live. HHS announced more than 135 million dollar investment this month to expand rural residency programs and nutrition services. The data is clear: when physicians train in rural communities, they are far more likely to stay and serve there. The President's budget puts all these priorities in action. It invests in prevention because preventing disease costs less and delivers better outcomes than treating it. As my uncle President John F. Kennedy said, "Progress is a nice word, but change is its motivator and change has its enemies." We see those forces clearly: entrenched interests, defenders of a failing status quo, and institutions that put profit ahead of the American people. That resistance underscores the urgency of this moment. We can reverse chronic disease, improve public health, and lower cost. I stand ready to work with this committee and Congress to seize this opportunity to implement and codify lasting and generational reform in American healthcare for our country, for our children, and for the health of the American people. Together, we can make America healthy again. Thank you. Thank you, Mr. Secretary. Glad that you've given a couple of these recently; you hit it right on time. I shall begin. Mr. Secretary, as I mentioned in my opening statements, I'm concerned that one of the Biden administration's pro-abortion policies, which is the removal of the in-person dispensing requirement for chemical abortion drugs, is still in place. And by the way, Senator Sanders objected to me objecting to that. We're speaking about people ordering drugs and tricking a woman into taking them or coercing them into taking it. These are subject to criminal charges in courts right now. We should all be opposed to that. Secretary Kennedy, why has the department not acted with greater urgency on reinstating the in-person dispensing requirement? Senator Cassidy, I appreciate your comments. I, unfortunately, will not be able to talk about that. I thought that might be the answer, which is frustrating because this has been litigated for years. At some point we'll need to, but moving off the litigation, last month my colleagues and I asked you to address unapproved and misbranded chemical abortion drugs coming from international providers bypassing the FDA. The first Trump administration worked on the issue, but the problem continues. Why hasn't the department taken steps to stop the illegal importation and sale of counterfeit and unapproved abortion drugs? Oh, we take our responsibility seriously to prosecute people who are selling misbranded drugs. I can talk to my enforcement unit at the FDA to figure out why that's not happening to your satisfaction, but as far as I know, we're doing that to the utmost possible. That is not the case. Put it this way: it may be as much as possible, but that would be no effort whatsoever. And I say that because however you feel about these issues, illegal and counterfeit drugs being sold and imported should be a common concern. Next, Mr. Secretary, you have talked about restoring trust in the agency around the issue of immunization. People lost trust during the pandemic. But I think it's safe to say the trust gap has worsened over the last year due to false statements about the safety and efficacy of vaccines for preventable diseases like measles. Now, I am a guy who has seen people die from vaccine-preventable diseases... I didn't hear you, Senator. I am a doctor who has seen people die from vaccine-preventable diseases. And when I see outbreaks numbering in the thousands and people dying once more from vaccine-preventable diseases, particularly children, it seems more than tragic. Now, the CDC director, I applaud. You have said in a previous hearing that you were not involved in her selection but you agree with President Trump's selection. I've not met with her yet formally but I agree as well. She seems to be a qualified person. I did not say that I was not involved in her selection. I didn't follow. So my question here, with the new CDC director, whomever she or he is: there are currently political appointees at the CDC who have worked to undermine trust in immunizations. Will the new director have the right to make decisions independently of those political appointees, and or replace them, or otherwise reassign them so they cannot continue to actively undermine trust in immunizations? Your characterization of the political appointees is wrong. And the CDC director has that power. Now, so she will have, if she wishes to make a decision independently of them, she shall be allowed to make that decision independently. That's correct? Yes. Okay. That's pertinent. This summer we're hosting the World Cup and celebrating America's 250th birthday. Tourists from all over the world will come to the United States. Unfortunately, some of them will bring vaccine-preventable diseases like measles. Unfortunately, our measles immunization rates have fallen, which means that this increases the risk of a measles outbreak in the US. What steps is HHS taking to address the outbreaks that may arise from the World Cup and the folks coming here? Mr. Chairman, we've done better at controlling the measles outbreak than any country in the world. This is a global outbreak. It started before I came to office. Eighty percent of the children affected are over five years old, which means their decision not to vaccinate predated my appointment. We have limited that last year to 2,200 cases. Mexico had three times that much with one-third of our population. Canada had double that much with one-eighth of our population. Europe had 127,000 cases the year before. We have a global pandemic. It has nothing to do with me. I wasn't in any of those countries. It has to do with... We should not be comparing ourselves... ...I've done a better job at limiting it than any country in the world and you can talk to Governor McMaster about that and Governor Abbott about the help that we've given them in Texas. We know how to control the outbreaks. The question was, what steps has the administration taken to track the diseases that would come in potentially through these? You're pointing out that other countries have outbreaks, which increases the likelihood they will bring that here and our immunization rates have fallen below 95 percent. Unfortunately, I'm out of time on this question. I will yield to Senator Sanders, but I may come back 'cause that question wasn't answered. And I can tell you we are laser-focused on that. We have booths and testing booths and institutions all over the games. Okay. Senator Sanders. Thank you. Secretary Kennedy, thanks for being with us. In your book, 'The Real Anthony Fauci', you wrote, and I quote, "A doctrinal canon of the germ theory credits vaccines for the dramatic declines of infectious disease mortalities in North America and Europe during the 20th century. Most Americans accept this claim as dogma. It will therefore come as a surprise to learn that it is simply untrue." But despite your assertion in your book, according to a peer-reviewed study in The Lancet led by the World Health Organization, vaccines have saved the lives of more than 150 million people and reduced infant deaths by 40 percent over the past 50 years. So my question is a simple one. Do you still believe that one of the central tenets of the germ theory—that vaccines sharply reduce infant mortality—is quote-unquote "simply untrue"? What I was saying in that book—first of all, the study you cited is a modeling study. It's a mod... sorry? A modeling study. The CDC has actually done a real study that answers that question. It's called Geyer, G-U-Y-E-R, 2000. And it says that the 80 percent decline in mortality that took place in the 20th century—that almost none of it was attributable to vaccination. It was attributable to hygiene, to sewer plants, to better water supplies, to engineering that got oranges... Wait, Secretary, I have to interrupt you 'cause I have other questions. You're entitled to your view. But that is... That's the CDC's view. Well, you're entitled to the CDC's view, but according to the World Health Organization and scientists all over the world, vaccines have played an enormous role in saving lives. I don't contest that. I'm just saying if you want to talk about why disease mortalities disappeared in the 20th century, it was not vaccines. Well, you are in a minority of opinion on that. All right, I got other questions. One second, all right. Secretary Kennedy, you have talked about, I think correctly, the fact that a lot of our kids are eating ultra-processed food, foods high in sugar. We have, as you know, an epidemic in obesity in this country. We spend billions of dollars dealing with diabetes caused by obesity. I believe that we should follow countries like Chile and Mexico and put strong warning labels on products that will show parents the nature of the food their kids are eating. So my question to you is, how long will it take before your agency puts strong warning labels on unhealthy food and beverages? Early in April, we did our final regulations finally having a definition for what ultra-processed food is. That is now in the interagency process. We have no control over what happens there with the other agencies. As soon as it comes back to us, we're going to do the labeling requirement. Let me ask you this. President Trump's nominee for Surgeon General, Casey Means, told this committee that she agreed with me that junk food ads in the United States should be banned from TV. What do you think? Do you agree with her? I would support that. Good. In other words, what we're seeing now is these big food industry companies spending huge amounts of money telling kids to eat crap. And I think it would make a lot of sense if we banned those ads from TV. I'm hearing you saying that you agree with me on that? The only hesitation I have was that we tried to do a smoking ban on TV and the tobacco companies voluntarily came to the table, which was a good thing. And I think the same argument applies for junk food. They're probably even worse for Americans than smoking. Good. Well, all right, I would hope you take a leadership role in making it clear that we should get these ads which entice kids, addict kids to really bad food, off the air. All right? Good. Thank you, Mr. Chairman. Senator Paul. Senator Kennedy, thanks for appearing. I think Senator Sanders makes a great point on obesity and type 2 diabetes. Replacing ads or warning people that food is bad would be one thing, but one thing we could do immediately would be having the taxpayer quit subsidizing bad food. Soda infused with sugar, chips, dips, Twinkies, Ding Dongs, donuts, bags of candy. We should agree, and I can't understand—I've had this bill for a couple of years—we need to get somebody across the aisle that cares about nutrition enough to say we shouldn't subsidize bad things. And one thing I think you have done is actually directed the states and given states waivers to try to do this, but we need to codify that. I won't ask you a question on this; I just hope that the administration will think about supporting the bill and letting other Republicans know that you will support it if it comes through. My question is this, though, 'cause I think there's agreement to a lot of problems, but we disagree on solutions. We agree with the problem of high drug prices. It's a problem. Some people, really on both sides, say well if we just publish the price the problem will go away. I'm one who doesn't believe that at all, 'cause I think most prices are fixed and most consumers really don't care about the price why the prices are high. Now, why don't they care about the price? Because they don't pay it. It's either fixed through their insurance company, it's fixed through Medicare, or it's fixed through Medicaid. Almost all the prices are fixed. The only people who actually pay the price of the drugs and care about the price are people who have a high deductible and have an HSA. So there are a couple of things we could do that would transform the drug pricing world and the insurance world that cost nothing. Cost nothing to the taxpayer. Only about 10 percent of people have an HSA. Why? Because the government rules say your insurance product is not eligible for an HSA. I'd make everybody eligible for an HSA. I'd let your health savings account pay for your premium and your deductible. I'd let your HSA pay for healthy things: gym membership, diet program, all kinds of stuff that could be for better health. Let everybody have an HSA; raise the limits. I would also let people negotiate. What happens is if I go to the store and want to negotiate over the price, nobody cares. But if I joined a co-op like Costco or Amazon through an association health plan, they could bid for my insurance. Costco has 44 million members. You think they're going to have more leverage than me and my five employees? So, in the first Trump administration, President Trump did an executive order on this. We have legislation to do this, and we're talking to people in the administration, but we'd appreciate help on letting people join a co-op or an association plan regardless of their employment. Right now, it has to be that you have to all be carpenters, or you have to all be farmers or cattlemen. We think anyone that belongs to Costco or Amazon, let them be a purchaser of insurance by loosening up the laws, and that would work. So, I'd appreciate your idea on increasing HSAs and then legalizing the concept of letting people buy insurance or drugs through a large co-op across state lines. And wouldn't it be great if we took the billions of dollars that we're giving to the insurance industry—which increased its share value by 1,000 percent after the passage of Obamacare—wouldn't it be better if we took that money and gave it to people to buy their own HSAs, which gives them value-based care, and then let them actually participate in a market that will drive down prices? The only reason I would differ on that—I'm not sure how you're talking about taking the money from the insurance companies—but I'd be for the idea that the insurance companies would get less of the take if we expanded HSAs and you were negotiating directly. That's another way to put it, Senator. Well, then the money would be more directed towards the individual and the individual would be able to use it. The other thing about it that people don't think through is they say only rich people have HSAs. Well, only 10 or 20 percent of the people had HSAs because they bid on the prices; it brings down the price for everybody. And when you have innovations in the marketplace, rich people do get them before the rest of us get them, but it always drives the price down as it spreads to the rest of the marketplace. But I think there are things we could do and we would be open to. And the thing on restricting bad foods from being subsidized and then the idea of negotiating as a co-op. Neither one of these costs money and ultimately would give a lot more power to the consumer. I would just add that 50 percent of the states have now at our request applied for waivers so that they can get sodas and candies off of SNAP. We're paying for the poorest kids, 63 million poorest kids in this country, to get diabetes and then we're treating 78 percent of them with Medicaid afterwards. So we're paying at both ends. It makes no sense. And once we finally finalize the ultra-processed food definition, we can ask the states to start adding that to their SNAP waivers. I agree completely, and if we did that across the United States, it could be the most important thing we do for the health of Americans and particularly for poor kids that we've ever done in our history. We have to get beyond this "everybody has a right to a bag of candy." No, we need to have healthy foods. There's lots of other food that you can buy, but we should restrict it from the unhealthy things. I appreciate your efforts on this. Senator Kaine. Thank you, Mr. Chair, and thank you, Mr. Secretary. Secretary Kennedy, who at HHS is responsible for monitoring whether health facilities like hospitals are closing as a result of changes that were included in the reconciliation bill last summer? The same guy who was responsible for monitoring the closures for hospitals due to the ACA regulations. Yeah, who is it and what department is that? That would be Dr. Oz. So right now Dr. Oz is responsible for monitoring those closures? He's ultimately responsible, right. Yeah. But I mean, do you have anybody at HHS, like in the Secretary's office, that is monitoring to see what's happening with the closures of facilities since the reconciliation bill passed? We have a CMS council, but that would be within CMS. Okay. 'Cause I want to bring to the attention of my colleagues and to the Secretary's office closures in Virginia because of the reconciliation bill. The bill was passed right at the beginning of July 2025. Less than two months later, on September 4th, Augusta Medical Group, which is located in Fishersville, Virginia, rural Shenandoah County, announced the closure of three clinics. These were two rural primary care clinics and one urgent care clinic. In their announcement, Augusta Health said, "The consolidation is part of Augusta Health's ongoing response to the One Big Beautiful Bill act and the resulting realities for healthcare delivery." Then on December 19th, 2025, Centra announced its decision to discontinue all OB-GYN services at their Centra Southside Community Hospital in Farmville, which is in rural Southside Virginia. In the announcement, Centra said, quote, "Centra, like other rural healthcare providers, must adapt to significant financial and operational challenges, including recently enacted reductions in federal healthcare funding." And then just in the last two weeks, Valley Health, which provides healthcare in the northern Shenandoah Valley in and around Winchester, announced it was making significant changes across its hospital system, including closing an observation unit that serves patients too sick for discharge. In the announcement, the system's president said it "has become more urgent following the adoption of the House Resolution 1, which will reduce Valley Health's funding by over 80 million dollars annually." Mr. Chair, I'd like to introduce into the record these announcements and press articles about the closures of facilities in Virginia because of the bill. Without objection. These are not hypothetical losses. The Medicaid adjustments from last year were actually not scheduled to be implemented for a while, but these institutions look at the Medicaid percentage of their population and the Medicaid changes and they realize they just can't make it work. All of these closures in Virginia—the OB-GYN ward in Farmville, so you have to travel a lot farther to have kids, primary health clinics—all of them are in rural Virginia. Overall, the Virginia Hospital Association indicates that they'll lose 34 billion dollars over 10 years as a result of the OBBA. Now that I know that CMS should focus on that, or Dr. Oz should focus on that, I'll direct my questions that way. But I think it's important for everybody in this room to know if these closures in rural Virginia are happening even before the Medicaid changes get implemented, we're going to see an awful lot more of them. I like the fact that you connect all the dots in this and I'm just urging my colleagues: as these Medicaid cuts actually get implemented, I think these closures are going to become even more dramatic and need this committee's attention. I yield back, Mr. Chair. Can I reply? Please. First of all, Senator, I think it's a tragedy. It's an absolute tragedy to lose those hospitals. I know what that does to rural areas from touring the country; you're not just denying people access to healthcare, but it is an economic driver for those communities. It's the largest employer in many cases, the highest pay. And businesses will never move there once that facility closes. So, we're doing everything in our power. This is not a problem that was created by the Trump administration or the One Big Beautiful Bill, which hasn't even gone into place yet. Yeah, but can I just say... ...on 120 of those hospitals over the past 10 years. Yeah, not in Virginia. Interestingly enough, not in Virginia. And all three of these cited the reconciliation bill as the reason for the closures. I'm glad you walked through the challenges because you're right, it's not just the loss of patient care. It's also the loss of jobs, healthcare jobs. As you point out, once the hospital closes, it's really difficult for businesses to move into these communities because they realize we can't move because there's no healthcare available. I like the fact that you connect all the dots in this. I'm urging my colleagues, as these Medicaid cuts actually get implemented, I think these closures are going to become even more dramatic. I yield back, Mr. Chair. Senator and Dr. Marshall. Oh, thank you, Mr. Chairman. I just want to remind everybody that Medicaid spending is going to be going up hundreds of billions of dollars over the next several years. I think the Trump administration has done more for rural healthcare and the rural economy than any administration. Mr. Secretary, maybe you can just talk a little bit about the moneys. I mean real moneys, a billion dollars in the Medicaid range, extra a billion dollars we've given to Kansas and another 220 million dollars to the Rural Health Transformation Fund. What has the Trump administration done to help fund rural healthcare? Well, first of all, one of the Democratic talking points is that we've cut Medicaid by a trillion dollars, but the CBO disagrees with that. The CBO report from two weeks ago says that Medicaid will rise from about 600 billion to 900 billion by 2036, a 47 percent increase. Only in Washington is that considered a cut. Second of all, the President is making the biggest investment in rural health of any administration in history. The Rural Health Transformation Fund gives 50 billion dollars. To put that in context over five years, Medicaid—which we've just heard is closing hospitals—gives only 7 percent of its funding to rural hospitals. It's about 20 billion dollars a year. We are now increasing that influx of federal funds by 50 percent per year—10 extra billion dollars a year. This gives those hospitals a chance to rebuild their infrastructure, to install telehealth, and to remodel themselves. As you've pointed out, a hospital cannot exist if the business model is failed for a lot of those hospitals. Again, 1.6 patients a day. You cannot have an overnight hospital and have a business model that works. We need to have another model and we need to help them transition to that, and that's one of the things that the Rural Health Transformation Fund will do. Yeah, thanks Secretary Kennedy. Let's talk about something near and dear to our hearts: you were committed to Make America Healthy Again and very specifically targeting children and their health. Just tell me some of the things that you've done and where are we going briefly? What are the goals for the next year? Well, I think thank you for that question, Senator. I think the most impactful thing that we've done is change the food pyramid. The food pyramid was written by food industry lobbyists for 50 years. It reflected the mercantile impulses of those companies that put Froot Loops at the top of the food pyramid. We have now done a science-based guideline. The best nutritionists in our country from the biggest universities came together for almost a year. It's a science-based guidance that emphasized protein, vegetables, and whole grains. That will now revolutionize the dietary culture in this country because we're changing the subsidy programs. We give 405 million dollars just from the USDA a day to food subsidies, to the WIC program, to school lunches, to the Indian health services, to SNAP. All these other programs will now change to align with the dietary guidelines. The military is now changing the food on all of its bases. We're going to see massive changes across the culture and more availability of this food. Getting rid of the GRAS standards, which we've had bipartisan support on, is another part. Senator Sanders has been a leader on that issue. We are actually doing it now. We have final regulations, again in the interagency process on GRAS right now. We're getting rid of food dyes. We're reforming, through Operation Stork Speed, infant formula—redoing the nutrients and making sure to test for all the contaminants. If I could just get one more question. I do commend you. You've done your job. You guys have done great work with transparency, with prior authorization, and you're starting to talk about GRAS. But I think we need to look in a mirror. Many of my colleagues are saying, "What have you done?" but we have bills right before us. Our transparency and Price-Tag bills have 18 bipartisan cosponsors. Our Prior Authorization bill has 68 cosponsors. Let's get those across the finish line. GRAS legislation, our MAHA package we just released, and a large HSA package of bills—all of these would drive down the cost of healthcare, codifying much of what you have done as well. But Congress needs to do our job, too, and quit treading water up here. Thank you, Mr. Secretary. Thank you, Senator Marshall. Secretary, before I go to Senator Hassan, what was the CDC paper you said attributed improvements in longevity to public sewer treatment, for example, not vaccines? I'm looking for that on ChatGPT and it says that it doesn't exist. So what is that? It's called Geyer; the lead author is Geyer. There's another one that came out in 1977 that was required reading in virtually every medical school that says the same thing. Who is the author of that one? That one was called McKinlay and McKinlay. McKinlay and McKinlay. That one is not CDC, according to ChatGPT. Well, that's not. The Geyer is CDC and Johns Hopkins. Senator Hassan. Well, thanks, Mr. Chair, and good to see you again Secretary Kennedy. Look, I agree with you that all Americans should have access to healthy food. However, President Trump has made it far harder for families to afford their groceries. Last year the typical family paid hundreds of dollars more than they did the previous year on groceries such as fresh vegetables, fresh fruit, meat, coffee and dairy. While a few items such as eggs have gotten cheaper this year, other staples like beef continue to reach historic highs under President Trump. Do these high grocery prices make it easier or harder for families to eat fresh healthy foods? I would say that beef has dropped by one percent in the last quarter. Look, beef prices are up 20 percent. Banana prices are up nearly 7 percent since President Trump took office. Cheese prices are up 6 percent. So again, when groceries get more expensive, is it easier or harder for families to afford the very healthy foods that you want them to eat? Well, there's also—you can eat beef, you can eat poultry, you can eat fish. The price of beef is dictated by the size of the herd. The herd dropped to the lowest... Look, you're not answering, but obviously, if the food is more expensive... You're blaming President Trump for something he didn't do. Well, let's take a look at this image. Did you post this image in 2024 about grocery prices being too high? I don't recall. Well, you did post it. That's your handle up there. And I agree that grocery prices were too high in 2024. But under President Trump, they have skyrocketed even further. So now let's go to an updated version of this post. Will you post this: the most expensive vehicle to operate in 2025-2026? Because President Trump actually brought down grocery costs for most of his term. I think there's been a raise recently in the price of everything. Look, families are facing historic prices at the register. You don't need to be a Secretary of Health and Human Services to know that. You don't need to be a United States Senator to know that. The people in New Hampshire and across this country know that because they go to grocery stores today. The fact that you either don't know it or you don't want to admit it just shows how out of touch you and the Trump administration are. Now let's go to another issue. Earlier this year, President Trump signed an executive order to increase production of the pesticide glyphosate, commonly known as Roundup, and to grant immunity from liability to the company that makes it. You've said that glyphosate causes cancer. You told Senator Schatz yesterday that you still believe that glyphosate causes cancer. You expressed displeasure about the President's glyphosate order, and you also told Senator Schatz that the President's reasoning had to do with national security. So does President Trump's executive order, does his decision mean that more Americans are likely to be exposed to this chemical and get cancer? Yes or no? No, it doesn't, because it's not increasing production. It's increasing domestic production to displace Chinese production, because 97 percent of our corn crop is dependent on glyphosate, 98 percent of our soy crop, and 100 percent of this is coming from China. But yet, here's the thing. When you were running to get the MAHA vote, you told people you would stand up to chemical companies and you would take carcinogens out of our agricultural system. You said that you would get these chemicals out of food. That's what you said you would do to make America healthy again. And yet, when push comes to shove and a big corporation—talk about the status quo here, it's Bayer Monsanto or something like that—is the big company. When the President of the United States, rather than trying to work to find alternatives or trying to get him to limit that executive order—you just stood down instead of standing up. Thank you. You ask what President Trump has done? He's given me 200 million dollars to help to get America off of glyphosate. Well, I would look forward to finding out who is doing the research and the science. Are you working with the EPA to do it? You guys created this problem. You guys have created this problem over the past 30 years. And you just told the big corporation that they don't even have liability for it anymore. He's dealing with a national security vulnerability where the Chinese could shut off our food supply in a single day. That's not a good situation. Before... Mr. Chair, I'm going to yield my time but I'm just going to say this. You have now been changing your testimony over time about this. I did not change my testimony. You said earlier you were displeased with this, you tried to talk the President out of it, and now you're just fully supportive of a huge corporation having no liability. Thank you. I sued that corporation and got 11 billion dollars from them and it put them on the edge of bankruptcy. Right, and now you're just folding right in front of them. Thank you. Senator Tuberville. Thank you, Mr. Chairman. Thank you, Mr. Secretary. Thanks for being here. Microphone on. Microphone on, man. Thank you very much. Pardon? Is your microphone on? Yeah, it should be on. Is it on? Yeah, my light's on. Is it working? Good. Thank you. Seven hearings in the last few weeks. My God. Can we not let you do your job? I mean, we don't do much up here, so we might as well let you work. But I'd like to address something on the food prices. I'm on the Ag Committee. That's where this ought to be. The Biden administration put 150,000 farms out of business when they were in charge. One hundred and fifty thousand. We're not going to have any damn food if we don't watch it. We better back our farmers and do everything we possibly can because that trash that we get out of Brazil and Asia that we're eating ain't MAHA, I promise you that. So thank you for what you're doing. You're a hero in my state because the kids are starting to understand the food pyramid. You have pushed it and you made it work. Again, I came from a former business that tried to eat right with supplements and working out, but it has caught fire. Thank you for what you're doing and continue to do what you're doing. One thing I want to ask you: I see where in our country we have 10,000 ingredients that we can use in food, but in Europe, they only use 4 or 500 food ingredients. Could you explain that? Oh, yeah, and thank you for that, coach. And this is something again Senator Sanders has shown leadership on. Congress has talked about this many years; I actually got it done. It's the GRAS standards. It's a loophole that was added in 1948 to the Food, Drug and Cosmetic Act to exclude from the necessity of testing foods that were traditionally used, like vinegar, salts, wheat, etc. The food industry, because the FDA has been captured for 50 years, hijacked that and put every new lab-created monstrosity through so it doesn't have to be tested. We have 10,000 ingredients in our country. The FDA doesn't even know what they are. We have no list of them anywhere. None of them have been adequately tested. They have 400 in Europe. We're trying to close that loophole now. We already have the 38 worst of those chemicals under review and we're going to get rid of the bad ones one at a time. Thank you. And food dyes—petroleum-based synthetic food dyes—thank you for what you're doing. It's so important. There's no reason we should be using those dyes in our food. I just want to say a few words about psychedelic treatments for PTSD. Thank you and President Trump for what you're doing for veterans. It's worked. It's worked in our state. Thank you for agreeing to look into it and make it happen more and more. Could you say something about psychedelics? Well, the chair of the department at Johns Hopkins said that Ibogaine specifically is the most promising treatment for depression and PTSD that anybody's ever seen. Right now, veterans who have served this country who have come back from deployment with severe PTSD and who are killing themselves, 23 a day, they're having to go down to Mexico to get this treatment. We don't know much about doses, we don't know much about screening, and we don't know much about the protocols. We need to get this promising treatment here. We need to do the proper studies and make it available to these vets, and we're doing that right away. Thank you. Thank you, Mr. Chairman. Senator Andy Kim. Thank you, Chairman. Secretary Kennedy, thanks for coming out here today. I just wanted to start by asking: are you familiar with the Limb Loss Resource Center? Limb Loss Resource Center? Remind me what it is. So this is really important. It's the only federally funded resource center dedicated to providing reliable information, support and assistance to individuals with limb loss as well as limb difference. It doesn't ring a bell? Individuals with limb loss. Oh, limb loss. Okay. Yeah. Have you heard of this before? No, I have not. I just raise this with you because you are proposing to eliminate it. And I wanted to ask you why. Well, I'm happy to talk to you about it. You know, we've been asked to make cuts across the department of 12 percent. I don't think Russ Vought wants to cut our department; nobody in our department wants to cut it. We have a 39 trillion dollar debt, and it may be there was a conclusion that these were duplicative services. I don't know. I'm happy to talk to you in your office. Look, here's what I'll raise: I heard from a disability leader who used this center for resources when they needed help. This budget that you're proposing zeroes out the dedicated funding for this. I also heard from a constituent, Darren, whose wife slipped while exiting a stairlift, becoming paralyzed. They relied on support from something called the Paralysis Resource Center, which is also being eliminated under this budget that you're proposing. And I'll just read through: Paralysis Resource Center, Limb Loss Resource Center, voting access for people with disabilities, University Centers for Excellence in Developmental Disabilities, and the Developmental Disabilities Projects of National Significance. Five projects there that right now are slated for elimination of dedicated funding. Can you tell me what these programs have in common? It sounds like they're programs that treat disability. Yes, right. And I just, I worry about that immensely. Because right now more than ever, I mean these families that I'm talking to, you know they are struggling so much. I, I cannot imagine, you know what some of them are going through. And when they're seeing that level of focus on cutting these resources dedicated for them, and I get it, you know you're talking about efforts to try to see if they can boost up some state-level funding, but the dedicated funding for these programs is very much on the chopping block. And I guess I just want to ask you: will you consider, would you reconsider funding for these programs? I'm happy to talk to your office about that. The other thing I wanted to ask you, because it raises, you know, again, these are all programs that are focused on disability and it worries me that there's not a prioritization of supporting the disability community and Americans with disability. Meanwhile, there is conversation, there are talks and proposals by this administration to move IDEA from the Department of Education to your Department of Health and Human Services. And we've heard that from the Secretary of Education and others. I think that, you know, I'm hearing from a lot of families that are very concerned, profoundly concerned about this kind of shift. So I wanted to ask you: will you oppose the idea and proposal of this move of IDEA from the Department of Ed to Health and Human Services? I will not. We're making, we're very committed to serving the disability community. I'm not sure you are because of what I just laid out here. I mean, the dedicated... ...we're talking about certain programs. We're putting enormous resources into disabilities throughout the agency. My uncle wrote the Americans with Disabilities Act, my cousin Teddy is, is missing a limb. These are issues that I'm, that I'm offering to talk to you about. Well, we will follow up on this. You know, we have a 2.3 trillion dollar agency. I don't know, you know, every cut. There's some that we've made mistakes on, but I, and I will tell you this: we are... well, I'm happy to talk to your office about it. Well, I'll follow back up, but I'll just leave you with this. And this is what I heard from these families and I wanted to just share with you. They say that if you are moving IDEA from the Department of Ed to Health and Human Services, you are defining these young students by their disability. Engaging them as patients rather than as students who have the right, as anybody else does, for opportunity. So I just want to point that out. Please do not single out these students and these families with disabilities. Mr. Chair, just before I finish here, I just want to ask unanimous consent to submit to the record a press release from the New Jersey Department of Banking and Insurance entitled, quote, "Cost increases from Washington's inaction drives nearly 70,000 New Jerseyans to drop health coverage since January." Without objection. Thank you. With that, I yield back. Senator Jim Banks. Thank you, Mr. Chairman. Secretary Kennedy, I think someone in the media told me this is your seventh appearance before a committee testifying. I can't think of any other cabinet secretary in the Trump cabinet who has been as transparent as you have been, and I appreciate you being here today. I appreciate your service. There's not a day that goes by when I don't hear from a Hoosier mom who tells me to tell you thank you for your leadership and what you're doing. And my wife is in the audience, she's right behind you, she, she wants me to tell you thank you as well for what you do for the families all over this country, but especially in Indiana. What, what message do you have as we wind down this committee hearing for families that are counting on you and this team to make America healthy again? You know, we spend, people have been talking about measles all day long. But we spend, when my uncle was President, we spent zero on chronic disease in this country. Now we spend 4.3 trillion dollars just in my agency. The military and Social Security spend more. All together, 48 cents out of every tax dollar paid to the federal government is going to healthcare. Ninety percent of that is going to chronic disease. This is an existential issue. We have the highest chronic disease burden of any country in the world. Seventy-seven percent of American kids cannot qualify for military service. It goes as a national defense issue as well. Diabetes rates, juvenile diabetes, have gone from essentially zero among children to 38 percent of our teens being diabetic or pre-diabetic. Autism rates have gone from one in 10,000 in 1970 to one in every 31 today. And we need to do something to protect our children. This has got to be our top priority. And infectious disease is important and one of my jobs is to make sure to stem the outbreaks, which I have done with measles better than any country in the world. But we also can't just talk about that in exclusion to the thing that's really killing our country, which is poor health. During COVID, we had the highest death rate of any country on earth. The highest. So we did worse with COVID than any nation in the world. We had a death rate of 3,000 per million population. There are other countries that had 14 deaths per million population from COVID. And when you ask CDC why did all these people die from COVID? Americans more than anybody else. They say it's because of our chronic disease epidemic. The average American who died had 3.8 chronic diseases. This was not an infectious disease that was killing healthy people. It was killing sick people. So the connection between chronic disease and infectious disease is there for everybody to see, but everybody ignores it. Every Democrat in this committee, all they want to do is talk about measles. And none of them, for years they've talked about trying to reform the food. I'm doing it now, and they're doing everything in their power to impede me from doing it. So it's all partisanship, it's all tribalism, and it's not real. It's, it's crazy. I, I listen to their questions over and over again and you've made so much progress, yet the Democrats have tried to obstruct everything that you just talked about trying to do to make this country healthy. I think it's, it's totally shameful. And there are so many things that we have yet, in the time that we have left with you as Secretary and President Trump in the White House, things to get done. And one thing that you and I have talked a lot about before is what China has done to dominate the pharmaceutical industry. Last year 42 percent of clinical drug trials were conducted in China. It used to be 100 percent of them that were conducted in the United States. Why, why, what are drug companies getting out of that? And can you talk about the threat of those drug trials occurring in China instead of in a safer environment in the US? Sure, I mean they're getting faster clinical trials over there because we have a system that was designed, you know, the typical drug from conception to commercialization because of the regulatory process takes 15 years. And we can't afford to do that anymore. And Senator, one of the... Is it true that they're less safe? Obviously they're less safe than in a safer environment. No, they're less safe but there's all of, and they're also breaking all kinds of ethical rules. They're, you know, Shanghaiing minority groups in China to participate in trials that they don't want to participate in. We're now doing inspections over there to catch that. More importantly, we need to compress the time from concept to commercialization in this country. And we have a bill that we're going to put before Congress to try to compress a phase one trial and actually make it all a seamless phase one, phase two, phase three, and that will help us. So we want to work with you on that. Thank you for your leadership, Mr. Secretary. Thank you, Senator Banks. Senator Bill Cassidy. Again, speaking as a physician, I will say we looked up, I looked up the McKinlay and McKinlay articles. There were 3.5 million cases of measles per year before the vaccine came along and about 550 deaths. And then the vaccine took those to less than 100 and like zero deaths. So the efficacy of the McKinlay paper was written before, for events before 1950, which is before the vaccine came out. But then the vaccine came out and that's when deaths went, cases went from 3.5 million down to near zero and deaths went to zero from 550 a year. So a tremendous impact of the vaccination. I was talking about mortality, Senator. That's what they're saying. The deaths have been virtually eliminated. Okay. Senator Alsobrooks. Thank you so much, Mr. Chair. Good afternoon Secretary. You're familiar with Aaron Siri, correct? Yes, I am. Okay, that's because he served both as your lawyer in your personal capacity and he's a lawyer for an anti-vaccine group as well. Is Mr. Siri on the federal government payroll? No, he is not. No, he's not. Okay, so that I find that interesting because he is apparently having a really outsized influence on you. And in fact, according to her testimony, Dr. Manorez last August said that during a very contentious argument with you or conversation, that you told her to talk to Mr. Siri so that she could fall in line with his vaccine policies. You let him present to your now illegal and discredited vaccination committee, and then this month you signed an updated charter for the Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices to focus on vaccine risk after Aaron Siri urged you to do so. So you know, despite him not being on the government payroll, it looks like he is clearly for you, he's a top, top advisor in your agency. Now last month Mr. Siri threatened to sue you if you didn't add hundreds of health conditions to the vaccine injury compensation programs and, you know, and just like that, you removed members of the Advisory Commission on Childhood Vaccines to pave the way for changes. So I just have to ask you, Mr. Secretary, who is running this department? Is Aaron Siri running the department? Are you running the department? This Aaron Siri guy has a huge, apparently, influence. Oh, ACIP has nothing to do with, with the, with the vaccine court, nothing. So. I just asked the question: is he running the department or are you running the department? This Aaron Siri guy has a huge, apparently, influence. You're, you're just filled with mischaracterizations. If you want to ask me about something, I will, but... Who is right in this question? He's suing me and criticizing me for something that he, for a policy that I've made that he doesn't like. So does he have an outside influence or not? Because you're arguing out of both sides of your mouth. Well he said he's actually, he's gone public with the whole thing now. He's on X and he said, you know, that you, he's actually trying to pressure you to immediately change your vaccine policies. In fact, he wrote on X, "I have no doubt RFK Jr. wants to update the table and the only reason that he wouldn't is because the White House won't let him." So is the White House preventing you from updating the VICP table? Aaron Siri is an American citizen, he has a right to say what he wants. He does, but I asked you, is the White House preventing you from updating this? Is Mr. Siri correct about that? Updating the vaccine table? No, they are not. Okay. Now regarding the new CDC director nominee, part of the new team that you said would revolutionize the CDC, Mr. Siri said that the pick will likely be a disaster and has said the only thing that Shwartze would likely restore is the CDC to business as usual. He said that left to his quote, left to your own devices you wouldn't have even chosen her, and that she is, and I quote Mr. Siri here, "the queen of mandating vaccines." So is Aaron Siri wrong about this new nominee or is the President? Well, I guess Aaron Siri doesn't have much influence on me after all, does he? So you can't have it both ways of saying he has an outside influence on our department at the same time he's suing me and criticizing me for something that he, for a policy that I've made that he doesn't like so. Who is right in this question? He is right. Let me go to my next question, Mr. Secretary. My next question. Now you've had some trouble with the truth, I've seen it myself during your appearances before Congress, and as we all saw clearly during your exchange last week with Congresswoman Sewell. Can you admit today that you said every Black kid can get re-parented on a wellness farm? Can you admit that you said that? Black kid re-parented on a wellness? Well, let me read exactly what you said. You said every Black kid is now just standard put on Adderall, on SSRIs, benzos which are known to induce violence, and those kids are going to have a chance to go somewhere and get re-parented to live in a community where there'll be no cellphones, no screens. You know, you claimed, in fact the whole transcript was even worse, you said that if you could, you would send quote "every Black kid," again your words not mine, to go live on farms and work. Is that your? I would have to see, hear that recording, because I have no memory of saying anything like that. Well, I actually have the recording that I can give to you, but it is absolutely what you said, and if you want me to play it, I can play it. If you ask me what my opinion is, I do not believe that every Black kid should be re-parented on a wellness farm or whatever, and I have never believed that. Well, you said it, sir. I have the video. In fact, you're saying I'm telling you I don't believe that. That is not my vision for our country or... Well, I'm glad because it was ignorant to say, it was dangerous, and it was irresponsible. Well, if I said it, I apologize, but I'd have to see the transcript. Thank you, Senator Alsobrooks. Senator Hawley. Thank you, Mr. Chairman. Mr. Secretary, good to see you again. Welcome. I'm going to start by just saying how much I appreciate your efforts to get big pharma back on a leash, under control. Particularly your efforts to stop direct-to-consumer advertising. I mean, there's just no reason that big pharma, in particular, should be getting federal subsidies to do this kind of advertising which is frequently misleading, bad for consumers. So you and the FDA have been leading on this. I appreciate that, thank you for that. Let me ask about something that I am concerned about. The department recently elected to allow Title X grants to flow to abortion providers for another year. Let me, let me just give you, let me contextualize this and give you an example from my state as to why this concerns me. You're going to see, I think, over my shoulder here a poster from an actual provider, abortion provider, in the state of Missouri. This is an entity called Beacon Reproductive Health Network that they just received about 8 million dollars from a Title X grant. Now Beacon Health, as you can see here, they do more than just refer for abortions. They also proclaim their support for all bodies. They provide gender-affirming care, including to young people, that's gender transition surgeries. These folks now, who by the way went to my state legislature and tried to prevent the legislature from writing in the difference between male and female, defining that in state law. They opposed our ban in the state of Missouri on transgender surgeries for minors. So that's the kind of folks here. They're getting now 8 million dollars in taxpayer funding from the Title X program because of the department's decision. When, can you tell me when are we going to stop funding people like this? I don't think that this kind of entity ought to be getting federal tax money. I agree with you, Senator. And you know, we paused those grants. These are five-year grants. We paused them. There's a year left on them and we looked at the litigation risk, and OGC advised us that it was very, very high. And so we're paying them out the last year. We're monitoring for Hyde Amendment compliance, but we've just released NOFOs today that I think are very aligned with your thinking and I think you'll be very happy with. Good. What about the Protect Life rule, Mr. Secretary? Bringing that back in to regulations that would prevent any, going forward, any federal tax dollars from flowing to an abortion provider or an, an abortion referrer? I mean, are you still pursuing that? You mean outside of the Hyde Amendment's reach? Yes, yes. But I'm happy to work with you on that. Okay, good. I think that it's important. I think that we should not be funding entities like this. And you know, personally, I think a little litigation risk... I'd rather take the risk in litigation than subject children in my state to transgender care funded by federal taxpayer dollars, so I appreciate you working on this. I think if you look at the new NOFOs, you'll be very happy. Good. On a different subject, there are currently in the state of Missouri something like 60 data centers. We have many, many more that want to come into the state. I think there are 4,000 data centers currently being built in the United States; over 3,000 are already in operation. When I go home now, I get asked all the time by people who are big MAHA people. I get asked constantly about the health risks around these data centers. And I honestly don't know what to tell them because I don't know what the science is, but I just want to rehearse those for you. I'm going to show you a picture of a data center. This is in North Kansas City. I mean, these are, these are massive. This is going to consume, I think it's 700 megawatts of power. That's, that's enough for 300,000 homes in, in one year. But I get asked constantly things about electromagnetic fields. You know, this is like having these data centers, like having multiple high voltage transformers all located in one place. Does, is that an increased cancer risk? I, I have people at, in my state who worry that it is, who are now living next to these data centers. They worry about decibel levels causing things like headaches, migraines, vertigo, other neurological disorders. My question to you is: is this something that HHS will study to look at the, the potential health effects here so we can get a handle on, on what is, what is a health risk, what's really not a health risk? So that we know what we're dealing with when we're talking about thousands and thousands and thousands of these being built, not just in my state but all over the country? Yes, sir. And I just want to say that I've litigated on this issue. We produced over 10,000 studies for the court of appeals and I won the case to get FCC to redo its regulations on, on EMFs. And you're right, there's a range of injuries that are very, very well documented. They're neurological, very, very grave neurological injuries, cancer risk, and then just the making the blood-brain barrier permeable so more toxins can get into the brain. It is, the, the risks to me are, are horrendous. But we are, I've asked both ARPA and the Surgeon General's office to either do meta-reviews or to do base studies on this issue so that we can better inform the American public. Good. Thank you very much. Thank you, Mr. Chairman. Senator Blunt Rochester. Thank you, Mr. Chairman. The United States first became measles-free over 25 years ago. A quarter of a century. We have maintained that status in every year since until you became Secretary. A simple yes or no: does the President know there is a historic measles surge occurring nationwide? You want me to ask a question to answer this or you're just gonna... The question is just a very simple question. ...do you want a grandstand? You're going to get your opportunity. Can you answer... I can answer that question. I didn't cause... Just the... Just a simple... It started before I came in. It is a global... Sir, I'm giving you the opportunity to answer the question. Mr. Chairman, can I get my time back from this, this rudeness? Mr. Chairman? I'm sorry. I was asking, can I get my time back from him interrupting a simple yes or no question? Yes, you can. Does the President know there is a historic measles surge occurring nationwide? I'm sure he does. You're sure he does. Remarkably, Ralph Abraham, your former CDC principal deputy director, said that losing our measles-free status is not really significant and just the cost of doing business with our borders. Another simple yes or no: does the President think this is not a significant issue? Losing our measles elimination status? Yes. Well, most of the country are losing. Does he think it's a significant... I'm sure he doesn't think it's a good development that's happening all over the world. Have you talked to him about it? About measles? About the outbreaks? About the crisis in this country? Yes, I have. Okay, so yes or no, he does know it's a problem? Yes. According to the Washington Post, the President makes public statements about his plans for the new gold ballroom at a pace that rivals and even exceeds the mentions of some major policy priorities. He talks publicly about the ballroom one out of every three days. And so I have a question. Do you know how many times the President has spoken about the measles epidemic? I don't count that. I don't, we don't keep track of that data at CDC. So as head of our nation's head health agency, have you advised the President to sound the alarm and encourage Americans to obtain the measles vaccine? That, that's my job and we do it. So yes or no? It's my job, not the President's job, and I do my job. I'm concerned about it. I've watched a lot of these hearings. You've blamed the Mennonites, you've blamed immigrants, you've blamed the globe. We've been part of the globe, and still we are in this situation. Do you take any responsibility in your role for the situation that we are in with this measles epidemic? As I said, the measles epidemic began before I came into office. The people... I'm going to take that as a no. The Pan American Health Organization was scheduled to review our measles-free status on April 13th. Did your department request to delay this independent international review of our measles elimination status to November, after the election? No. So you did not request it, you had no knowledge of the request, you had no communication with the White House? Thank you. It's awfully convenient that we're postponing it until after November when people won't have the opportunity to know our measles status. I would just say this. There has been a lot in terms of polling on trust. You've talked a lot about trust. The people don't trust you, and I would ask: what is different from all these other years? You are. If the American people don't trust you, which most of the polls show, I don't know why the President should trust you either. I yield back. Senator Ashley Moody. Thank you, Mr. Chairman. Thank you, Mr. Secretary, for being here to answer our questions in your marathon hearing schedule here. Mr. Secretary, prior to your swearing in, the overwhelming majority of medical students received no nutrition education. None. That's insane. That is insane when you're looking at this chronic disease and the rates that we're facing. Less than one percent of lecture hours in medical schools were dedicated to this fundamental part of health. Just last month you secured historic agreements with well over 50 of the top medical schools in America to require comprehensive nutrition education, giving our future healthcare providers the tools necessary to attack chronic disease at the most fundamental level. I'm so proud six Florida schools were part of this agreement, and they are very excited that HHS has invested 5 million dollars to support the integration of this curriculum. So all in all, before you came along and thought to do these things and pushed for these things, we expected to keep working within the status quo and expecting our chronic disease rates to change. So thank goodness you came along. Florida wants to be ground zero for the fight for nutrition. I know our moms want that. I'm part of that caucus and you and your team have grabbed the bull by the horns. You've partnered with industry, you're standing strong for our kids and for Americans. How transformative do you think that HHS's new nutrition guidelines combined with new nutrition education requirements for medical students will be for America? You know, 80 percent of young doctors today say that they feel inadequate to offer nutritional advice. And you have most of our, 80 percent of our, 90 percent of our healthcare costs are chronic disease that are diet induced. Oh, upwards of 70 percent of type 2 diabetes can be cured just by changing diet or vastly improved. And a doctor should know that, that there's other things other than the pharmacopeia to turn to. That you can have dietary changes and lifestyle changes that save your life without getting addicted to a lifetime of pharmaceutical interventions. I see that I have blown past my time, so thank you very much, Secretary. The information of senators: we will take a short break at. At that point, I'll ask our guests in the audience to remain seated until the Secretary steps out. But that'll be at the bottom of the hour. And with that, Senator Murray. Thank you very much, Mr. Chairman, and Secretary Kennedy. You were before Appropriations yesterday and I asked you about the insanely bloated military budget that we are seeing from the White House this year. At the same time, the President isn't doing anything to invest in our healthcare here at home. And now we have learned he's not even investing in our troops' health. Secretary Hegseth announced that the US military will no longer require American troops to get the flu vaccine. My own colleague Senator Wicker called it a mistake and pointed to his own experience noting quote, "I dutifully took my flu shot every year. As a whole it made for a healthier and flu-free force." Secretary Kennedy, is CDC or any agency at HHS ready and able to detect or monitor localized flu outbreaks in our military as a result of this new, what I think is a backwards, policy? Secretary Hegseth was just recognizing that these soldiers being sent over to fight for our freedoms and that they should have some freedom too. The flu shot is an intervention that is often ineffective, has a 20 percent efficacy rate. There are studies that show that getting a flu shot actually increases the chance of a non-flu infection. A soldier... Well, you and I may differ on that, but my question to you wasn't about that. My question to you is: is HHS or CDC either one now going to monitor any flu outbreaks on our military ships or... I don't know if there's any program specifically for the military, but we definitely monitor flu outbreaks. Well, this was a very old policy. It wasn't based in science, it was based in history. Secretary Hegseth is improving the food at all of the military bases. Mr. Secretary, if I can finish, the 1918 pandemic actually sickened 40 percent of our service members and killed 20,000 soldiers. If you have a submarine full of people who we are counting on and a virus goes flying through them, we don't have a ready military. So my question to you was not about that. You and I are going to have to disagree about... ...you're comparing a Spanish flu to a flu. We have to have some... Mr. Secretary, it is my time. I'm asking you simply, and you didn't answer me, whether or not we were going to monitor that to make sure that we do have a troop-ready, two troops ready for in these very critical times. But let me go on here 'cause I just have a few minutes left. Yesterday when we talked, you went on a very long tangent about how you were just cancelling woke grants. But I want for the record put the numbers here. You cancelled 17, at least 17, maternal health grants, 4 million dollars, because apparently any research involving women is woke. You cancelled 58 grants for vaccine research, 94 million dollars. And 59 for Alzheimer's research, that was 33 million dollars, and a whopping 108 cancer research grants, 29 million dollars' worth. I, I just have to say, cancer is not woke. Neither is Alzheimer's or women who die in childbirth. These are deadly issues. They deserve serious research. I am appalled that it was tossed in the shredder and meanwhile, as I said, we're shovelling money on war spending. So I just want to be clear that the budget that was sent to us is not a MAGA budget, it's not a MAHA budget, it is a war budget. No one can call it anything other than that. Why on earth would we take from researchers in rural healthcare, maternal healthcare? You justified these cuts because of a need to reduce the debt yesterday.
